## 1. Overview

### 1.0 What Is Mesh Infinity?

Mesh Infinity is a private, encrypted communications app. You can chat with friends, share files, and stay connected — without any company servers, without anyone in the middle, and without your activity being visible to outside observers.

It works on phones, tablets, and computers. You use it like any other messaging app. The privacy happens automatically.

### 1.1 Tell Me More

**How is it different from Signal or WhatsApp?**
Those apps rely on company-owned servers to deliver your messages. If those servers go down, get seized, or the company changes its policies, you lose access. Mesh Infinity has no central servers. Your messages travel through the devices of people in your network — there is nothing to shut down.

**Who can see my messages?**
Only the people you send them to. Messages are encrypted end-to-end, and the network routes them through multiple layers so that even the people helping deliver them cannot read them.

**Who are the people helping deliver my messages?**
Other Mesh Infinity users — including you. When the app is running, your device quietly helps route other people's encrypted messages. You cannot read them. In return, their devices help route yours. This is what makes the network private for everyone: the more people participate, the harder it is for anyone to single out any one person's activity.

**Do I have to understand any of this to use it?**
No. Open the app, add contacts, start chatting. Everything else happens in the background.

**What about my identity?**
You can present different identities to different groups of people — a personal one for friends, a professional one for colleagues — without those groups knowing about each other. You are always in control of who knows what about you.

**Can I do more than chat?**
Yes. File sharing and advanced network controls are available when you need them, but hidden by default so they don't get in the way.

### 1.2 Complete Overview

*The sections below cover everything in depth — how the network works, how trust and identity are managed, how messages are encrypted, and how to build on top of the platform. You do not need to read any of it to use Mesh Infinity. The explanation above gives you everything you need to get started. This document exists for contributors, implementers, and anyone who wants to understand exactly how the system works under the hood.*

#### 1.2.1 What Makes It Different

**No central servers.** There is no Mesh Infinity company server handling your messages, storing your keys, or knowing who you talk to. Your messages travel through the network itself — through devices belonging to people in your trust circle and beyond.

**No single point of failure or control.** There is no server to seize, subpoena, or shut down. The network is the people on it.

**Routing through relationships, not random nodes.** Unlike Tor, which routes through randomly selected relays chosen by a directory authority, Mesh Infinity routes through your social graph. Nodes you trust become your routing infrastructure. Nodes your contacts trust are reachable via them. The network topology emerges from human relationships.

**Transport-agnostic.** Unlike I2P, which forms a standalone overlay, Mesh Infinity can route over Tor, I2P, WireGuard, Bluetooth, RF radio, or the regular internet — and can mix them simultaneously. If one path is cut, others remain.

**Fully distributed.** Unlike Signal, which relies on centralised Signal servers for message delivery, key distribution, and group management, Mesh Infinity performs all of these functions across the peer network itself.

#### 1.2.2 Identities and Masks

Every user has a **self** — a core cryptographic identity that is the root of all their activity on the mesh. The self is never exposed directly. Instead, users present **masks**: contextual identities for different situations.

A mask is not a separate account. It is a face the self wears. You might have a personal mask for friends and family, a professional mask for work contacts, and an operational mask for high-security contexts. You can wear multiple masks simultaneously in the same app session. Contacts who know your professional mask have no way to discover your personal one unless you choose to link them.

This models how people actually behave in the real world — presenting differently in different contexts while remaining the same person. The app makes this explicit and safe rather than forcing a single public identity.

There are also **anonymous masks**, which are architecturally isolated from the self. Not even the app can prove the link between an anonymous mask and the person using it.

#### 1.2.3 Trust Model

**Anonymization in Mesh Infinity is directed at adversaries, not at trusted contacts.** The goal of the privacy architecture is to prevent surveillance by ISPs, state actors, and unknown parties — not to hide from people you trust. Within a trusted channel, the experience is as natural as any modern chat app. Trusted peers are trusted.

Trust is graduated, explicit, and user-controlled. A complete stranger starts with minimal access. Someone your closest contacts vouch for gets more. Someone you have verified in person and elevated to your inner circle gets the most. Every capability in the system is gated on appropriate trust levels that you set.

#### 1.2.4 Your Node Always Participates

When Mesh Infinity is running, your device is an active node in the mesh — regardless of which features you have enabled. It routes encrypted messages for others (which it cannot read), maintains connections, and sends cover traffic that makes everyone's activity harder to analyse.

This is not optional and it is not a bug. It is the mechanism by which the network provides privacy. The more nodes participate, the larger the anonymity set, and the harder it is for any observer to distinguish real traffic from noise. Your participation protects others; their participation protects you.

#### 1.2.5 Feature Tiers

Mesh Infinity starts as a chat app. Additional capabilities can be enabled as needed:

- **Social** (always on): Chat, communities, contacts, and settings. This is the full experience for most users.
- **File Sharing** (opt-in): Send files to contacts and publish files to the distributed storage network.
- **Network Controls** (opt-in): Exit nodes, VPN mode, and advanced transport configuration.

Enabling or disabling a tier changes what appears in the app's navigation. It does not change what the node does on the network — the node always participates fully at every tier.

#### 1.2.6 Architecture in Brief

The Rust backend is the trusted security boundary. The Flutter UI is a thin rendering layer that issues intent-based commands and renders backend state. Keys, cryptography, transport, and storage never leave Rust. A compromised or buggy UI cannot compromise security — all security decisions are made and enforced by the backend.

The application runs as a single process. There is no IPC between components, which eliminates an entire class of privilege escalation attack.

#### 1.2.7 Intended Use

This application is not intended to enable immoral activities regardless of their legality. The threat model assumes environments where institutions meant to protect people have become the threat — state security services in authoritarian regimes, persecution of journalists, activists, dissidents, and minorities, domestic situations where "arrested" means "taken by a hostile actor with state power who intends harm." Immigration enforcement operations in countries where administrative warrants carry the same practical weight as judicial ones are a concrete domestic example of this category.

This distinction must be explicit so that implementers reason correctly about edge cases: every design decision must serve people who need protection, not people who need concealment for predatory purposes.

---

*If you have read this far, you have a solid understanding of what Mesh Infinity is and why it works the way it does. The rest of this document is a comprehensive specification covering every technical detail of the project — the network protocol, cryptography, identity model, routing, and everything needed to implement or build on top of it. It is here when you need it.*

---

## 2. Design Principles

### 2.0 Threat Model Baseline

The following assumptions underpin every design decision in this specification. They are not goals or aspirations — they are the conditions under which the system is designed to operate.

- Everyone who is not an explicitly trusted contact is a potential adversary.
- A device belonging to a trusted contact can only be trusted with *that contact's* data and capabilities — not with the full trust surface of the network.
- Anyone who has not been granted access is assumed to have hostile intent toward the user.
- The system is designed for situations where rule of law has broken down or has been turned against the people it was meant to protect.
- The weakest link is always the human — no technical system survives physical coercion.
- Physical seizure by a sophisticated adversary (device imaging in a Faraday cage before power-on) is an accepted risk that cannot be fully mitigated. This is documented honestly so users are never misled about the limits of their protection.

These are not hypothetical edge cases. They are the primary design context. Features that work "well enough in practice" under a benign threat model but fail under these assumptions are rejected. The maximally hostile observer is the default; reduced-security modes are explicit, informed opt-ins layered on top of the safe baseline.

Note that routing encrypted traffic for unknown nodes is not in tension with this baseline. Unknown nodes are not trusted — their traffic is routed because doing so benefits the anonymity set for everyone on the network, including the local user. Routing a packet is not granting access. See §1.1 for the plain-language explanation and §1.2.4 for the full technical rationale.

The principles in §2.1 are the design rules that follow directly from these assumptions. When a principle appears to conflict with a feature, the threat model baseline is the tiebreaker.

### 2.1 Principles

The following principles govern every design and implementation decision. When two principles conflict, the one with the lower number takes precedence.

1. **Assume a maximally hostile observer.** Every design decision must be evaluated against the threat model: all activity is surveilled, all metadata is retained, all exposures carry the highest possible consequence. "Probably fine in practice" is not acceptable reasoning. The question to ask when designing any feature is: *if every byte of metadata this produces were handed to the most hostile imaginable authority, what would they learn, and who would be harmed?* Default settings must reflect this worst case. Reduced-security modes exist only as explicit, informed opt-ins layered on top of a safe baseline — never the other way around.

2. **Every known risk must be classified and addressed at the appropriate level.** Not all risks can be solved at the protocol level — requiring that would make the app unusable, which defeats the purpose. The classification determines the response:

   - **Protocol-level mitigation** — enforced by the system, no user action required. Used where possible.
   - **Environmental gating** — the feature is only offered when conditions make it reasonable (e.g. LoSec requiring minimum ambient traffic). Reduces exposure without eliminating it.
   - **Consent-and-disclosure** — the risk is clearly communicated, the tradeoff is honest, the user decides. Used for opt-in features where protocol mitigation would make the feature unusable (e.g. public profiles, LoSec mode, direct connections).
   - **Accepted and documented** — the risk cannot be meaningfully mitigated at any level. Documented honestly in §2.2 (Known Limitations) so users are never misled.

   What is never acceptable is an *unexamined* risk — one that was not thought about, not disclosed, and not deliberately chosen. Every risk in this spec was considered. The §2.2 entries exist because we looked, not because we didn't.

3. **If you can do it on the open internet, you must be able to do it on Mesh Infinity.** Chat, file sharing, web browsing, hosting services, VPN routing, social profiles — all must have a mesh equivalent. This is the feature completeness bar.

4. **Complexity belongs to the system, not the user.** The average user's mental model is: add contacts, chat, it's secure. Advanced features exist and are accessible, but never required to achieve safety or functionality. Default settings must be safe defaults.

5. **No cloud dependencies for core function.** Google Play Services, Apple cloud, Microsoft cloud, and equivalent vendor-specific services are prohibited from the critical path. They may be used as optional, non-critical enhancements — cloud push notifications as a wake signal carrying zero message content (§14), commercial VPN providers as exit node configurations (§13.9) — but the system must function completely without them. Removing cloud access degrades convenience; it must never degrade security or core functionality.

6. **Trust is explicit and user-controlled.** No system makes automatic trust decisions on behalf of the user. Trust levels are set by the user; propagated trust is advisory and always overridable.

7. **The node always participates fully, regardless of UI tier or authentication state.** Once Layer 1 (mesh identity) is active — which happens at device unlock, before any app interaction — the node is a full mesh participant: routing, relaying, cover traffic, and tunnel coordination. Feature tiers (§1.2.5) control what the UI exposes. Authentication state (whether the user has entered their PIN) controls whether Layers 2/3 are active. Neither changes what Layer 1 does. Any implementation that allows a node to opt out of mesh participation while Layer 1 is initialized violates this principle.

8. **Participation in anonymizing infrastructure is reciprocal. Parasitism is penalised.** Consuming anonymity without contributing degrades the network for everyone. When a node uses an anonymizing network (Tor, I2P, the native mixnet tier, Katzenpost), relay participation on that network is enabled by default and expected. The only routine configuration is bandwidth — how much you contribute, not whether you do. Opting out is permitted but carries real consequences enforced by the network, not by policy:

   - **Bandwidth throttling:** Non-contributing nodes are de-prioritised by other nodes' transport solvers. They receive lower-quality routes, slower relay selection, and reduced priority in store-and-forward queues.
   - **Trust floor reduction:** A node that has disabled relay on all anonymizing transports has its effective anonymization score reduced in the solver — it benefits less from the cover traffic it declines to provide. This is not punitive; it is accurate.
   - **Peer deprioritisation:** Nodes that contribute relay are preferred as routing peers over nodes that do not. A non-contributing node may find itself with fewer willing hops, higher latency paths, and reduced redundancy.
   - **Visible disclosure:** The UI displays an honest status: *"You are not contributing relay capacity. Your anonymity and routing quality are reduced as a result."* This is not a warning to dismiss — it is an accurate description of the node's standing in the network.

   Exit node participation is always opt-in with explicit legal and operational warnings — that gate requires informed consent because it exposes the operator to real legal risk. But relay (not exit) carries no such exposure, and declining it is a choice to take from the network without giving back. The network remembers.

9. **Relay activity is cover traffic.** A node that is actively relaying traffic for others on a given transport needs significantly less synthetic cover traffic on that transport — the relay traffic itself provides plausible deniability for every byte the node sends. The transport solver accounts for relay status when calculating cover traffic obligations. The diversity floor (§5.10.5) still applies regardless of relay activity — relay on one transport does not substitute for transport diversity.

10. **We expand allied networks, not just consume them.** Mesh Infinity is not the only network trying to give people secure, private, censorship-resistant communication. Where another network's protocol and ours are compatible, we run both — not as an interoperability feature, but as an obligation. When Bluetooth is active, we are a full Briar node. When Tor is active, we are a Tor relay. The measure of success is not the size of the Mesh Infinity network alone — it is the aggregate reach of all the networks we participate in. Our infrastructure exists to help as many people as possible, including people who will never know Mesh Infinity exists but benefit from the Briar relay we ran for them.

### 2.2 Known Limitations and Accepted Tradeoffs

These problems are acknowledged but not fully solved. They are documented here — at the front of the spec, not buried in an appendix — so that users and implementers are never misled about the boundaries of the system's protections. Per §2.1 principle 2, every entry here was examined, classified, and deliberately accepted.

**1. Ratchet window after device seizure.**

PFS protects the past. An adversary who seizes a device holds the current ratchet state (position N). When contacts send messages with new DH ratchet keys, the adversary can advance the ratchet forward and keep decrypting until contacts stop sending to the compromised identity. This compounds with the trusted peer device seizure problem (the adversary holds a live, authenticated, trusted node during the window between seizure and network awareness).

**2. Dead man's switch / liveness dilemma.**

Any periodic liveness signal is a deanonymisation vector. No liveness signals means no dead man's switch. These are in direct conflict. The pre-committed distress message (§15.5) is a partial mitigation, not a solution. The cancellation pattern is itself an observable signal, and a stopped cancellation tells an adversary *when* something happened. False positives (imprisonment without device compromise) trigger Self-Disavowed unintentionally.

**3. Physical seizure without killswitch activation.**

A sophisticated adversary images the device in a Faraday cage before powering it on. Remote killswitch signals cannot reach the device. Local data exposure (trusted-channel keys, local contact mappings, message history, the full local contact graph) is an accepted risk against this adversary class. No technical mitigation exists for a physical seizure by a sufficiently resourced adversary.

**4. The $5 hammer.**

No cryptographic system survives coercion. This is out of scope for technical mitigation. The system documents this honestly so that users who think they have protection they do not are not in a worse position.

**5. Friend-Disavowed threshold gossip consistency.**

The network can briefly hold inconsistent state about whether a threshold has been reached. This is an inherent distributed systems property accepted as unavoidable.

**6. Threshold manipulation window.**

A device has up to 1 week after a `friend_disavow_threshold` change before the cooldown kicks in. During this window, a captured device could manipulate its own threshold. Accepted.

**7. Profile sharing permanence.**

Once a private profile is shared with a trusted peer, that sharing is permanent regardless of subsequent trust level changes. Trust downgrade removes future access, not past access. The data has already been delivered and cannot be un-delivered.

**8. Social engineering.**

The mutual trust model protects against technical attacks. It cannot prevent users from granting high trust to malicious parties who gain trust through social manipulation. This is a human problem, not a protocol problem.

**9. Forgotten verification passphrase.**

If both parties forget the verification passphrase established at pairing, in-band approval with maximum friction (72-hour mandatory waiting period, explicit risk acknowledgment, PIN re-authentication) is the only recovery path. No cryptographic recovery exists for a forgotten passphrase.

---

## 3. Identity and Cryptography

### 3.0 Overview

This section covers how identities are structured, how cryptographic material is derived and stored, how privacy and deniability are achieved at the cryptographic layer, and how identity material is protected, backed up, and recovered.

The key architectural concept is the **three-layer identity model** (§3.1): a mesh identity layer active at device unlock, a self layer requiring app authentication, and a mask layer for all identity-bearing communication. These layers are cryptographically isolated — each is covered in §3.1.

From there the section covers:
- §3.2 — how trusted-channel keys are derived between peers
- §3.3 — the address and keypair model for multiple public identities
- §3.4 — forward reference to cryptographic primitive negotiation (§5.0)
- §3.5 — deniability mechanisms and zero-knowledge proofs
- §3.6 — how key material is stored, protected, and PIN-gated
- §3.7 — backup types, cloud storage model, restore process, and safety numbers
- §3.8 — recovery when a key is compromised
- §3.9 — emergency data destruction and the duress PIN flow
- §3.10 — PIN enforcement properties

### 3.1 Identity Model

Every Mesh Infinity node operates with three distinct identity layers. Each layer has different cryptographic material, different accessibility, different capabilities, and different threat surfaces. They are deliberately isolated from each other — compromising one layer reveals nothing about the others.

#### 3.1.1 Layer 1 — Mesh Identity

The mesh identity is the node's always-available presence on the network. It is the only layer active before the user authenticates into the app — but once Layers 2 and 3 are active, all layers are peers in terms of WireGuard capability. Any keypair can establish and maintain tunnels.

**What makes Layer 1 special is accessibility, not capability.** It is stored at device-unlock level, initializes before the app opens, and can participate in the mesh without any user interaction. Layers 2 and 3 have equivalent WireGuard capability but require user authentication to load.

**Characteristics:**
- Generated fresh on each app install — never reused, never restored from backup
- Stored at device-unlock accessibility (`kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly` on iOS, equivalent on other platforms)
- Initialized when the device is unlocked — before the user opens the app or enters a PIN
- Holds WireGuard keypairs, participates in tunnels, relay, and cover traffic
- Participates in tunnel coordination gossip (§6.10) — advertises availability, responds to coverage requests, accumulates relay reputation
- Cannot decrypt message content, cannot access the trust graph, cannot perform any identity-bearing operation — it only handles encrypted transport

**Privacy properties:**
All WireGuard tunnel traffic is indistinguishable at the observer level — relay traffic, cover traffic, and personal communications from any layer all look the same. An observer sees encrypted WireGuard packets. They cannot determine which layer originated any given packet, which packets are personal communications versus relay, or whether the device is currently authenticated.

The mesh identity's accumulated relay history is genuine, which means any personal communication traffic that follows is invisible in the noise of a device that has been doing mesh work since install.

**Persistence:** The mesh identity accumulates relay reputation over its lifetime. This reputation has implications for the emergency erase flow (§3.9).

#### 3.1.2 Layer 2 — Self

The self is the user's core cryptographic identity. It is the root of all trust relationships and never appears on the mesh directly.

- Stored at full app-authentication level — requires PIN entry (if set) or explicit app unlock
- Never used to sign anything on the mesh
- Sole function: derive and authorize mask keypairs
- Holds the trust graph, network map, and threat context — shared resources that belong to the person, not to any specific contextual presentation
- Initialized only after the user authenticates into the app

See §17.2 for the full self/mask architecture.

#### 3.1.3 Layer 3 — Masks

Masks are contextual presentations derived from the self. Each mask has its own keypair, address(es), profile, and group memberships. All identity-bearing mesh activity — messaging, trust operations, profile exchange — happens through masks.

**Public mask:** One mask is designated the public mask. It handles operations that are public-facing or context-independent: trust endorsements, public profile exposure, and any action that isn't scoped to a specific relationship context. When a trust endorsement is gossiped to the network (§8.5), it is signed by the endorsing node's public mask key. This ensures that endorsements are attributable to a stable, network-visible identity without exposing the self root key.

**Mask selection for operations:** Operations that occur within a specific relationship context (messaging, file transfer, group participation) use whichever mask the contact was paired with. Operations that are network-wide (endorsements, Disavowed/Compromised announcements) use the public mask.

**Anonymous masks** are treated with the same security priority as the self and other masks — they are equally sensitive, not less. They differ architecturally in that they are isolated from the self: their keypairs are generated independently (not derived from the root), they do not share the trust graph or network map, and the backend enforces no linkable relationship between an anonymous mask and the self or any other mask. Anonymous masks follow the same initialization and PIN-gating rules as regular masks (Layer 3), not the reduced-security Layer 1 rules. The user manages them through the same interface, but the app cannot prove any link between them.

See §17.2 for the full mask architecture and keypair derivation.

#### 3.1.4 Initialization Sequence

**Trigger: device unlock (after first unlock since boot)**

1. Platform keystore becomes accessible at device-unlock level
2. Mesh identity keypair loaded from keystore (Layer 1)
3. WireGuard tunnel manager starts — tunnels established to known peers
4. Cover traffic begins
5. Tunnel coordination gossip begins (§6.10)
6. Relay participation begins

At this point the node is a full mesh participant. No identity context is loaded. No messages can be decrypted or signed.

**Trigger: user opens app (no PIN configured)**

7. `identity.key` loaded from device-unlock-level keystore
8. `identity.dat` decrypted using `identity.key`
9. Self (Layer 2) loaded into memory
10. Mask keypairs derived from root (Layer 3)
11. Trust graph and network map available
12. Message decryption, signing, and all identity-bearing operations available

**Trigger: user opens app (PIN configured) — step A**

7. App opens to PIN entry screen
8. Flutter signals "authentication required" to native layer
9. Native PIN input handles entry (bypasses Flutter — see §3.10)
10. Argon2id key derivation runs (fixed duration regardless of correctness)
11. Derived key decrypts `identity.key`
12. Steps 8–12 from the no-PIN path above

**First launch (no identity exists):**

Steps 1–6 proceed normally — the mesh identity is generated fresh and Layer 1 activates. After the user completes onboarding and a self is created, the normal app-open path applies on subsequent launches.

**Summary:**

| Layer | Initializes when | Required for |
|-------|-----------------|--------------|
| 1 — Mesh Identity | Device unlock | Routing, relay, cover traffic |
| 2 — Self | App open + auth | Trust graph, network map |
| 3 — Masks | Immediately after Layer 2 | Messaging, signing, profiles |

#### 3.1.5 Peer ID

The **peer ID** is the canonical identity reference for a mask (Layer 3). It is distinct from the mesh identity.

```
peer_id = SHA-256("meshinfinity-peer-id-v1" || ed25519_public_key_bytes)
```

Domain separation prevents cross-protocol attacks. Peer IDs are used in routing tables, trust records, and the network map. Addresses are routing targets; peer IDs are identity anchors.

The mesh identity does not have a peer ID in this sense — it is identified only by its WireGuard public key.

### 3.2 Trusted Channel Key Derivation

Trusted-channel symmetric keys are derived from the X25519 DH exchange between two peers:

```
shared_secret = X25519(my_x25519_secret, their_x25519_public)
channel_key   = HKDF-SHA256(shared_secret, salt="meshinfinity-channel-v1", info=peer_id_a || peer_id_b)
```

`peer_id_a` and `peer_id_b` are sorted lexicographically so both sides derive the same key regardless of which initiated.

The derived `channel_key` is used as the **WireGuard Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** for connections with peers at trust Level 6+ (the Trusted tier — defined in §8.1). Connections to lower-trust peers use WireGuard without a PSK. Both sides independently derive the same `channel_key` — no open key exchange is needed. An adversary holding only a peer's public key cannot complete the trusted WireGuard handshake without the PSK.

When a peer is promoted to Level 6+, the `channel_key` is derived and stored, then set as the WireGuard PSK. When trust drops below Level 6, the PSK is removed and the `channel_key` is discarded.

**Trusted-channel PSK and WireGuard tunnels.** Any keypair — mesh identity, self, or any mask — can establish WireGuard tunnels. The trusted-channel PSK derived above is an additional encryption layer applied within a WireGuard tunnel between two trusted-tier peers. It is not a separate tunnel type. An observer sees a WireGuard connection; the PSK is invisible beneath the WireGuard layer.

### 3.3 Multiple Public Addresses and Keys

Every node deliberately maintains multiple public addresses. A node bound to a single stable public address is a deanonymization vector — an observer correlating traffic across transports can link all activity to one identity via that address.

Each public address corresponds to a distinct keypair. Rotating or adding addresses does not require updating trusted-channel relationships with existing contacts, because trusted channels use the DH-derived PSK (§3.2), not the public address.

**Address types by layer:**

| Type | Layer | Derivation | Propagated In | Scope |
|------|-------|-----------|---------------|-------|
| **WireGuard** | 1 — Mesh Identity | Generated at install | Tunnel coordination gossip (§6.10) | Any mesh node |
| **Primary** | 3 — Mask | Deterministic from mask Ed25519 public key | Network map | Anyone |
| **Secondary** | 3 — Mask | Additional keypairs, same mask | Network map | Anyone |
| **Trusted Channel** | 3 — Mask | X25519 DH between two specific peers | Direct trusted exchange only | That peer only |
| **Group Channel** | 3 — Mask | X25519 DH with group keypair | Group member exchange | Group members only |
| **Ephemeral** | Any | Random per session | Never stored or propagated | Single session |

Public mask addresses and their corresponding public keys are broadcast in the network map. Any node can encrypt an initial message to a public address using the public key. Ephemeral addresses are generated for individual sessions and discarded immediately after use — they prevent session correlation without requiring key material management.

The WireGuard address (Layer 1) is propagated separately through tunnel coordination gossip and is never included in the identity network map. An observer cannot correlate a mesh identity WireGuard key with any mask address from the map alone.

### 3.4 Encryption Negotiations

Cryptographic primitive negotiation is a connection-level concern, not an identity concern. The specification for which primitives are used, how hardware acceleration is handled, and how downgrade attacks are prevented is in §5.0 (Cryptographic Primitive Negotiation).

#### 3.4.1 Post-Quantum Migration — PQXDH

X3DH (§7.0.2) uses X25519 exclusively. A sufficiently capable quantum computer breaks X25519 via Shor's algorithm, exposing all sessions — including retroactive decryption of traffic collected today ("harvest now, decrypt later"). This is not a theoretical concern under the §2.0 threat model: state-level adversaries routinely perform bulk traffic collection.

**Migration plan — PQXDH (Post-Quantum Extended Diffie-Hellman):**

The X3DH handshake (§7.0.2) will be extended to PQXDH by adding an ML-KEM-768 (FIPS 203) encapsulation alongside the existing X25519 DH operations. The combined secret incorporates both the classical DH output and the KEM shared secret, so security degrades gracefully — if ML-KEM is broken, X25519 still protects; if X25519 is broken, ML-KEM still protects.

```
// PQXDH extension to §7.0.2
(pq_ss, kem_ct) = ML-KEM-768.Encapsulate(preauth_B_kem_pub)

master_secret = HKDF-SHA256(
    salt = 0x00 * 32,
    ikm  = 0xFF * 32 || DH1 || DH2 || DH3 || pq_ss,
    info = "MeshInfinity_PQXDH_v1",
    len  = 32
)
```

**Wire format — KEM ciphertext in init header:**

```rust
PqxdhInitHeader {
    eph_pub:          [u8; 32],       // X25519 ephemeral public key
    encrypted_ik_pub: [u8; 48],       // Encrypted IK (see §7.0.2 — upgraded to AEAD)
    kem_ciphertext:   [u8; 1088],     // ML-KEM-768 ciphertext
    kem_binding:      [u8; 32],       // HMAC-SHA256(eph_DH_secret, kem_ciphertext)
                                       // Prevents KEM ciphertext substitution attacks —
                                       // binds the KEM to the ephemeral DH context
}
```

`kem_binding` authenticates the KEM ciphertext against the ephemeral DH shared secret. An attacker who intercepts the init message cannot substitute a different KEM ciphertext without also knowing the ephemeral DH secret — the binding check fails on Bob's side.

Bob verifies: `HMAC-SHA256(X25519(preauth_B_secret, eph_pub), kem_ciphertext) == kem_binding`. If mismatch, the init is **silently dropped** — no error is sent to the sender (to prevent oracle attacks). The sender's message times out and is retried via S&F or a fresh X3DH init. This silent-drop behavior applies to ALL X3DH/PQXDH validation failures (bad signature, expired token, unknown preauth key, KEM binding mismatch).

**Preauth key extension:** Each node's preauth key (§7.0.1) adds an ML-KEM-768 encapsulation public key alongside the existing X25519 public key. The KEM public key is distributed through the same paths and with the same trust weights as the X25519 preauth key.

**Implementation schedule:** PQXDH is a v1.0 requirement — not deferred. Sessions initiated without the KEM component (X3DH-only) are accepted during the transition period but flagged in the UI: *"This session uses classical key agreement only. It is not protected against future quantum attacks."*

**X3DH downgrade prevention:** After the transition period (defined as 6 months post-v1.0 release), peers that advertise a KEM public key in their preauth key MUST reject X3DH-only init messages from peers that also advertise a KEM key. A peer can only use X3DH-only when the recipient has no KEM key published. This prevents a MITM from stripping the KEM component.

**Deniability caveat:** PQXDH does not meet the same deniability guarantees as X3DH due to the KEM encapsulation. The KEM ciphertext is bound to the recipient's public key in a non-deniable way. This is an accepted tradeoff — post-quantum security takes precedence over deniability against a quantum adversary. The deniability properties of the Double Ratchet (§7.0.3) and message-level HMAC (§3.5.1) are unaffected.

### 3.5 Deniability and Zero-Knowledge Proofs

**Design goal:** No interaction between two parties should produce persistent evidence that a third party can use to prove what was said, who proved what, or that a specific interaction occurred at all.

This section covers two related but distinct mechanisms:

- **Deniability mechanisms** (§3.5.1–3.5.3): cryptographic and architectural properties that prevent evidence from accumulating — symmetric MAC-based message authentication, ring signatures for group membership, and connection deniability through routing.
- **Zero-knowledge proofs** (§3.5.4): interactive proofs used during pairing that prove key possession without transmitting the key and without producing a replayable transcript.

Both share the same underlying property: **transcript non-persistence**. No ZKP transcript, MAC state, or proof exchange may be written to disk, logged, or retained in memory beyond the duration of the relevant session. After verification completes, all transcript material is zeroed and discarded. This is an implementation requirement, not a suggestion.

For ZKPs specifically, deniability comes from two properties held together: the proofs are *interactive* (the challenge is chosen fresh by the verifier each session, so old transcripts cannot be replayed), and the transcripts are *never stored*. The proof is valid to the verifier in the moment; it cannot be used as evidence to a third party because the verifier could have fabricated a consistent transcript.

#### 3.5.1 Message-Level Deniability

Messages within trusted channels are authenticated using **HMAC-SHA256 with the ratchet-derived `msg_key`** (produced by the Double Ratchet, §7.0), rather than Ed25519 signatures. Since the `msg_key` is derived from shared ratchet state that both parties possess, neither party can prove to a third party who authored any specific message — both have the cryptographic capability to produce any MAC under that key.

The `channel_key` from §3.2 serves only as the WireGuard PSK and is not used for message authentication. Message-level deniability is provided entirely by the ratchet-derived `msg_key`.

Ed25519 signatures are applied at the routing layer for hop-by-hop forwarding authentication, but these cover only the encrypted payload. A forwarding node cannot determine whether the signature on a packet represents the original author or a re-signed relay.

#### 3.5.2 Group Membership Deniability — Ring Signatures

Group operations use **AOS (Abe-Ohkubo-Suzuki) 1-out-of-n ring signatures** over Ed25519 keys to prove membership without revealing which member is acting. Reference: Abe, Ohkubo, Suzuki, "1-out-of-n Signatures from a Variety of Keys" (Asiacrypt 2002). Implementation: use the `ring-signature` or equivalent Rust crate implementing Schnorr-based AOS over Curve25519. The algorithm is standard — the spec does not reproduce it here but the implementation must match the original paper's construction:

- A ring signature proves the signer holds *one of* the private keys in a set (the ring), without revealing which one
- Group membership proofs, message authentication, and admin actions all use ring signatures over the current member public key set
- An external observer can verify that *some* group member performed an action, but cannot determine which member
- The ring updates as members join or leave
- **Unlinkable at the ring layer:** AOS ring signatures are unlinkable — two signatures by the same member cannot be correlated by an external observer. This is the correct property for deniability. Linkable variants (LSAG, CLSAG) are explicitly not used.
- **Attributable inside encryption:** The sender's identity is signed inside the encryption envelope (§7.2 Step 3) before the ring signature is applied. Group members who decrypt the message can verify the sender; external observers see only an unlinkable ring signature proving *some* member acted.
- **Verification cost:** O(n) in ring size — acceptable for the 300-member group cap (§7.0.4). Each verification requires n Ed25519 scalar multiplications.

The ring signature scheme guarantees deniability at the cryptographic layer. The operational behavior during member transitions — including the superset ring model and rekeying policy — is specified in §8.7 (Trusted Groups).

**Ring signatures are used only in Private and Closed groups** (§8.9). Open and Public groups use attributable Ed25519 signatures — ring signature overhead is not justified when membership is not secret.

#### 3.5.3 Connection Deniability

**Design goal:** No connection event should produce persistent evidence linking a specific identity to a specific communication session.

This is achieved through wrapper node routing (§4.4) combined with ephemeral source addresses (§3.3). The mechanism is a routing and transport concern — the full specification is in §4.4 and §6. The deniability guarantee is: routing metadata is not preserved beyond the active session, and no persistent logs are kept of which identities connected to which.

#### 3.5.4 Zero-Knowledge Proofs — Sigma Protocol

During pairing, a **Sigma protocol** proves knowledge of the private key corresponding to a presented public key, without transmitting the private key or producing a replayable transcript:

1. **Commit:** Prover generates ephemeral keypair `(r, R)` where `R = r*G`. Sends `R` to verifier.
2. **Challenge:** Verifier sends a fresh random challenge `c`.
3. **Respond:** Prover sends `s = r + c*sk` where `sk` is the secret key.
4. **Verify:** Verifier checks `s*G == R + c*PK`.

The transcript `(R, c, s)` is non-replayable — `c` is chosen fresh by the verifier and cannot be forged. The interaction proves knowledge of `sk` without revealing it. The transcript cannot prove anything to a third party because the verifier could have fabricated a consistent `(R, c, s)` tuple.

**Critical: the Σ-protocol MUST run inside an ephemeral WireGuard session.** The flow is:

1. Both parties exchange public keys via the out-of-band pairing channel (QR/NFC/etc.)
2. Using those exchanged public keys, both parties immediately establish a WireGuard session — this is a standard WireGuard handshake using ephemeral DH (X25519), giving inherent forward secrecy
3. The Σ-protocol proof runs inside this WireGuard session

This ordering is essential. A Σ-protocol transcript observed over clearnet reveals both public keys at a point in time, enabling later correlation. Running it inside WireGuard means the proof is encrypted under ephemeral keys — an attacker who records the traffic and later compromises one or both long-term keys still cannot recover the pairing transcript. The protocol's PFS comes from WireGuard's ephemeral DH, not from an assumption about the transport layer.

All pairing flows use this protocol. The transcript is zeroed immediately after verification completes.

### 3.6 Identity Persistence and Key Accessibility

#### 3.6.1 Storage Model Overview

All key material — across all three identity layers — is stored at `AfterFirstUnlock` keychain accessibility. The differentiation between layers is not which keychain level they use, but whether the stored bytes are PIN-wrapped and whether the in-memory derived key is cleared on screen lock or timeout.

**Three functional capability levels:**

| Level | Condition | What's available |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| A — Infrastructure | After first device unlock since boot | Layer 1 WireGuard keys; Layer 4 outer decryption keys (if PIN entered this boot) |
| B — Receive-aware | PIN entered at least once since boot | Layer 4 outer decryption: knows a packet is addressed to this device, can queue it |
| C — Full identity | PIN entered and not yet cleared | Layers 2/3: message content, signing, trust graph, profiles |

**Screen lock and timeout behavior:**
- Device reboots: all levels reset. Layers 2/3 in-memory derived key is cleared. Layer 4 in-memory key is cleared. Only Layer 1 becomes available on next device unlock.
- Screen locks (no reboot): if "Require PIN after lock" is enabled, the Layers 2/3 in-memory derived key is zeroed. Layer 4 key persists (`AfterFirstUnlock` — unaffected by screen lock). Layer 1 unaffected.
- Timeout fires (no lock): same as screen lock if "or after timeout" is enabled.
- Screen unlocks: no keys automatically re-derive. User must re-enter PIN to restore Level C.

This means: a phone grabbed off a table with the screen locked cannot read message content or sign as any identity. It can still route mesh traffic (Layer 1) and identify inbound Layer 4 packets as addressed to this device. It cannot read their content.

#### 3.6.2 Key Material Storage

**Layer 1 — Mesh Identity:**
- WireGuard keypair stored at `AfterFirstUnlock`
- No PIN wrap — available immediately after device unlock, no user interaction required
- Generated fresh at install; never backed up

**Layers 2/3 — Self and Masks:**
- `identity.key` — 32 bytes of random data, the encryption key for `identity.dat`
- `identity.dat` — 12-byte random nonce + ChaCha20-Poly1305 ciphertext of the JSON-serialized identity payload

If PIN is configured:
- `pin.dat` stores: Argon2id params + salt + `encrypt(Argon2id(PIN), identity.key)`
- `identity.key` is NOT stored directly in the keystore when PIN is enabled — only `pin.dat`
- Loading Layers 2/3 requires PIN → Argon2id derivation → decrypt `pin.dat` → obtain `identity.key` → decrypt `identity.dat`

If PIN is not configured:
- `identity.key` stored directly at `AfterFirstUnlock`
- Loading Layers 2/3 requires only device unlock

**Layer 4 — Outer decryption keys:**
- "Layer 4" refers to the outermost encryption layer of the four-layer message encryption scheme specified in §7.1. These keys allow the node to identify that an incoming encrypted packet is addressed to it, and to unwrap the outer envelope, without being able to decrypt the message content (which requires the inner ratchet session keys from Layers 2/3).
- Stored at `AfterFirstUnlock`, PIN-wrapped using same mechanism as Layers 2/3
- After PIN entry, Layer 4 keys are derived and held in memory
- These keys survive screen lock (unlike Layers 2/3 when PIN-after-lock is enabled)
- Cleared on reboot or if timeout fires while screen is locked

**Private key serialization safety:** Private key fields are excluded from JSON serialization paths. The `identity.dat` plaintext buffer is constructed as:

```
[json_len (4 bytes LE)] [json_bytes] [ed25519_secret (32 bytes)] [x25519_secret (32 bytes)]
```

The entire buffer is wrapped in a `SecureBytes`/zeroize wrapper. On load, the decrypted buffer is split — JSON slice deserialized, key bytes extracted directly — then the buffer is zeroed before drop. The `zeroize` crate is used throughout to ensure private key material does not persist in process memory after use.

#### 3.6.3 Platform Key Storage

All layers use `AfterFirstUnlock` accessibility. Platform-specific storage:

| Platform | All layers storage |
|----------|--------------------|
| Android | Android Keystore, `setUserAuthenticationRequired(false)` — the Keystore provides tamper-evidence and hardware-backed storage but NOT access control after device unlock. PIN wrap (§3.6.2) is the sole enforcement layer for Layers 2/3 access. This is an accepted tradeoff: requiring `setUserAuthenticationRequired(true)` would force re-authentication on every key access, including background message decryption and relay operations, making Layer 1 mesh participation impractical. |
| iOS | Keychain `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly` |
| macOS | Keychain (may be software-backed on non-T2/Apple Silicon) |
| Linux (desktop) | Secret Service API (GNOME Keyring, KWallet); fallback: filesystem `0600` |
| Linux (headless) | PKCS#11 token preferred; Secret Service if available; `0600` fallback with startup warning |
| Windows | DPAPI (user-level); PIN wrap provides additional protection |

**TPM explicitly prohibited.** Intel fTPM runs inside Intel Management Engine (ME) — a known attack surface controlled by Intel, not the user. TPM-FAIL (CVE-2019-11090) demonstrated timing side-channel extraction of ECDSA keys from Intel fTPM in 4–20 minutes with local access. CIA differential power analysis attacks on TPM are confirmed (Snowden revelations). UEFI firmware has authority over TPM PCR values. Microsoft controls TPM 2.0 reference implementation signing keys. TPM must never be referenced as a security mechanism in this codebase. PKCS#11 hardware tokens (YubiKey, Nitrokey, etc.) are the correct alternative — genuinely user-controlled external hardware with no vendor backdoor surface.

On platforms where no secure storage is available, the filesystem keyfile is used with the most restrictive permissions the OS allows. Full-disk encryption is strongly recommended (documented in the setup guide).

#### 3.6.4 PIN Setup and Lock Policy

PIN setup occurs during onboarding. The UI specification — including the checkbox layout, skip confirmation flow, and timeout selector — is in §22.42 (Onboarding Flow). This section specifies the security behavior that the UI must enforce.

**Options presented to the user:**
1. PIN or password entry
2. "Require PIN after phone lock" — **default: enabled**
3. "...or after timeout" — **default: disabled** — with timeout selector (5 min / 15 min / 1 hr / 4 hr)
4. Skip — requires double-confirmation with explicit risk disclosure

**Behavior by configuration:**

| PIN set | After-lock | After-timeout | Layers 2/3 in-memory key cleared when |
|---------|-----------|--------------|---------------------------------------|
| Yes | ✓ | — | Screen locks |
| Yes | — | ✓ | Timeout fires |
| Yes | ✓ | ✓ | Whichever comes first |
| Yes | — | — | Never (until reboot) |
| No | — | — | Never (until reboot) |

Layer 4 keys follow the same clearing rules as Layers 2/3, **except** they survive screen lock — they are only cleared by reboot, timeout-while-locked, or manual sign-out. This allows the node to identify inbound packets as addressed to it (Layer 4 decryption) while Layers 2/3 are locked.

**Key clearing atomicity:** The Layer 2/3 key clearing operation MUST be guarded by a mutex or `Once` primitive. Both the screen-lock listener and the timeout listener call the same `clear_identity_keys()` function, which is idempotent — calling it on already-zeroed memory is a no-op. The implementation MUST NOT use separate clearing logic per trigger. Pattern: `static CLEAR_ONCE: AtomicBool`; first trigger sets it and clears; subsequent triggers check the flag and skip.

**Settings location:** Settings → Security → App PIN. Lock and timeout options remain configurable after onboarding.

#### 3.6.5 Identity Payload Contents

The `identity.dat` payload (Layers 2/3) contains:
- Ed25519 secret key (32 bytes)
- X25519 static secret key (32 bytes)
- Display name (optional)
- Public profile fields
- Private profile fields

On startup, if identity files exist, Layer 1 initializes from the keystore. Layers 2/3 load after PIN entry (if configured) or device unlock (if no PIN). If no identity exists, the onboarding flow runs after Layer 1 initializes.

### 3.7 Backup, Restore, and Safety Numbers

#### 3.7.1 Backup Types

Two distinct backup types serve different purposes. They share the same encryption envelope but differ in scope and size.

**Standard backup — reconnection:**
Gets you back on the mesh after losing your device. Small (typically < 1 MB). Contains no message history.

**Extended backup — full recovery:**
Everything in a standard backup, plus message history and contact key material. Larger (size proportional to history). Re-encrypts message content under the new identity on import.

Private keys are **never** included in either backup type. This is non-negotiable: a compromised backup cannot be used to impersonate the original identity or decrypt messages encrypted to the original keys. The tradeoff is honest — after restore, your cryptographic identity has changed, and contacts will see a key change warning.

#### 3.7.2 Standard Backup Contents

**Included:**
- Network map snapshot: known peer IDs, their public addresses, their Ed25519/X25519 public keys
- Public profile: display name, bio, avatar hash, visibility settings
- Public paired profile: display name, tagline, bio, avatar hash, contact hint
- Private profile: private display name, private bio, contact hints
- Trusted group membership records: group IDs, group public keys, member lists
- Contact trust levels

**Not included:**
- Any private key (signing, DH, or derived)
- Message history
- Double Ratchet session state
- Sender Key state

#### 3.7.3 Extended Backup Contents

Everything in §3.7.2, plus:

**Message history:**
- Message content stored as plaintext *inside* the backup encryption envelope
- Encrypted with Argon2id(passphrase) at the backup level — not with session keys
- On import: content re-encrypted with the new identity's derived keys
- The original session keys are gone; the backup passphrase is the sole decryption mechanism

**Contact key material:**
- All known public keys for each contact (including historical keys from rotations)
- Trust relationship records and endorsement history
- Group keypairs (public keys only)

**Session metadata:**
- Message timestamps, delivery status, read receipts
- Room/conversation structure
- File transfer records (metadata only, not file content)

Private keys are still never included. After restore, identity has changed — key change warnings fire for all contacts, which is correct and honest behavior. The trust relationship public keys allow contacts to verify the new key via the WoT propagation mechanism (§3.7.6).

#### 3.7.4 Encryption Model

Both backup types use the same encryption envelope:

```
EncryptedBackup {
    version:          u32,
    backup_type:      u8,       // 0 = standard, 1 = extended
    argon2id_salt:    [u8; 32],
    argon2id_params:  { m_cost, t_cost, p_cost },
                                // minimum: m_cost=65536 (64 MB), t_cost=3, p_cost=4
    nonce:            [u8; 12],
    ciphertext:       Vec<u8>,  // ChaCha20-Poly1305 of BackupPayload
}
```

Argon2id parameters below the minimum are rejected on import with an explicit error. These defaults are reviewed against OWASP recommendations at each major version bump.

**Parameter upgrade on import:** When importing a backup created with older (weaker) Argon2id parameters, the app prompts: *"This backup uses older encryption. Re-encrypt with current (stronger) parameters? This requires re-entering your passphrase."* If the user accepts, the backup payload is decrypted with the old parameters, re-encrypted with current parameters, and the re-encrypted backup replaces the original. The `backup_type` byte and `version` field in `EncryptedBackup` allow the implementation to detect parameter vintage.

**Passphrase requirements:**
- **Local-only backup:** Minimum 8 characters. Weak passphrase warning below 16 characters.
- **Cloud-synced backup:** Minimum **16 characters** (enforced in the Rust backend — the `create_backup()` FFI function rejects passphrases shorter than 16 characters for cloud-destined backups with error `BackupError::PassphraseTooShort`. Flutter cannot bypass this check). A cloud-accessible backup is an offline brute-force target for anyone who obtains the file. The 16-character minimum ensures practical resistance against GPU-accelerated cracking at the specified Argon2id parameters.
- Passphrase strength meter shown during entry. Passphrases (multiple words) are encouraged: *"Try a phrase like 'correct horse battery staple' — easy to remember, hard to crack."*

**The passphrase never leaves the device.** Argon2id derivation happens locally. The cloud provider (or anyone who intercepts the file) receives opaque ciphertext.

#### 3.7.5 Cloud Storage Policy

Cloud backup is **permitted** if and only if the backup is encrypted client-side before leaving the device. The app must enforce this — there is no unencrypted cloud destination.

**Rationale for permitting cloud:** The original prohibition was based on the assumption that cloud providers could read the backup. Client-side encryption before upload makes the provider's access irrelevant — a subpoena returns ciphertext the provider cannot decrypt. Prohibiting cloud entirely meant most users would have no backup at all, which is a worse outcome than a well-encrypted cloud backup.

**Implementation requirements:**
- Encryption is applied before any network transmission, in Rust, with no intermediary
- The passphrase is entered locally and used only for Argon2id derivation; it is zeroed from memory after use and never transmitted
- The app may offer cloud storage destinations (iCloud, Google Drive, Dropbox, etc.) but must display: *"This backup is encrypted on your device. Your passphrase is never uploaded."*
- The app must never offer an "unencrypted" or "sync my passphrase" option

**Recommended but not required:** Local physical media (USB drive, local network storage). Users in high-threat environments should prefer local-only.

#### 3.7.6 Restore Process

**Standard restore (merge into existing identity):**

Restore merges backup data into the currently loaded identity; the keypair and peer ID
are preserved. This is appropriate when the user still has their identity (e.g., recovering
lost contacts or message history) and does not want to force a re-pairing cycle.

1. User provides the backup blob (JSON) and passphrase
2. Argon2id derives decryption key; backup decrypted and verified
3. Existing identity remains loaded — no new keypairs generated
4. Contacts, rooms, and message history from the backup are merged in
5. Node connects using the merged network map as an updated bootstrap list
6. Contacts already paired see the same peer ID — no key change warning fires

**Extended restore (additional steps after step 6):**
7. Message history decrypted from backup using Argon2id(passphrase)
8. History written to local vault (§17.9) under existing identity keys
9. Trust relationships from backup pre-populate the trust store

**Mesh identity on restore:** Layer 1 is generated fresh at install, before restore. The restored identity operates over the new mesh identity's infrastructure. The mesh identity has no relay history — expected and normal.

**History sync alternative to extended backup:** After restore, a contact who has your message history may share it with you directly. This is always an explicit action with consent — never automatic. The prompt shown to the sharing contact: *"You are about to share message history with [name]'s restored identity. Their device keys have changed. Only do this if you are certain this is genuinely [name] on a new device."*

#### 3.7.7 Safety Numbers

Safety numbers provide a short human-verifiable fingerprint for a relationship. They enable out-of-band verification and are the mechanism by which key changes are confirmed as legitimate rather than adversarial.

**Derivation:**

```
identity_hash = SHA-256(
    "meshinfinity-safety-v1" ||
    min(peer_id_a, peer_id_b) ||
    max(peer_id_a, peer_id_b)
)

key_hash = SHA-256(
    "meshinfinity-safety-v1" ||
    min(ed25519_pub_a, ed25519_pub_b) ||
    max(ed25519_pub_a, ed25519_pub_b)
)

safety_number_bytes = identity_hash || key_hash  // 64 bytes total
```

Lexicographic sort ensures both parties derive the same value regardless of who initiated. The two-hash structure encodes two distinct facts:
- `identity_hash` — stable across key rotations; confirms you're talking to the right *person*
- `key_hash` — changes on every key rotation; confirms you have the right *current keys*

When a key change warning fires, the app can display which component changed. If only `key_hash` changed: normal key rotation, verify the new number. If `identity_hash` changed: different person entirely.

**Display formats (user-selectable):**

All formats encode the same 64 bytes of safety number data.

*Numeric (60 digits in groups of 5, Signal-compatible style):*
```
12847 39021 74839 20183 74920 18374
92038 17483 92018 37492 01837 49201
```

*Alphanumeric (20 characters in groups of 4):*
```
A3F2-KX91-B7M4-C9PL-M2WQ
```

*Word phrase (8 words from BIP-39 wordlist):*
```
anchor bridge cloud dance echo flight garden harbor
```

*Emoji (16 emoji from a fixed 256-emoji palette):*
```
🌊🔑🎯🌙 🦋🏔️🌺🎸 🐬🌿🎭🔮 🦅🌸🎪🌟
```

The user may change their preferred format at any time. Both parties must use the same format to compare verbally — the app shows which format is currently displayed.

**Verification flow:**

Accessible from any conversation: tap peer name → Verify safety number.

Shows the safety number in the user's preferred format with the note:
*"Compare this with [name] over a channel you trust — a phone call, in person, or a different app."*

On match: user taps "Numbers match" → relationship marked as out-of-band verified with timestamp.

On mismatch: user taps "Numbers don't match" → warning shown, key change review flow initiated (§22.43).

**WoT propagation for key change verification:**

After a key rotation, a contact who has out-of-band verified the new safety number can propagate that verification to the trust web, allowing mutual contacts to accept the key change without individual verification. The full propagation model — including the threshold mechanism and UI behavior — is specified in §8.5.

**Safety number in the pairing flow:**

During initial pairing (§8.3), the safety number is displayed to both parties after the Sigma protocol handshake completes. They are encouraged (not required) to verify it verbally before assigning trust above Level 2. For in-person NFC or QR pairing, the verification step is offered immediately. For remote pairing methods (link share, pairing code), the verification prompt appears after the first message is exchanged.

A verification passphrase established at pairing time (§8.3) serves as a shared secret for future out-of-band verification channels — it is distinct from the safety number but complementary to it.

#### 3.7.8 Cross-Device State Sync

When a user has multiple devices (§10.1.7), adding a new device requires bootstrapping its local state from the identity root. §10.1.7 specifies `DeviceKeyTransfer` for the identity root and `SyncHello` for ongoing message sync. This section specifies the full vault-level sync that complements message sync.

**What syncs cross-device:**

| Data | Sync mechanism | Direction |
|------|---------------|-----------|
| Identity root + mask keypairs | `DeviceKeyTransfer` (§10.1.7) — one-time at device addition | Primary → new device |
| Trust graph | Included in `DeviceKeyTransfer` snapshot | Primary → new device; bidirectional thereafter |
| Message history | `SyncHello` + version vector reconciliation (§10.1.7) | Bidirectional, delta-based |
| Conversation state (muted, archived, labels) | Version vector sync (§10.1.7) | Bidirectional, merge rules per field |
| Settings and preferences | Settings blob sync — see below | Bidirectional, last-write-wins |
| Vault encryption keys | Derived from identity root — never transmitted separately | Derived locally on each device |
| Network map | Merged on connection — standard map exchange (§4.1) | Bidirectional |
| Contact records (paired profiles, trust levels) | Included in trust graph sync | Bidirectional |

**Settings sync:**

Settings are synced as a versioned JSON blob over the intra-identity encrypted channel. Each device maintains a `settings_version: u64` counter incremented on every local change. On sync:
- Higher version wins for most settings (last-write-wins)
- Per-device settings (notification permissions, hardware toggles, PIN config) are **excluded** from sync — they are device-local by nature
- `ThreatContext` syncs across devices — changing threat level on one device updates all devices (the threat is to the person, not the device)

**Bootstrap sequence for a new device:**

```
1. DeviceKeyTransfer received — identity root, masks, trust graph loaded
2. Vault key derived locally from identity root
3. Empty vault created on new device
4. SyncHello exchanged with all existing devices
5. Version vector comparison determines sync direction per data type
6. Trust graph and contact records transferred (typically small — <1 MB)
7. Settings blob transferred
8. Message history: transferred incrementally by conversation, newest-first
   — user can start using the device immediately while history backfills
9. Network map merged from connected peers (standard §4.1 process)
```

**History backfill priority:** Conversations with recent activity sync first. Old conversations backfill in the background. The user sees their most recent conversations immediately; older history appears as it arrives. A progress indicator in Settings → Devices shows: *"Syncing history — 47 of 123 conversations complete."*

**Bandwidth considerations:** Full history transfer for a heavy user (100K messages across 200 conversations) is ~50–200 MB compressed. The sync runs over the standard mesh path at `Low` priority (§16.9.3) so it doesn't compete with active conversations. On metered connections, sync pauses automatically and resumes on WiFi.

### 3.8 Key Compromise Recovery

If a mask keypair or self identity is believed compromised (device stolen, disk imaged by an adversary), the user must:

1. **Trigger the killswitch** (§3.9) on the compromised device if accessible, destroying the local key material.
2. **Generate a new identity** on a clean device (via the normal onboarding flow or backup restore).
3. **Announce the key rotation** to trusted peers: the user signs a `KeyRotationAnnouncement` using the *old mask's* Ed25519 key (not the self root key — masks are what's visible on the mesh) that binds the old peer ID to the new peer ID:

```
KeyRotationAnnouncement {
    old_peer_id:      [u8; 32],
    new_peer_id:      [u8; 32],
    new_ed25519_pub:  [u8; 32],
    new_x25519_pub:   [u8; 32],
    timestamp:        u64,
    is_emergency:     bool,        // false = planned rotation
                                   // true  = emergency (any unplanned situation)
    old_signature:    [u8; 64],    // Ed25519 signature by old mask key over all other fields
}
```

The `reason: String` field from earlier versions is replaced with `is_emergency: bool`. The previous `reason` field leaked operational intelligence — broadcasting `"compromise"` announces to the entire network that an attack was detected and when. The boolean field preserves the only distinction that matters to peers: was this planned or unplanned?

Trusted peers who receive and verify a `KeyRotationAnnouncement` signed by the old mask key may automatically accept the new key at the same trust level, skipping the WoT corroboration requirement of §4.6. The user is still notified and can review.

4. **If the old key is no longer available** (e.g., device is gone and no backup exists), key rotation cannot be cryptographically proven. Trusted peers must re-pair directly with the new identity. The old peer ID is handled by the Disavowed/Compromised state machine (§8.2) — peers who have ground-truth knowledge of the compromise use the Friend-Disavowed mechanism rather than issuing ad-hoc revocations.

5. **Mesh identity (Layer 1) on compromise:** The mesh identity is not tied to the self or any mask and does not need to be rotated as part of key compromise recovery. Its WireGuard keypair has no derivation relationship with the compromised identity. Generate a new mesh identity on the clean device at install; it will accumulate its own reputation from scratch.

**Important:** A `KeyRotationAnnouncement` from an *unrecognised* old key is not automatically trusted — this would allow an attacker to pre-generate a chain of key rotations. The announcement is only accepted if the old key matches the previously known key for that peer ID.

### 3.9 Killswitch / Emergency Data Destruction

The killswitch permanently destroys the local self and mask identities (Layers 2 and 3) and renders all associated data unreadable. The mesh identity (Layer 1) is handled separately — see the Duress PIN flow below.

**Activation methods:**

1. **Emergency Erase menu** — Settings > Security > Emergency Erase. Requires confirmation.
2. **Configured gesture** — Optional. User-configurable. Disabled by default.
3. **Emergency Erase PIN (Duress PIN)** — A separate PIN, stored encrypted in the keystore. Entering it on the unlock screen looks identical to normal unlock — same UI, same one-second pause. The one-second pause is present on ALL unlock attempts to normalize timing.

**Critical requirement:** The killswitch must be activatable WITHOUT entering the normal PIN first.

#### 3.9.1 Standard Emergency Erase

Destroys all three layers completely.

**Destruction sequence:**
1. Self-Disavowed broadcast attempted (requires signing key still in memory)
2. `identity.key` overwritten with 32 bytes of fresh random data — permanently orphans the `identity.dat` ciphertext
3. `identity.key` deleted
4. `identity.dat` deleted
5. Network map deleted
6. Profile data deleted
7. Cached messages deleted
8. Mesh identity keypair deleted

Steps 1–2 are security-critical. The overwrite must complete before any deletion. After step 2, the ciphertext is irrecoverable even if the adversary holds a copy. This is irreversible without a prior backup.

#### 3.9.2 Duress PIN — Mesh Identity Preservation

The duress PIN flow is designed to be indistinguishable from a normal unlock while producing a plausible post-wipe state. The key insight: the mesh identity (Layer 1) has genuine history — real relay participation, real uptime, real reputation — accumulated since install. The self and masks get wiped; the mesh identity does not. Because reputation is stored against the mesh identity's WireGuard public key, and that key is not destroyed in the duress flow, the reputation simply persists in place — there is nothing to transfer.

**Duress PIN sequence:**

1. Duress PIN entered on unlock screen
2. One-second pause (identical to normal unlock — timing indistinguishable)
3. Foreground: reset profile loads immediately — app appears normal
4. Background, in order:
   a. Old self (Layer 2) and all masks (Layer 3) wiped using standard destruction sequence (steps 2–7 above) — Self-Disavowed broadcast attempted first
   b. **New mesh identity (Layer 1) generated** and stored — this becomes the infrastructure identity going forward
   c. **Old mesh identity retained**: its WireGuard keypair and accumulated reputation remain untouched. It continues operating until the new mesh identity is ready, then the new identity takes over for future connections. The old identity's reputation record persists in the local store under its WireGuard public key.
   d. New self initialized under the reset profile configuration
5. Duress PIN becomes the legitimate unlock PIN for the new self

**What an observer sees:**
- The mesh identity in use was created at app install ✓ (genuine)
- It has real routing history and relay reputation ✓ (genuine)
- The self is a fresh account — consistent with "I reinstalled" ✓
- No timing spike at the mesh layer — routing continued uninterrupted during the transition ✓
- The new mesh identity's freshness is explained by normal key rotation ✓

**Transition masking:** The old mesh identity must continue operating normally — routing traffic, responding to tunnel coordination, generating cover traffic — for a **gradual wind-down period** (60–120 seconds, randomized) after the new identity takes over. This prevents the sudden cessation of old-identity traffic from creating a detectable timing signal. During this period:
- The old identity accepts no NEW tunnel connections but maintains existing ones
- Existing tunnels drain naturally and are not forcibly closed
- Cover traffic on old-identity tunnels gradually decreases to zero over the wind-down period (§16.2 gradual transition rules apply)
- The new identity ramps up simultaneously — total traffic volume remains constant

An observer sees a smooth, gradual transition that resembles normal mesh churn (§16.3 — "routine tunnel establishment and teardown is normal mesh behaviour"). The old identity fades; the new identity appears. Neither event is abrupt. The 500ms window applies only to the internal key swap — the external traffic transition takes 60–120 seconds.

**Post-Wipe Identity Configuration:** Configured entirely in advance, stored encrypted alongside the duress PIN. User-selectable presets: Empty (blank slate), Light use (minimal plausible activity), Full decoy (populated with plausible decoy data). No prompts occur during the emergency flow — all decisions are made in advance.

#### 3.9.3 Remote Killswitch

```
RemoteKillswitchRequest {
    target_peer_id:  [u8; 32],
    requester_id:    [u8; 32],
    timestamp:       u64,
    signature:       [u8; 64],   // signed by requester's InnerCircle key
}
```

Requires InnerCircle (Level 8) trust level. The killswitch fires on first verified delivery. Subsequent deliveries of the same or additional remote killswitch requests to an already-wiped device are silently ignored — the device has no identity to wipe and no signing key to verify against; the request is dropped without error or response. Known limitation: a sophisticated adversary using a Faraday cage prevents delivery entirely.

#### 3.9.4 Interaction with State Machine

- Local killswitch: broadcasts Self-Disavowed before destruction
- Remote killswitch: counts as Self-Disavowed trigger
- Duress PIN: broadcasts Self-Disavowed for the old identity; new identity starts clean
- Post-wipe new identity is never Self-Disavowed

### 3.10 App PIN

The PIN setup flow, lock policy, and timeout behavior are specified in §3.6.4. This section covers the security enforcement properties of the PIN system.

**Architecture:**
- PIN wraps `identity.key` via Argon2id — see §3.6.2 for full storage model
- Without PIN entry: Layer 1 active, Layer 4 available (if entered this boot), Layers 2/3 inaccessible
- With PIN entry: Argon2id derives key, decrypts `identity.key`, full identity loads
- Default during onboarding: PIN enabled with "Require PIN after lock" checked

**Attempt counter:**
- Stored in platform keystore — same hardware-backed store as key material
- Persists across app restarts — killing and restarting does not reset it
- Tampering with or deleting the counter file when PIN is enabled triggers immediate Self-Disavowed broadcast followed by identity wipe

**Backoff curve (not user-configurable — security property):**
```
Attempts 1–5:   No delay
Attempt 6:      30 seconds
Attempt 7:      2 minutes
Attempt 8:      10 minutes
Attempt 9:      1 hour
Attempt 10:     24 hours
Attempt 11+:    72 hours per attempt
```

At 72 hours/attempt: 4-digit PIN exhaustion ≈ 80 years. 6-digit PIN exhaustion ≈ 8,000 years.

**Wipe threshold:** User-configurable. On threshold: Self-Disavowed broadcast first (requires signing key in memory), then identity wipe per §3.9.1.

**PIN input must bypass Flutter entirely.** The PIN is security-critical input that must never pass through the Flutter rendering layer:
- iOS: native Swift `UITextField` with `isSecureTextEntry`, result passed directly to Rust via FFI
- Android: native Kotlin `EditText` with `inputType="textPassword"`, result passed directly to Rust via FFI
- Linux: native credential prompt or direct terminal input if headless
- Windows: native credential UI

Flutter signals "authentication required" — the native platform layer handles input — Rust receives PIN bytes directly.

**Timing normalization:** Normal PIN, emergency PIN, and incorrect PIN all take exactly the same wall-clock time to respond. Argon2id derivation runs for every attempt regardless of outcome. Emergency wipe and identity rotation happen after the UI has already transitioned.

**Process monitoring mitigation:** PIN value must never appear in process memory in readable form. PIN bytes are zeroed immediately after Argon2id derivation.

**Biometric prohibition:** Biometric authentication is explicitly prohibited for any security-critical unlock path. In most jurisdictions law enforcement can legally compel biometric authentication. Biometrics bypass the emergency PIN concept entirely — there is no biometric equivalent of a duress PIN.

---

## 4. Network Model

### 4.0 Overview

This section covers how the network map is built, maintained, and protected — and how peers discover and route to each other.

- **§4.1 Bootstrapping and Network Map Propagation** — how a new node joins the mesh, the startup sequence, map trust typing by source, and the Group-as-LAN namespace extension (see §8.7 for full group network configuration).
- **§4.2 Network Map Data Structure** — the wire format of map entries, field validation requirements, and Sybil/storage-exhaustion defenses.
- **§4.3 Server-Mode Directory Nodes** — nodes that actively maintain map freshness, serve as bootstrap sources, and retain map history snapshots.
- **§4.4 Anonymization: Wrapper Nodes** — optional onion-routing layer for high-privacy connections; recipient-configured, trust-graph-routed.
- **§4.5 Map Update Authentication** — self-ratcheted map entries, signature requirements, sequence number rules, and replay/fork prevention.
- **§4.6 Key Change Validation via Web of Trust** — how the network handles the ambiguity between legitimate device migration and impersonation; WoT corroboration, two-channel verification, and the in-band fallback path.
- **§4.7 Map Conflict Resolution** — merge rules for non-key-change updates.
- **§4.8 Unified Discovery System** — the full taxonomy of discovery mechanisms with threat profiles, hostile-environment defaults, the autodiscovery pipeline, and path building.


### 4.1 Bootstrapping and Network Map Propagation

Mesh Infinity has no central directory server. Discovery is bootstrapped as follows:

1. On startup, attempt to connect to known **friend nodes** (peers in the local trust store at level `Trusted` (Level 6+, defined in §8.1) or above) first.
2. If no friend nodes are immediately reachable, attempt connection to any previously known node in the network map.
3. On any new connection, perform a **map exchange**: each side serialises their known network map and sends it. The receiving side merges the incoming entries with their local map using the sequence-and-signature rules defined in §4.5–4.7 — higher sequence number with valid signature wins; timestamp is a secondary sanity check only, not the merge key (a pure timestamp-wins rule is trivially exploitable).
4. The merged map is then gossiped to other connected peers, who repeat the merge process. Changes propagate across the network in rounds of gossip until they reach all connected nodes.

There is no guarantee of delivery speed. The network is **eventually consistent**: a new node appearing in the map will be visible to all connected peers within some number of gossip rounds, proportional to the diameter of the connected graph.

**Bootstrap redundancy:** New nodes connect to multiple bootstrap sources simultaneously. There are no "official" bootstrap nodes — anyone can bootstrap anyone. Map cross-validation uses majority view to resolve disputed entries.

**Map trust typing by source:**

| Source | Initial trust | Routing use |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| Direct pairing | Full — cross-reference anchor | Immediate |
| NFC peer-to-peer | Elevated — physical touch required | After WireGuard handshake |
| NFC tag read | Unverified — tag may be stale or planted | Read-only until pairing |
| Ultrasonic | Elevated — proximity required (~1-5m) | After WireGuard handshake |
| BLE/local transfer | Elevated — physical proximity signal | After WireGuard handshake |
