```
  A node relaying at or above COVER_TRAFFIC_TARGET_BPS needs zero
  synthetic cover traffic on that transport — relay traffic IS the cover.
  This reduction applies per-transport independently.
  The diversity floor (§5.10.5) still applies regardless of relay status.

self_hosted_boost:
  ZeroTier and Tailscale score 0.3 when using the vendor coordination server.
  When configured with a self-hosted controller (ZeroTier) or Headscale (Tailscale),
  the anonymization score is boosted to 0.5 — no third-party visibility.
  Self-hosted status is stored in PeerTransportPrefs and applied at scoring time.

reliability:
  health == Live, latency_ema stable    -> health_ema (0.0–1.0 from §5.11)
  health == Degraded                    -> health_ema * 0.5
  latency_ema == None (new transport)   -> 0.5 (unknown, assume average)

latency_score:
  1.0 / (1.0 + normalized_latency)
  where normalized_latency = T.latency_ema / LATENCY_REFERENCE_MS (default: 500ms)
  latency_ema == None -> 0.5

bandwidth_score:
  Low    -> 0.2
  Medium -> 0.6
  High   -> 1.0

battery_cost (inverse — lower cost = higher score):
  High cost (BT5, WiFi Direct, sustained BLE)  -> 0.2
  Medium cost (BLE, clearnet, Tor)             -> 0.6
  Low cost (USB, serial, Layer 2)              -> 1.0

diversity_contribution:
  If this transport type has zero active connections -> 1.0
  If this transport type has 1 active connection     -> 0.5
  If this transport type has 2+ active connections   -> 0.1
```

**Weight vectors by context:**

Weights sum to 1.0. Applied as `final_score = Σ(weight_i * score_i)`.

| Weight | Normal | Elevated | Critical | High priority hint | Stream hint |
|--------|--------|----------|----------|--------------------|-------------|
| anonymization | 0.25 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.20 | 0.20 |
| reliability | 0.20 | 0.20 | 0.20 | 0.25 | 0.30 |
| latency_score | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.10 | 0.30 | 0.30 |
| bandwidth_score | 0.15 | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.15 | 0.15 |
| battery_cost | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.05 | 0.05 | 0.05 |
| diversity_contribution | 0.10 | 0.10 | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.00 |

When `hint.priority == Critical`, the anonymization weight increases to 0.60 (Critical threat context weight vector used regardless of global ThreatContext).

**Per-peer preference boost:** If `peer_prefs` specifies T as preferred, add 0.15 to `final_score` (capped at 1.0). This can lift a peer's preferred transport above a higher-scoring alternative without overriding hard constraints.

Candidates are ranked by `final_score` descending. Top candidate proceeds to layer 3.

#### 5.10.4 Layer 3 — Multi-Transport Composition

Decide whether to use a single transport or compose multiple.

```
Single (default):
  Use top-ranked candidate from layer 2.
  Used for: normal messages, background sync, most connections.

Parallel redundant:
  Send identical payload on top N candidates simultaneously.
  First successful delivery wins; duplicates at destination are discarded.
  Triggered when ANY of:
    - hint.redundancy_preference == Redundant
    - hint.priority == Critical
    - top candidate health == Degraded AND second candidate exists
    - ThreatContext == Critical AND 2+ anonymizing transports available
  N = 2 (default); 3 if ThreatContext == Critical and 3+ candidates survive layer 1.

Split payload M-of-N:
  Split payload into M chunks, any N sufficient for reconstruction (erasure coding).
  Triggered when ALL of:
    - payload_size > SPLIT_THRESHOLD (default: 1 MB)
    - hint.stream == false (one-shot payload, not a stream)
    - 2+ candidates survive layer 1 with different bandwidth classes
  Default: M=3, N=2 (send 3 chunks, need 2 to reconstruct).
  Erasure coding: **Reed-Solomon** (systematic) via the `reed-solomon-erasure` Rust crate.
  Each chunk routed over a different transport where possible.
  Purpose: maximises traffic analysis resistance for large transfers;
  no single observer sees the full payload.
```

#### 5.10.5 Layer 4 — Diversity Enforcement

The diversity target is **3 active transport types simultaneously**. Falling below this target generates increasing pressure in the solver, with user-visible warnings at defined thresholds.

**Diversity state tracking:**
```rust
DiversityState {
    active_transport_types:   HashSet<TransportType>,
    traffic_share:            HashMap<TransportType, f32>,  // 0.0–1.0, sums to 1.0
    relay_active:             HashMap<TransportType, bool>, // relay reduces cover obligation
}
```

**Diversity floor and sliding pressure model:**

```
|active_transport_types| >= 3:
  Healthy. Solver operates normally.
  No diversity override applied.

|active_transport_types| == 2:
  Solver begins soft-preferring any available third transport type.
  diversity_contribution weight increased by 0.05 in scoring.
  No user-visible signal.

|active_transport_types| == 1:
  Sliding pressure based on traffic share of the single transport:

  traffic_share[T] > 0.60:
    Solver actively deprioritizes T in scoring (score penalty: -0.15).
    Ambient UI indicator shown (§20 — transport diversity status).

  traffic_share[T] > 0.80:
    Solver aggressively seeks alternatives regardless of score gap.
    Diversity override: select any alternative transport within 0.35
    of top score (wider gap tolerance than the 2-transport case).
    UI indicator becomes more prominent.

  traffic_share[T] > 0.95 for > 5 minutes:
    Explicit user warning: "Most of your traffic is using a single
    transport. This reduces your anonymity."
    Solver attempts to establish cover traffic on any available
    alternative transport even if no real traffic needs it.

  traffic_share[T] == 1.0 for > N minutes (N configurable, default 10):
    Stronger warning with specific recommendation.
    N is tighter in higher threat contexts:
      ThreatContext::Normal:   N = 10 minutes
      ThreatContext::Elevated: N = 3 minutes
      ThreatContext::Critical: N = 1 minute
```

**Relay participation reduces cover traffic obligation, not diversity requirement:**
When this node is an active relay on transport T, synthetic cover traffic on T is
reduced proportionally (see §5.10.3 relay_cover_reduction). However, relay participation
does NOT satisfy the diversity floor — a node relaying on only one transport still
has a diversity problem. The relay benefit is cover traffic efficiency only.

**Diversity override score gap:**
```
|active types| == 2, seeking third:  accept transport within 0.25 of top score
|active types| == 1, > 80% share:   accept transport within 0.35 of top score
|active types| == 1, > 95% share:   accept any surviving transport regardless of score
```

#### 5.10.6 Failure Handling

```rust
SolverFailure {
    reason:               FailureReason,
    relaxable_constraints: Vec<Constraint>,
    queue_eligible:        bool,
    estimated_retry_ms:    Option<u32>,
}

FailureReason {
    NoTransportsAvailable,       // hardware/all dead
    PeerUnreachable,             // peer has no compatible transport hints
    ConstraintsTooTight,         // hint requirements exclude all candidates
    ThreatContextExclusion,      // ThreatContext eliminated all options
    PayloadTooLarge,             // no transport can handle this payload size
}
```

When `FailureReason == ConstraintsTooTight`, `relaxable_constraints` lists which hint fields could be relaxed to find a solution. The application layer may re-submit with relaxed constraints — it does not happen automatically.

`queue_eligible = true` means store-and-forward is viable (§6.8). The solver sets this when: the peer is known, the payload is small enough for low-bandwidth delivery, and the failure reason is transient (peer unreachable, not hardware missing).

`estimated_retry_ms` is set when the solver can estimate when a transport may become available (e.g., Tor circuit building typically takes 10–30 seconds).

**Store-and-forward as solver failure path:** When `queue_eligible = true`, the message is handed to the S&F subsystem (§6.8) rather than returned as a failure to the application. The application sees a `Queued` status, not a failure.

#### 5.10.7 Per-Peer Transport Preferences

Stored locally, updated by direct peer preference signals and by observation.

```rust
PeerTransportPrefs {
    peer_id:         [u8; 32],
    preferred:       Vec<TransportType>,  // boost +0.15 in scoring
    rejected:        Vec<TransportType>,  // hard veto in layer 1
    last_successful: Option<TransportType>, // used as tiebreaker
    updated_at:      u64,
}
```

Peers can signal their inbound transport preferences in their network map entry. These are stored as `preferred` entries. A peer who consistently fails on a given transport has that transport moved to `rejected` after N consecutive failures (default: 5).



### 5.11 Transport Health Monitoring

The TransportManager continuously monitors the health of active connections via an internal event interface:

```rust
TransportHealthEvent {
    transport_id:           TransportId,
    peer_id:                [u8; 32],
    event:                  HealthEvent,
    measured_latency_ms:    Option<u32>,
    measured_bandwidth:     Option<BandwidthClass>,
    timestamp:              u64,
}
```

The transport solver subscribes to these events; they are not directly exposed to the UI.

**Latency baseline model:** Global per-node exponential moving average (alpha ~ 0.05). Baseline update is suspended during a detected latency spike — the spike triggers rotation, baseline resumes on the new circuit.

**Bandwidth estimation:** Precise measurement is replaced with a three-class passive system:
```rust
BandwidthClass { Low, Medium, High }
// Default by transport:
//   Low:    RF, Ultrasonic, Dial-up (V.90), CAN bus, USB serial, Telephone subchannel
//   Medium: BLE, USB serial (high-speed)
//   High:   Clearnet, WiFi Direct, USB tethering, Layer 2/PPPoE, Layer 2/raw Ethernet
// Refined by passive observation of actual transfers
// No active probing -- eliminates regular-interval fingerprint
```

- **Keepalive probes** sent on idle connections at a configurable interval (default: 25s, matching WireGuard's persistent keepalive recommendation)
- **Dead connection detection**: connections with no response within a timeout are marked dead and the peer is attempted via the next available transport
- Transport health metrics are exposed via the FFI for UI display

### 5.12 Ultrasonic Transport

Ultrasonic transport uses inaudible acoustic frequencies to transmit data between devices using their built-in speakers and microphones. No radio emissions. No network interface. Works through thin walls and at close range.

**Physical properties:**
- Frequency range: 18–22 kHz (above human hearing, within microphone/speaker capability on most modern devices)
- Range: ~1–5m indoors depending on ambient noise, speaker power, and microphone sensitivity
- Bandwidth: ~100–400 bps at robust encoding; up to ~1–2 kbps under ideal conditions
- Latency: ~50–200ms per packet depending on encoding and error correction

**Use cases:**
- Air-gapped device pairing (no other transport available or desired)
- Presence/discovery signal in sensitive environments (no RF emissions)
- Short token/key exchange between physically proximate devices
- Out-of-band verification during telephone calls (see §5.16)

**Not suitable for:**
- Sustained mesh routing (bandwidth too low)
- Environments with significant ambient noise above ~60 dB
- Devices without speakers or microphones (headless servers, some IoT devices)

**Encoding:**
Frequency-shift keying (FSK) across multiple sub-bands within the ultrasonic range. Error correction: Reed-Solomon. Framing: HDLC-derived with start/stop sequences. The encoding is designed to be robust against mild acoustic interference and to avoid patterns recognizable as structured data to passive audio analysis tools — the signal resembles high-frequency noise or codec artifacts.

**Privacy properties:**
The acoustic signal is detectable by nearby recording devices but its content is encrypted (WireGuard layer applies). Passive analysis reveals only that high-frequency acoustic activity occurred. Active analysis by a party who knows exactly what to look for can detect the subchannel exists but cannot read its content.

**Platform implementation:**
- Android: AudioTrack/AudioRecord at 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz sample rate
- iOS: AVAudioEngine
- Linux: ALSA/PulseAudio/PipeWire
- macOS: CoreAudio
- Windows: WASAPI

Requires microphone permission. The app must request this permission with an honest explanation: *"Used to receive data from nearby Mesh Infinity devices using inaudible sound."*

### 5.13 USB and Serial Transport

Wired direct connections between devices. Zero radio emissions. Suitable for air-gapped scenarios and high-security local connections.

#### 5.13.1 USB Tethering

When a mobile device is USB-tethered to a computer, the OS presents a network interface (RNDIS on Windows/Android, CDC-ECM on Linux/macOS). Mesh Infinity treats this as a local network interface and establishes WireGuard tunnels over it normally. No special handling required — it appears as a local IP interface.

**Properties:** High bandwidth (~100 Mbps+), zero radio emissions, requires physical cable, requires user action to connect.

#### 5.13.2 USB Direct / Serial

Raw serial transport over USB or RS-232/RS-485. No IP stack required. WireGuard packets are framed directly over the serial link using HDLC framing.

**Properties:** Low to medium bandwidth (9600 bps–3 Mbps depending on hardware), zero radio emissions, works without any OS network stack, suitable for embedded/headless nodes.

**Platform implementation:** Platform serial API (Android USB host, Linux `/dev/ttyUSB*`, Windows COM port, macOS `/dev/cu.*`).

**Discovery:** Serial transport has no auto-discovery. The connection is initiated by the user specifying a port. Once connected, normal Mesh Infinity pairing and network map exchange proceed.

#### 5.13.3 Serial as Infrastructure Bridge

A headless Mesh Infinity node (e.g., a Raspberry Pi) can bridge between serial-connected devices and the broader mesh. This enables mesh participation for devices that have no network capability whatsoever — only a serial port.

### 5.14 Layer 2 / Raw Link Transport

Mesh Infinity can operate below the IP layer, using raw Ethernet frames or other layer 2 framing as the transport substrate. This allows mesh participation on network segments that have no IP routing, no DHCP, and no internet connectivity.

**Core principle:** WireGuard packets can be carried in any frame that delivers bytes between two endpoints. The IP layer is not required.

#### 5.14.1 PPPoE

Point-to-Point Protocol over Ethernet. PPPoE sessions are established directly between Ethernet MAC addresses using PPPoE discovery frames (PADI/PADO/PADR/PADS). No IP address required at the PPPoE layer — WireGuard packets are the PPP payload.

**Use cases:**
- ISP-provided connections that present as raw PPPoE (DSL, some fiber) — Mesh Infinity can claim the link before any IP stack
- Direct node-to-node on shared Ethernet without any routing infrastructure
- Emergency bridge nodes where IP configuration is unavailable or undesirable

**Implementation:** Raw socket on the Ethernet interface, PPPoE session negotiation in Rust, WireGuard carried as PPP payload.

#### 5.14.2 Raw Ethernet (Custom EtherType)

Mesh Infinity registers a custom EtherType value and sends WireGuard packets directly in Ethernet frames, bypassing the IP stack entirely.

**EtherType:** `0x88B5` (locally administered experimental use; pending IANA registration — deployments should coordinate locally to avoid conflicts with other experimental uses on the same segment). Frames are sent to specific destination MAC addresses (unicast) or the broadcast address (for discovery).

**Discovery:** Mesh Infinity broadcasts a discovery frame on the local segment. Responding nodes send their mesh identity WireGuard public key. The responding node is then reachable via direct Ethernet frames.

**Use cases:** Air-gapped local networks, high-security infrastructure nodes, environments where IP stack is not available or not trusted.

#### 5.14.3 Dial-Up (V.90/V.92 Modem)

PSTN telephone line as a point-to-point byte pipe. A V.90/V.92 modem connection provides ~56 kbps. WireGuard packets are framed over the modem connection using PPP.

**Use cases:**
- Environments where internet is blocked but telephone infrastructure exists
- Rural areas with no broadband
- Infrastructure bridge nodes with attached modem hardware

**Properties:** ~56 kbps bandwidth, high latency (~200ms+), requires physical telephone line and modem hardware. Suitable for low-bandwidth mesh participation and key exchange; not suitable for voice or large file transfers.

**Acoustic coupling:** A modem with an acoustic coupler (handset-placed microphone/speaker) can establish a connection without a physical telephone line connector. This allows a dial-up connection from any telephone handset.

### 5.15 CAN Bus Transport (Plugin Category)

Controller Area Network (CAN bus) is a vehicle and industrial network standard. Mesh Infinity nodes embedded in vehicles, industrial equipment, or IoT infrastructure can participate in the mesh over CAN bus.

**Properties:** 125 kbps–1 Mbps bandwidth, short range (within a vehicle or building), no IP, deterministic timing, extremely reliable in harsh environments.

**Use cases:**
- Mesh nodes embedded in vehicles (police vehicles, military vehicles, fleet vehicles)
- Industrial infrastructure nodes (factories, power infrastructure)
- IoT device clusters

**Plugin interface:** Same `RFTransportCapabilities` plugin model as RF (§5.7), with `infrastructure_only: true`. Requires CAN hardware interface (SocketCAN on Linux, platform-specific on other OSes).

**Routing considerations:** CAN bus bandwidth is sufficient for mesh coordination packets and short messages. Large payloads (media, files) are not suitable. The transport solver excludes CAN bus for any payload above a configurable size threshold (default: 4 kB).

### 5.16 Telephone Network Transport

The telephone network — both PSTN (landline) and cellular — is a transport substrate for Mesh Infinity. Two distinct modes:

#### 5.16.1 PSTN Dial-Up Bridge

See §5.14.3 for the underlying modem transport. The telephone network provides the physical path; the transport is a raw byte pipe identical to a direct serial connection.

**Additional capability:** A PSTN dial-up node can act as a bridge between the telephone network and the mesh. Nodes that can only reach a dial-up number can reach the broader mesh through this bridge.

#### 5.16.2 Cellular Voice Subchannel

When two Mesh Infinity users are on an active phone call, their clients can exchange data inaudibly using a codec-surviving modem encoding embedded in the call audio. This is distinct from the ultrasonic transport (§5.12) — it must survive lossy voice codec processing (AMR, EVS, G.711) which destroys true ultrasonic frequencies.

**Encoding:** Narrow-band FSK tones within the codec's preserved frequency range (500–3000 Hz), designed to survive AMR/EVS compression. Resembles line noise or codec artifacts to passive analysis. Bandwidth: ~50–200 bps. Error correction: Reed-Solomon.

**Use cases:**
- Out-of-band pairing verification (see §5.16.3)
- SOS / distress signal
- Compromise notice delivery
- Short encrypted messages when no other transport is available
- Store-and-forward delivery trigger
- Safety number exchange and verification

**Properties:**
- The phone call is established through normal cellular infrastructure — Mesh Infinity has no involvement in call setup
- The subchannel activates automatically when both clients detect an active audio channel and each other's presence signal
- From any surveillance perspective: two people had a phone call. The audio contains noise that is indistinguishable from normal codec artifacts without knowledge of the encoding scheme. Active analysis reveals only that structured high-frequency activity occurred; content is encrypted.

**Platform implementation:** Uses the same platform audio APIs as §5.12 (ultrasonic) — AudioTrack/AudioRecord on Android, AVAudioEngine on iOS, etc. — but with entirely different frequency and encoding parameters. The ultrasonic transport uses 18–22 kHz; the telephone subchannel uses 500–3000 Hz to survive codec processing. Same APIs, different encoding. Requires microphone permission during active calls.

#### 5.16.3 Telephone Pairing and Verification Flow

The telephone subchannel is the most powerful out-of-band verification mechanism in the system. It enables full cryptographic pairing and verification without either party speaking, typing, or using any app-visible network connection.

**Pairing via telephone:**
1. User A initiates pairing with User B in the app
2. User A calls User B on their normal phone number (outside Mesh Infinity)
3. When the call connects, both clients detect the subchannel carrier signal
4. Clients perform the full Sigma protocol handshake (§3.5.4) over the subchannel
5. Safety numbers (§3.7.7) are exchanged and displayed on both screens
6. App notification fires on both devices (see §5.16.4)
7. Both users silently confirm in the app — no verbal exchange required
8. Pairing complete; WireGuard session established

Neither user has spoken anything related to pairing. The phone call itself is the verification channel. An observer who intercepts the call hears a normal conversation (or silence) plus inaudible noise.

**Reverification via telephone:**
Same flow, triggered when a key change is pending. App notification says "reverification" instead of "pairing."

#### 5.16.4 Subchannel Pairing Notification

When the subchannel detects a pairing or verification handshake, a notification fires on both devices. The notification is designed for silent, covert confirmation.

**Notification text — new pairing:**
```
🔒 Mesh Infinity Pairing Channel Active

Do not speak aloud.

A secure pairing channel has been opened with this call.
Are you speaking with [identity display name]?

[Yes]    [No]    [Yes — under duress]
```

**Notification text — reverification:**
```
🔒 Mesh Infinity Verification Channel Active

Do not speak aloud.

A secure verification channel has been opened with this call.
Is this [identity display name]'s new device?

[Yes]    [No]    [Yes — under duress]
```

**Button behaviors:**
- **Yes** — confirmation sent over subchannel, pairing/verification proceeds, both devices show success
- **No** — abort sent over subchannel, pairing/verification fails, potential impersonation flagged locally, user decides next steps
- **Yes — under duress** — externally identical to Yes (coercing party sees success), but silently triggers pre-configured duress actions: Self-Disavowed broadcast, distress message release (if configured in §15.5), alert to InnerCircle contacts (see §3.9.2 for the full duress flow specification)

**Delivery requirements:**
- Notification fires even if screen is off or phone is face-down
- Distinct haptic pattern (not the standard notification vibration) — signals the user to look without audio
- Notification must not display on lock screen preview — full content only after device unlock
- Times out after 60 seconds with automatic "No" if no response

**Subchannel confidentiality:** The notification text, the identity name displayed, and the user's response are never transmitted over the cellular call audio. The subchannel carries only cryptographic material. The identity confirmation is derived locally from the exchanged key material.

### 5.18 Yggdrasil

Yggdrasil is a fully decentralized end-to-end encrypted IPv6 overlay network. Every node gets a stable IPv6 address cryptographically derived from its public key — no central registry, no DHCP, no coordination server. Routing uses a spanning-tree algorithm with cryptographic addressing.

**Why it fits:** Yggdrasil's architecture is philosophically aligned with Mesh Infinity. Addresses are public keys. The network is self-organizing. There are no trusted third parties. A Mesh Infinity node on Yggdrasil gets a stable globally-routable IPv6 address that persists as long as its keypair does.

**Use cases:**
- Stable reachable address without any ISP cooperation
- Reaches Mesh Infinity nodes on Yggdrasil without knowing their clearnet IP
- Useful in environments where clearnet IPs change frequently (mobile, dynamic ISPs)
- Low-latency alternative to Tor for peers who are both on Yggdrasil

**Anonymization level:** Partial (0.6) — traffic is encrypted end-to-end but the routing path is not anonymized. An observer on the Yggdrasil network can observe that two nodes communicated.

**Implementation:** `yggdrasil-go` has a Rust bindings project; alternatively, Mesh Infinity can speak the Yggdrasil protocol natively (preferred — avoids external binary dependency). Integration presents as a virtual network interface (TUN/TAP) to the OS; WireGuard tunnels run over it normally. TUN/TAP interface creation requires elevated privileges — this is gated behind the same VPN mode permission that governs other virtual interface transports. See §15 (Security Policies) for the VPN mode permission model.

**Relay participation:** Yggdrasil nodes are inherently routing nodes — participation in the network IS relay. No separate relay setting needed.

### 5.19 cjdns

cjdns is Yggdrasil's architectural predecessor. Both use cryptographic IPv6 addressing and fully decentralized routing. cjdns uses a distributed hash table for routing rather than spanning-tree.

**Differences from Yggdrasil:**
- More mature, longer deployment history
- DHT-based routing (vs Yggdrasil's spanning-tree) — different performance characteristics on large networks
- Somewhat lower adoption in recent years as Yggdrasil has grown

**Why both:** Supporting both provides redundancy on the "crypto-addressed IPv6 overlay" category. A peer reachable on one may not be on the other. The shared abstraction: both appear as network interfaces to the OS, both derive addresses from public keys, both run WireGuard over the top.

**Implementation:** cjdns has a Rust implementation (cjdns-rs, community). Same integration model as Yggdrasil — virtual network interface, VPN mode permission required (§15).

**Anonymization level:** Partial (0.6) — same as Yggdrasil.

### 5.20 Blockchain-Dependent Networks (Experimental)

Some overlay and anonymizing networks require staking cryptocurrency tokens to operate nodes. These networks are fundamentally incompatible with Mesh Infinity's privacy philosophy — the blockchain is a public ledger, participation in token economics creates financial correlation vectors, and network health is entangled with token price. **These networks are always treated as deanonymizing regardless of their theoretical properties.**

They are available as an experimental opt-in category for users who consciously accept these tradeoffs — for example, to reach peers who are only reachable on these networks.

**Access:** Settings → Experimental → Blockchain Networks

**Per-network configuration** (stored separately from main transport config):
```
BlockchainNetworkConfig {
    network_id:         String,      // "lokinet", etc.
    enabled:            bool,        // default: false
    token_wallet:       Option<String>, // user-configured if required
    accepted_risk:      bool,        // must be explicitly set to true
    anonymization_score: f32,        // always 0.0 regardless of network claims
}
```

The `anonymization_score` is hardcoded to `0.0` for all blockchain-dependent networks. The solver treats them identically to clearnet. Users enabling these networks see: *"This network requires cryptocurrency participation. It is treated as non-anonymizing. Your activity may be visible to network observers and correlated with your wallet activity."*

#### 5.20.1 Lokinet (Blockchain-Dependent)

Lokinet is an onion routing network built on the Oxen service node infrastructure. Despite offering onion routing, its staked node model creates economic correlation vectors incompatible with our threat model.

**Why it's here instead of with Tor/I2P:** Service nodes stake Oxen tokens. Node operators are economically identifiable. Network health depends on token economics. These are not implementation maturity concerns (like I2P's emissary caveat) — they are structural incompatibilities.

**Use case for enabling:** Reaching peers who are only reachable via Lokinet. Not recommended for anonymizing your own traffic — use Tor or the native mixnet tier for that.



### 5.21 GNUnet

GNUnet is a decentralized, censorship-resistant networking framework with no central infrastructure. It includes its own transport layer, onion routing, and distributed storage.

**Anonymization level:** Full (0.9) — onion routing with no central coordination points.

**Alignment:** GNUnet's threat model aligns closely with ours. It was designed from the ground up for adversarial environments. The storage components (GNS, file sharing) are relevant to §11 and §6.8.

**Current state:** GNUnet is mature but has limited deployment. Rust bindings exist but are not comprehensive. Support is best-effort with similar maturity caveats to I2P.

**Relay participation:** GNUnet router participation enabled by default when GNUnet is enabled, same policy as Tor and I2P.

### 5.22 ZeroTier

ZeroTier is a software-defined virtual Ethernet network. Nodes join virtual networks and communicate as if on the same LAN, regardless of physical location.

**Anonymization level:** Low (0.3) — traffic is encrypted but the ZeroTier coordination server (or a self-hosted controller) knows which nodes are on which networks and when they connected. Self-hosted controller: 0.5.

**Why include it:** ZeroTier is widely deployed in enterprise and enthusiast infrastructure. Mesh Infinity as a ZeroTier client allows users to participate in their existing ZeroTier networks without running a separate ZeroTier app — particularly critical on mobile where only one VPN-mode application can be active at a time.

#### 5.22.1 Mesh Infinity as a First-Class ZeroTier Client

Mesh Infinity implements the ZeroTier protocol directly. It does not depend on an external ZeroTier daemon — it IS the ZeroTier client. This means:

- **Single VPN slot on mobile:** iOS and Android permit only one active VPN extension. Mesh Infinity's VPN extension handles mesh routing, ZeroTier, Tailscale, and Infinet simultaneously in a single tunnel. Users do not need to run a separate ZeroTier app.
- **Desktop:** Mesh Infinity manages its own ZeroTier virtual interface, independent of any installed ZeroTier service.
- **Authentication:** Users authenticate via API key (from ZeroTier Central or a self-hosted controller). The key is stored encrypted in the vault (§3.5). A first-time setup flow guides enrollment.
- **Network management:** Users can view member lists, authorize/deauthorize peer devices, and view network configuration directly within Mesh Infinity — functions normally requiring the ZeroTier Central dashboard or CLI.

#### 5.22.2 ZeroTier Authentication Flow

```
Settings > Network > Transports > ZeroTier
  → [Enable ZeroTier]
  → ZeroTierSetupScreen:
      1. Choose controller type: ZeroTier Central / Self-hosted
      2. Enter API key (from my.zerotier.com > Account > API Access Tokens)
         OR enter controller URL + API key for self-hosted
      3. Enter Network ID(s) to join (16-char hex, e.g. "8056c2e21c000001")
      4. Tap "Join"
      5. Network authorization: if network requires manual auth (private network),
         show waiting screen "Waiting for network admin to authorize this device"
         with the device's ZeroTier Node ID displayed for the admin to approve.
         Auto-approve networks: proceed directly.
      6. Connected → shows assigned virtual IP, network name, member count
```

Credentials and network IDs are stored encrypted in the vault. Re-authentication is handled automatically via the stored key.

#### 5.22.3 ZeroTier Network Management UI

When ZeroTier is connected (ZeroTierStatusScreen within Network settings):

- **Connected networks:** List of joined networks with name, ID, assigned IP, member count, and status (Connected / Awaiting auth / Disconnected)
- **Members:** For each network, list of authorized members visible from the controller (name, Node ID, assigned IP, last seen). On self-hosted controllers where the API key has admin rights, member authorization/deauthorization is available.
- **Relay indicator:** Shows whether current ZeroTier traffic is using ZeroTier's PLANET/MOON relay infrastructure or a direct peer connection. Mesh relay preference (§6.11) is togglable: "Prefer mesh relay over ZeroTier relay" (default: on — mesh relay avoids ZeroTier infrastructure visibility).

#### 5.22.4 ZeroTier Trust and Relay Policy

**Trusted context:** ZeroTier is a trusted context by default (§4.8.3). Network membership requires authorization from the network controller — this pre-authentication relationship justifies the elevated peer discovery behavior.

**Relay preference:** When ZeroTier's own PLANET/MOON servers would be used for relay, Mesh Infinity prefers its own mesh relay nodes (§6.11) instead. ZeroTier's relay infrastructure is visible to ZeroTier Inc. (or the self-hosted operator). Mesh relay nodes are selected by trust level. The preference is configurable; a warning is shown when ZeroTier relay is active:

> **"ZeroTier relay active"** — *(amber indicator)* — "Traffic to [peer] is routed through ZeroTier's infrastructure. For better privacy, ensure a mesh relay node is available."

**Self-hosted controller:** Users running their own ZeroTier controller eliminate the ZeroTier Inc. trust dependency. Self-hosted scores 0.5 on anonymization (no third-party visibility). Headscale for Tailscale serves the same role.

### 5.23 Tailscale / Headscale

Tailscale is a managed WireGuard overlay network. Nodes authenticate via Tailscale's coordination server (or a self-hosted Headscale instance) and receive stable addresses in the 100.64.0.0/10 range.

**Anonymization level:** Low (0.3) — coordination server knows network topology. Headscale: 0.5.

**Why include it:** Same reasoning as ZeroTier — wide deployment, single VPN slot on mobile. Additionally, Tailscale's exit node feature is directly useful as a clearnet routing option, surfaced as a first-class choice within Mesh Infinity's exit node selection.

#### 5.23.1 Mesh Infinity as a First-Class Tailscale Client

Mesh Infinity implements the Tailscale control protocol and WireGuard data plane directly — it does not depend on an external Tailscale daemon or the `tailscale` CLI. On mobile, this is mandatory: there is one VPN slot. On desktop, Mesh Infinity manages its own network interface independent of any installed Tailscale service.

**Implementation note — WireGuard over WireGuard:** The outer Tailscale WireGuard provides the overlay network path; the inner Mesh Infinity WireGuard provides mesh-level encryption and authentication. Both layers use WireGuard but serve different roles. The outer layer is transport; the inner layer is identity and routing. This double encryption is intentional and harmless.

#### 5.23.2 Tailscale Authentication Flow

```
Settings > Network > Transports > Tailscale
  → [Enable Tailscale]
  → TailscaleSetupScreen:
      1. Choose: "Tailscale (tailscale.com)" or "Headscale (self-hosted)"
         If Headscale: enter control server URL (e.g. https://headscale.example.com)
      2. Tap "Sign in"
         → Opens in-app browser (or system browser) to:
             Tailscale: https://login.tailscale.com/a/<auth_key>
             Headscale: <control_url>/register/...
         → User completes OAuth / SSO login
         → Auth key returned, stored encrypted in vault
      3. Device enrolls: shows assigned Tailscale IP (e.g. 100.x.y.z), device name, tailnet name
      4. Connected indicator appears in transport list
```

Alternative: **Auth key enrollment** (for headless/server nodes):
- User pastes a pre-auth key from the Tailscale admin panel
- No browser required; useful for automated deployments and nodes without a display

#### 5.23.3 Tailscale Network Management UI

When Tailscale is connected (TailscaleStatusScreen within Network settings):

- **This device:** Name, Tailscale IP, OS, tailnet name, connected/disconnected, last seen
- **Tailnet peers:** List of all devices on the tailnet (name, IP, last seen, online/offline status). Filterable. Tapping a device shows its details.
- **Key expiry:** If key rotation is enabled on the tailnet, shows days until key expiry with a "Re-authenticate" button. Headscale nodes: shows renewal status.
- **ACL info:** Read-only display of the ACL rules that apply to this device (what traffic it can send and receive). Edit is always done via the Tailscale admin panel / Headscale config — Mesh Infinity never modifies ACL.
- **Tailscale exit nodes** (§5.23.4): Selection and status.
- **Tailscale Serve / Funnel** (§13.15): configuration if applicable.

#### 5.23.4 Tailscale Exit Nodes for Clearnet Traffic

Tailscale exit nodes route clearnet traffic through a designated node on the tailnet, substituting that node's IP for the user's real IP for outbound internet traffic. This is functionally equivalent to a VPN in the consumer sense.

Mesh Infinity surfaces Tailscale exit nodes as a first-class clearnet exit option alongside mesh exit nodes (§13.2) in the exit node selector:

```
VPN Screen > Exit Node:
  ○  None (direct clearnet)
  ◉  Mesh exit node: [node-name] (Level 6, 3 hops)     ← mesh-native, preferred
  ○  Tailscale exit node: [tailnet-node-name] (via Tailscale)
  ○  Tailscale exit node: [tailnet-node-name-2] (via Tailscale)
```

**Security demarcation — Tailscale exit node active:**

When clearnet traffic is routed through a Tailscale exit node, a persistent indicator appears in the shell status bar and on the VPN screen:

```
[ Tailscale Exit: home-server · 100.90.1.5 ]    ← amber, not green
```

Tapping the indicator opens an information panel:

> **Via Tailscale exit node**
>
> Your internet traffic is exiting through `home-server` (100.90.1.5) on your Tailscale network. This hides your IP from websites but does not hide your traffic from:
>
> - The exit node operator (you, if this is your device)
> - Tailscale's coordination server (knows device topology)
> - Your Headscale instance, if self-hosted
>
> Mesh traffic remains end-to-end encrypted and is not affected by the exit node.

Contrast with mesh exit node indicator (green):

```
[ Mesh Exit: some-peer-name · Level 6 ]    ← green
```

Mesh exit nodes route through the decentralized mesh — no coordination server visibility.

**Availability:** Tailscale exit nodes are only shown if:
1. Tailscale transport is active and authenticated
2. At least one peer on the tailnet has exit node capability advertised

If no exit nodes are available but Tailscale is connected, the option is greyed out with "No exit nodes available on this tailnet."

#### 5.23.5 Trusted Context Behavior

Tailscale is a trusted context by default (§4.8.3). Tailscale membership requires OAuth authentication through the coordination server — this pre-authentication justifies elevated peer discovery and pairing trust boost. Headscale: same behavior; the self-hosted server is the authentication authority.

Peer discovery over Tailscale uses mesh peer exchange (not mDNS multicast — Tailscale does not route multicast). Mesh peers on the same tailnet are discovered by cross-referencing the tailnet peer list with the mesh network map.

### 5.24 libp2p Integration

libp2p is a modular networking stack underlying IPFS, Ethereum, and many other decentralized projects. Rather than a single transport, it provides transport multiplexing, peer discovery, NAT traversal, pub/sub, and stream multiplexing behind a single abstraction.

**What we use:**
- **Transport backends** — TCP, WebSocket as pluggable backends feeding into the Mesh Infinity transport layer. Note: QUIC is explicitly not used — WireGuard already provides encrypted multiplexed transport with better properties for our use case. Adding QUIC under WireGuard would be redundant encryption; replacing WireGuard with QUIC would lose WireGuard's authentication and PSK properties.
- **GossipSub** — battle-tested gossip pub/sub with configurable fanout and scoring; candidate for network map propagation
- **Rendezvous protocol** — lightweight peer discovery at known rendezvous points, useful as a bootstrap mechanism
- **NAT traversal** — AutoNAT provides NAT detection. Hole punching is explicitly not used — Mesh Infinity uses mesh relay nodes (DERP-style, §6.11) instead of STUN-coordinated hole punching. libp2p's Circuit Relay is also not used (our wrapper nodes handle this).

**What we explicitly do not use:**
- **Identify protocol** — automatically exchanges all listen addresses and supported protocols on connection. This is a metadata leak we do not want. Disabled entirely; replaced with our own capability exchange.
- **Kademlia DHT** — DHT queries reveal peer interest patterns (§4.8). Not used for routing or discovery. Content routing via DHT is a §11 concern (IPFS integration), not a transport concern.
- **Circuit Relay** — libp2p's relay is for availability, not anonymity. Our wrapper node model (§4.4) handles this.

**Privacy model:** libp2p is an implementation accelerator, not an architectural dependency. Our privacy model remains ours. All libp2p channels are treated as untrusted transport; our encryption and validation layers sit on top.

**External participation:** libp2p's protocol interoperability means external nodes (running other libp2p-based software) can potentially exchange discovery information and use shared relay infrastructure with Mesh Infinity nodes. The extent of this interoperability is configurable.

### 5.25 Native Mixnet Tier

The native mixnet tier is a **routing mode** built on top of the existing wrapper node infrastructure (§4.4), not a separate transport. It adds mixnet properties — fixed-size packets, per-hop batching with deliberate delay, mandatory cover traffic — to produce timing-attack-resistant routing.

This section specifies the protocol. The routing behavior is specified in §6 (Routing).

#### 5.25.1 Design Goals

- Defeat timing correlation attacks that Tor and I2P are vulnerable to
- No external network dependency — runs on Mesh Infinity infrastructure
- External nodes can participate using the open base protocol (§5.25.5)
- Mesh Infinity nodes use a superset of the base protocol with additional trust-graph-aware routing

#### 5.25.2 Core Properties

**Fixed-size packets:** All mixnet-tier packets are padded to a fixed MTU (default: 1500 bytes). Payload shorter than MTU is padded with random bytes. Payload longer than MTU is split across multiple packets. An observer cannot infer payload size from packet size.

**Per-hop batching and shuffling:** Each mix node collects packets into a batch window (default: 500ms). At the end of the window, the batch is shuffled and forwarded. An observer watching a mix node sees packets arrive in one order and leave in a different order with a different timing distribution.

**Mandatory cover traffic:** Every mix node MUST send at least one packet per batch window, even if its batch is empty. Empty batch windows generate a dummy packet (encrypted padding, indistinguishable from real traffic). A node that goes silent reveals when it has no traffic — mandatory cover traffic prevents this.

**Relay reduces cover obligation:** Per §2.1 principle 9 and §5.10.3, a node actively relaying mixnet traffic generates proportionally less synthetic cover. At full relay throughput, the relay traffic itself satisfies the cover obligation.

#### 5.25.3 Packet Format (Base Protocol)

```
MixnetPacket {
    version:     u8,                          // protocol version
    packet_id:   [u8; 32],                   // random 256-bit ID, for deduplication
    onion_layer: [u8; SPHINX_HEADER_SIZE],   // fixed-size encrypted routing header
                                              // SPHINX_HEADER_SIZE is a compile-time
                                              // constant derived from max path length;
                                              // constant size is a core Sphinx property —
                                              // variable length would reveal path depth
    payload:     [u8; MTU - 32],             // fixed-size encrypted payload
}
```

The `onion_layer` is decrypted by each hop using Sphinx (the cryptographic packet format used by Lightning Network and Katzenpost). Sphinx provides:
- Unlinkability: each hop sees only its own decrypted layer; cannot link incoming to outgoing packet
- Constant-size headers: header size doesn't reveal path length
- Replay protection: packet IDs are cached per-node for the batch window duration

#### 5.25.4 Participation Levels

**Mix node** — receives, batches, shuffles, forwards packets. All server-mode Mesh Infinity nodes act as mix nodes when the mixnet tier is active.

**Client** — sends packets through the mix network, receives at an endpoint address. Mobile and desktop Mesh Infinity nodes are clients by default.

**Gateway** — accepts packets from external (non-Mesh Infinity) nodes and injects them into the mixnet. Required for external participation (§5.25.5).

#### 5.25.5 Open Base Protocol

External nodes can participate as mix nodes or clients using the base protocol. The base protocol is a strict subset of the full Mesh Infinity mixnet protocol:

**Base protocol requirements:**
- Sphinx packet format (§5.25.3)
- Fixed MTU (1500 bytes, negotiable to 9000 for jumbo frame segments)
- Mandatory cover traffic per batch window
- Batch window: 500ms default (negotiable)
- No trust graph awareness required — external nodes forward packets without knowledge of the Mesh Infinity WoT

**Mesh Infinity superset (not required for external nodes):**
- Trust-graph-aware path selection (prefer trusted peers as mix nodes)
- S&F integration (deliver to offline recipients via the mix network)
- Wrapper node onion construction layered on top of Sphinx routing

The base protocol spec will be published as a standalone document for external implementors.

#### 5.25.6 Katzenpost Compatibility (Experimental)

Katzenpost is an open-source mixnet framework with a compatible design. Katzenpost support in the experimental menu allows:
- Using Katzenpost mix nodes as an alternative to native mixnet nodes
- Contributing native Mesh Infinity nodes as Katzenpost-compatible mix nodes
- Cross-network routing (packets can traverse Katzenpost and Mesh Infinity mixnet segments)

Katzenpost uses SPHINCS+ for post-quantum signatures and a slightly different packet format. The experimental integration handles translation at the gateway level. Maturity caveat: Katzenpost is production-quality but small-deployed; its anonymity set is limited by network size.

**Relay participation:** Katzenpost relay enabled by default when Katzenpost is enabled in experimental menu. Same relay_cover_reduction applies.

### 5.26 Traffic Obfuscation

Traffic obfuscation addresses a distinct problem from anonymization: not *who* is communicating but *what protocol* they're using. Deep packet inspection (DPI) can identify WireGuard traffic by its handshake pattern and packet characteristics, enabling protocol-based blocking even when the content is encrypted. In some environments (censored networks, hostile ISPs), being identified as a WireGuard user is itself a risk.

Obfuscation wraps WireGuard in a carrier protocol that looks like legitimate traffic. The carrier is not providing security — WireGuard still provides all security. The carrier is providing camouflage.

**This section resolves DQ-3 (traffic obfuscation against targeted surveillance).**

#### 5.26.1 Obfuscation Modes

**Mode 1 — WireGuard over QUIC (TLS camouflage):**

QUIC is a UDP-based protocol that uses TLS 1.3 for all communication. WireGuard packets carried inside QUIC look identical to HTTPS traffic from the outside — same port (443 by default), same TLS fingerprint, same UDP characteristics. A DPI system that passes HTTPS traffic cannot selectively block WireGuard-in-QUIC without blocking all QUIC/HTTPS.

This is the primary use case for QUIC in Mesh Infinity. QUIC is not used for its transport properties (WireGuard's are superior) — it is used as a TLS-shaped envelope that makes WireGuard traffic indistinguishable from web browsing.

```
Obfuscated stack:
Application message
  -> 4-layer application crypto (§7)
  -> WireGuard encrypt
  -> QUIC framing (TLS 1.3 handshake, UDP)
  -> Network (looks like HTTPS traffic)
```

**Implementation:** Quinn (pure Rust QUIC implementation). The QUIC TLS certificate uses a legitimate-looking self-signed cert or a real domain cert if the node operator has one. The ALPN negotiation advertises `h3` (HTTP/3) to make the traffic fingerprint convincing.

**Mode 2 — WireGuard over WebSocket:**

WebSocket connections are initiated as HTTP upgrades and maintained as persistent TCP connections. WireGuard over WebSocket is detectable with deep inspection of the WebSocket payload, but passes shallow inspection and many corporate proxies.

Better than raw WireGuard for corporate network traversal. Less convincing than QUIC for state-level DPI.

**Mode 3 — obfs4 (Tor pluggable transport):**

obfs4 is a Tor pluggable transport that makes traffic look like random noise — no identifiable header structure, no TLS fingerprint, no protocol signature. Requires a bridge node running obfs4proxy on the other end.

Strongest obfuscation against DPI. Requires infrastructure (bridge nodes). Cross-compatible with the Tor bridge network.

**Mode 4 — Shadowsocks-style AEAD stream:**

A lightweight stream cipher wrapper that produces traffic indistinguishable from random bytes. No TLS fingerprint. Server-side requires a compatible endpoint. Lower overhead than obfs4.

**Mode 5 — meek (domain fronting):**

Traffic is routed through a CDN (Cloudflare, AWS, etc.) that is unlikely to be blocked. The actual destination is hidden behind the CDN's domain. Expensive (CDN egress costs) and slower, but extremely difficult to block without blocking the entire CDN.

#### 5.26.2 Obfuscation vs Anonymization

These are orthogonal:
- **Anonymization** (Tor, mixnet) hides *who* is communicating
- **Obfuscation** hides *what protocol* is being used

They compose: WireGuard-in-QUIC over Tor is both obfuscated (looks like HTTPS) and anonymized (routed through Tor). The solver can combine them.

#### 5.26.3 Solver Integration

Obfuscation is a property of the transport wrapper, not a separate transport. The solver accepts an obfuscation preference in `SolverHint`:

```rust
SolverHint {
    ...
    obfuscation:  Option<ObfuscationMode>,  // None | QuicTls | WebSocket | Obfs4 | Shadowsocks | Meek
}
```

When `obfuscation` is set, the solver selects transports that support the requested wrapper. Not all transports support all wrappers — obfuscation is only meaningful for IP-based transports (clearnet, Tor, I2P). Proximity transports (BLE, NFC, ultrasonic) don't need obfuscation.

**ThreatContext integration:**
- `ThreatContext::Critical` — obfuscation strongly preferred; solver defaults to `QuicTls` if no explicit preference and clearnet is in use
- `ThreatContext::Elevated` — obfuscation available, solver hints toward it but doesn't force
- `ThreatContext::Normal` — obfuscation available on request, no default preference

#### 5.26.4 QUIC Implementation Notes

The QUIC wrapper (Mode 1) deserves additional detail since it's the primary obfuscation mode:

- **Port:** 443 by default (matches HTTPS). Configurable.
- **TLS certificate:** Self-signed with a plausible-looking domain CN, or operator-provided real cert
- **ALPN:** `h3` advertised — this matches HTTP/3 and makes the handshake fingerprint convincing
- **0-RTT:** Disabled — 0-RTT data replay is a security risk we don't accept even for camouflage
- **Connection IDs:** Rotated regularly to prevent QUIC connection tracking
- **Padding:** QUIC frames padded to consistent sizes to reduce traffic analysis from packet size correlation

**Platform implementation:** Quinn (pure Rust). No external dependency. Available on all platforms including iOS (no App Store restrictions on QUIC).

---

### 5.27 Traffic Unidentifiability

§5.26 covers obfuscation — making WireGuard traffic look like HTTPS. This section goes further: making it impossible for a passive observer to build a reliable signature of Mesh Infinity traffic at all, even if they know what to look for. This is an opt-in mode, not a default — it has real performance cost and is appropriate for high-threat contexts.

**Threat model:** A sophisticated passive observer (state-level DPI, ISP-level analysis, exit node operator) watches traffic flows and attempts to identify Mesh Infinity nodes by statistical fingerprint — packet timing distributions, size distributions, handshake patterns, connection lifetimes, protocol byte patterns. §5.26 addresses protocol fingerprinting. §5.27 addresses statistical fingerprinting.

#### 5.27.1 Polymorphic Encryption (Evasion Mode)

Standard encrypted traffic, even with obfuscation, has detectable statistical properties: regular packet sizes (from padding buckets), predictable inter-packet timing, consistent cipher suite negotiation. An observer who has collected enough Mesh Infinity traffic samples can build a classifier.

Polymorphic evasion mode varies these properties per-session in ways that defeat classifier training:

**Per-session cipher suite variation:**
Rather than always negotiating the same cipher suite, the session negotiates from a diverse menu of equivalent-security options. Different sessions between the same two peers may use different suites:
- ChaCha20-Poly1305 (standard)
- AES-256-GCM (hardware-accelerated alternative)
- XChaCha20-Poly1305 (extended nonce variant)

To an observer, two sessions between Alice and Bob look like they might be from different applications because they negotiate differently. Classifier training requires knowing all variants.

**Per-session padding variation:**
Standard padding uses fixed buckets (§9 padding policy). In polymorphic mode, the bucket selection algorithm is seeded with a per-session random value, so the same message length produces different on-wire sizes across different sessions. Bucket boundaries shift within a constrained range, making size-based fingerprinting unreliable.

**Timing jitter variation:**
§5.10 specifies timing jitter ranges. In polymorphic mode, the jitter distribution itself varies per-session — sometimes uniform, sometimes Gaussian, sometimes exponential — with parameters seeded per-session. A classifier trained on one jitter distribution fails on another.

**Handshake polymorphism:**
The WireGuard handshake has a fixed byte structure. In polymorphic mode, a synthetic preamble of random length (0–512 bytes of random data) is prepended before the handshake. The peer recognises the actual handshake start via a per-session agreed offset derived from the preauth key material. To an observer, the handshake start position is unpredictable.

```rust
PolymorphicSessionParams {
    cipher_suite:       CipherSuiteChoice,
    padding_seed:       [u8; 16],   // per-session seed for bucket variation
    jitter_distribution: JitterDistribution,
    handshake_preamble: u16,        // bytes of synthetic preamble
}
```

These parameters are negotiated during session setup using the existing preauth key channel, so both sides agree without the negotiation itself being observable.

#### 5.27.2 Memory Obfuscation of Key Material

This addresses a different threat: an adversary with read access to process memory (via kernel exploit, cold boot attack, or memory forensics after a device seizure). The goal is to make key material hard to find and read in memory even with direct access.

**Key material is never in plaintext in heap memory for longer than necessary:**

```
Standard approach (vulnerable):
  key_bytes = [1,2,3,...,32]   // plaintext key in heap
  decrypt(ciphertext, key_bytes)
  key_bytes zeroed

Obfuscated approach:
  key_xor_mask = random_32_bytes()
  stored_key = key_bytes XOR key_xor_mask  // stored form
  ...
  live_key = stored_key XOR key_xor_mask   // reconstructed only during use
  decrypt(ciphertext, live_key)
  live_key.zeroize()                       // zeroed immediately after use
```

Keys are stored in an XOR-masked form with a random mask that changes on each access. The plaintext key exists in memory only for the duration of a single cryptographic operation, then is immediately zeroed. This does not protect against an attacker who can observe memory during an active operation, but significantly raises the bar for forensic extraction after the fact.

**Guard pages:** Key storage regions are flanked by guard pages (memory mapped as no-access). Any read or write outside the key storage area triggers a fault, which can be caught and used to zero all sensitive material before the process terminates. This detects buffer overflow exploits attempting to read adjacent key memory.

**mlock and swap prevention:** All memory regions containing key material are locked with `mlock()` / `VirtualLock()` to prevent the OS from swapping them to disk. This is already specified in §10.0.0 and §3 pending pass — the polymorphic mode applies the same requirement to all active session keys at the transport layer.

#### 5.27.3 DPI Resistance for Clearnet Users

Clearnet users accessing Garden or other services via the web bridge have no mesh client — they are using a standard browser. They cannot benefit from WireGuard-level obfuscation. But we can extend meaningful DPI resistance to them through the bridge layer:

**TLS fingerprint normalization:**
The clearnet bridge presents a TLS fingerprint that matches common legitimate traffic (matching the JA3 fingerprint of popular browsers). This makes the bridge's TLS handshake indistinguishable from a normal web server at the fingerprint level.

**HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 support:**
The bridge supports HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 (QUIC). H3 traffic is particularly hard to inspect because QUIC encrypts all headers.

**Certificate transparency and legitimate certs:**
The bridge uses Let's Encrypt or equivalent certificates, so the certificate chain is identical to any other legitimate HTTPS server. There is no self-signed cert that could serve as a fingerprint.

**Response timing normalization:**
DPI correlation attacks can sometimes identify specific applications by response timing patterns (how long between request and response for different content types). The bridge introduces controlled jitter in response timing to blur these patterns.

**Pluggable transport bootstrapping for censored regions:**
Users in censored regions attempting to install Mesh Infinity or reach the clearnet bridge may find the bridge itself blocked. The bootstrap problem: how do you get your first connection when the normal path is blocked?

Solutions, in order of strength:
1. **Domain fronting via clearnet bridge** — bridge domain is fronted behind a CDN that cannot be blocked selectively. Noted in §5.26 Mode 5 (meek). Expensive but effective.
2. **Mirror domains** — multiple clearnet bridge addresses, any one of which may be unblocked. App ships with a list; tries each.
3. **Built-in bootstrap nodes** — known mesh nodes that can act as initial relays, reachable via obfs4 or other pluggable transports.
4. **Community-shared bridges** — users in non-censored regions can share their node as a bootstrap bridge for censored-region users. Opt-in, bandwidth-limited.

The bootstrap process is the hardest part of censorship resistance. It is specified here as a design goal; full implementation is deferred to a focused censorship-resistance pass.

#### 5.27.4 Hardware Acceleration Integration

Polymorphic mode has CPU cost. To prevent performance from being a barrier to enabling it:

**Hardware detection and fallback:**
At startup, the crypto module detects available hardware acceleration:
- AES-NI (x86/x64) — enables AES-256-GCM at near-line-speed
- ARMv8 Crypto Extensions — enables AES and SHA acceleration on mobile
- Apple Secure Enclave — key storage and operation offload (already §3.6)
- AVX2/AVX-512 — enables vectorized ChaCha20

When hardware acceleration is available, the performance cost of polymorphic variation is negligible — the CPU overhead is dominated by the hardware-accelerated primitive, not the variation overhead. On devices without hardware acceleration, polymorphic mode carries a measurable CPU penalty and is highlighted as such in the UI: *"Unidentifiability mode is active. Older devices may experience slower performance."*

#### 5.27.5 Activation

Traffic unidentifiability mode is opt-in via `ThreatContext` and explicit user setting:

```rust
UnidentifiabilityMode {
    Disabled,           // §5.26 obfuscation only (default)
    Enabled,            // full §5.27 polymorphic mode
    Auto,               // enabled when ThreatContext >= Elevated
}
```

This is a per-session setting negotiated between peers — both sides must support and agree to it. If one side doesn't support polymorphic mode, the session falls back gracefully to standard §5.26 obfuscation. Capability is advertised in the session handshake extension.

---

### 5.28 Offline Transport

Offline transport is sneakernet — physical media, external storage services, and other non-networked carriers treated as first-class transport mechanisms. The mesh packet format is identical to any other transport; the "medium" is physical rather than radio or wire.

**Design principle:** The network layer doesn't care how bits get from A to B. A USB drive physically carried between two nodes is a valid transport. A shared S3 bucket that both nodes poll is a valid relay. An air-gapped machine reading a QR code is a valid channel. All of these are specced here as transport mechanisms with defined wire formats.

#### 5.28.1 Physical Transfer Header

All offline transport bundles carry a physical transfer header prepended to the standard mesh packet stream. This header determines routing behaviour on arrival:

```rust
PhysicalTransferHeader {
    magic:          [u8; 4],    // 0x4D_49_4F_54 ("MIOT") — identifies Mesh Infinity offline transfer
    version:        u8,         // format version; currently 1
    routing_mode:   RoutingMode,
    source_id:      [u8; 32],   // sender's mesh peer ID (or [0u8;32] for anonymous)
    dest_id:        Option<[u8; 32]>,  // destination peer ID if point-to-point; None if routed
    bundle_id:      [u8; 16],   // random; deduplication across multiple delivery attempts
    created_at:     u64,        // unix timestamp; receiver rejects if too stale (default: 30 days)
    expires_at:     u64,        // receiver deletes undelivered bundle after this
    packet_count:   u32,        // number of mesh packets in this bundle
    total_size:     u64,        // total bytes of all packets (excluding this header)
    checksum:       [u8; 32],   // SHA-256 of all packet bytes; integrity check on arrival
}

RoutingMode {
    PointToPoint,   // dest_id is the final recipient; receiver delivers directly or holds for pickup
    NeedsRouting,   // dest_id is the next intended hop or None for best-effort; receiver injects
                    // into mesh routing; behaves like a store-and-forward node on arrival
    Broadcast,      // no specific destination; all receiving nodes process as applicable
}
```

The receiving node:
1. Verifies `magic` and `version`
2. Checks `expires_at` — if past, discards silently
3. Verifies `checksum` against packet bytes — on mismatch, discards
4. Deduplicates on `bundle_id` — already-seen bundles are discarded silently
5. Routes or delivers based on `routing_mode`

#### 5.28.2 Physical Media Transport

Physical media (USB drives, SD cards, optical discs, NFC tags with payload, printed QR codes) are explicitly supported carriers.

**Initiating a physical media export:**
The user selects "Export to media" in the app. The app:
1. Gathers all queued outbound mesh packets (those waiting for offline delivery)
2. Wraps them with a `PhysicalTransferHeader`
3. Encrypts the bundle with the recipient's public key (for point-to-point) or leaves as encrypted mesh packets (for routed/broadcast)
4. Writes the file as `meshinfinity_bundle_{bundle_id_hex}.mib` to the target media

The `.mib` extension is Mesh Infinity Bundle — a flat binary file: header + packet stream.

**Importing from physical media:**
The user selects "Import from media" in the app. The app scans for `.mib` files on accessible media (no autorun, always manual). Found bundles are displayed with: source ID, creation time, packet count, estimated size. User confirms import. App processes per §5.28.1.

**NFC payload:** For small bundles (<16 KB), the bundle can be written to an NFC tag in the standard `NFCTagPayload` format (§5.9) with `payload_type: OfflineBundle`. The receiving device reads the tag, extracts the bundle, and processes it normally.

**QR codes:** For very small bundles (<2 KB after encoding), a bundle can be encoded as a QR code for camera-based transfer. The QR encodes the binary bundle in Base45. Multiple QR codes with sequence numbers can carry larger bundles — the app assembles them in order before processing.

**Security:** Physical media is untrusted. The standard mesh packet encryption applies — receiving a bundle from USB doesn't grant elevated trust. Malformed bundles are discarded. `expires_at` prevents replay of old bundles. `bundle_id` deduplication prevents double-delivery.

#### 5.28.3 External Storage Relay

External storage services (S3-compatible, Dropbox, WebDAV, rclone-compatible targets, NAS shares) can act as async relay points. Node A writes a bundle; Node B reads it.

This is configured at two levels:

**Node-level (server-mode nodes):**
Server-mode nodes can be configured with one or more external storage relay endpoints. The node periodically polls configured endpoints for incoming bundles and writes outbound bundles to them. This runs as a background service.

```rust
ExternalStorageRelay {
    relay_id:       String,      // local name for this relay config
    backend:        StorageBackend,
    poll_interval:  Duration,    // how often to check for incoming (default: 5 minutes)
    outbox_prefix:  String,      // path prefix for outbound bundles
    inbox_prefix:   String,      // path prefix for inbound bundles
    direction:      RelayDirection,
}

StorageBackend {
    S3Compatible { endpoint: String, bucket: String, credentials: CredentialRef },
    Rclone       { remote: String, path: String },
    WebDAV       { url: String, credentials: CredentialRef },
    LocalPath    { path: String },  // NAS mount, shared drive, etc.
}

RelayDirection { InboundOnly, OutboundOnly, Bidirectional }
```

**User-level (any node, opt-in):**
Any mesh node (not just server-mode) can be configured with a personal external storage relay — e.g., their own Dropbox or a shared folder on a NAS. The user initiates a manual sync in the app (no background polling by default, to avoid unintended data writes to personal cloud accounts). Auto-sync is opt-in with explicit user configuration.

**Bundle naming on external storage:**
Bundles are stored as `{inbox_prefix}/{dest_peer_id_hex}/{bundle_id_hex}.mib`. This enables efficient filtering — a node only downloads bundles addressed to its own peer IDs. Node A writes to `{outbox_prefix}/{dest_id}/{bundle_id}.mib`; Node B reads from its own `{inbox_prefix}/{own_id}/`.

**Security:** The external storage provider sees encrypted bundle files. It cannot read content. It knows: that bundles exist, their sizes, and the destination peer ID (used as a path component). For stronger metadata protection, use a flat namespace (a single shared prefix) at the cost of requiring full listing to find relevant bundles.

#### 5.28.4 Air-Gap Bridge

An air-gapped node has no network connectivity by design. Offline transport is its only path to the mesh.

The air-gap scenario:
1. A "courier" node is connected to the mesh normally and accumulates outbound bundles for the air-gapped node
2. The courier exports bundles to physical media
3. Physical media crosses the air gap (physically carried)
4. The air-gapped node imports from media, processes packets, generates responses
5. Responses are exported to media, physical carry back to the courier
6. Courier imports and delivers to the mesh

This is specced as a supported operational mode. The `created_at` / `expires_at` fields in the header accommodate the latency of physical transport (default `expires_at` is 30 days — configurable for longer air-gap cycles). The air-gapped node maintains its own message queue and identity state between courier visits.

**Trusted courier model:** The courier node holds bundles but cannot read them (mesh packet encryption applies). A malicious or compromised courier can delay, replay (blocked by `bundle_id` dedup), or drop bundles — but cannot decrypt or forge them.

#### 5.28.5 Solver Integration

Offline transport is a last-resort transport in the solver's hierarchy — used when all network transports are unavailable or explicitly when a node is configured for offline-only operation.

```rust
ObfuscationMode / TransportMode additions:
    OfflinePhysicalMedia,
    OfflineExternalStorage,
```

The solver advertises offline transport capability in the node's capability flags. Peers that know a node is air-gapped or offline-preferred can route packets through the offline path rather than treating the node as unreachable.

---

### 5.29 Community Overlay Networks

Community overlay networks are volunteer-operated IP networks that exist alongside (or instead of) the commercial internet. They provide censorship-resistant connectivity but generally offer low or no anonymization. Mesh Infinity can route WireGuard over them as additional transport options.

These are distinct from anonymizing networks (Tor, I2P) and from obfuscation wrappers (§5.26). Their value is: censorship resistance through decentralized BGP routing, community ownership, and independence from commercial ISPs.

**General properties of community overlay networks:**

| Property | Tor/I2P | Community overlays |
|----------|---------|--------------------|
| Anonymization | High | Low–None |
| Latency | High | Low–Medium |
| Bandwidth | Limited | Higher |
| Censorship resistance | Very high | High |
| Service hosting | Onion/Eepsite | Direct IP |
| Trust model | Zero-trust | Community trust |

#### 5.29.1 Anonet

Anonet is a friend-to-friend network using VPNs and software BGP routers. It makes it difficult to learn the identities of others on the network, enabling anonymous service hosting over IPv4 and IPv6.

**Properties:**
- BGP routing with OpenVPN tunnels between peers
- Decentralized — no central authority
- Anonymous service hosting is a primary use case
- Traffic analysis is possible by BGP peers
- Active but small community

**Mesh Infinity integration:** Route WireGuard over an Anonet connection exactly as with clearnet. Anonet provides an alternative IP layer; WireGuard provides encryption. Useful for hosting Garden instances and mesh relay nodes accessible to Anonet users without revealing the operator's clearnet IP.

**Anonymization score:** 2/10. BGP peers can observe routing. Better than clearnet for service hosting anonymity; incomparable to Tor.

**Service hosting value:** High. A Garden instance hosted on Anonet is reachable by Anonet users without a clearnet presence. Combined with mesh-native access, this provides redundant connectivity paths.

#### 5.29.2 dn42

dn42 (Decentralized Network 42) is a large dynamic VPN network using internet technologies: BGP, WHOIS database, DNS, and multiple tunnel protocols (GRE, OpenVPN, Tinc, IPsec). The 172.22.0.0/15 address space and private AS numbers are used.

**Properties:**
- Significantly larger and more active than Anonet
- Full BGP routing, allocated address space, WHOIS registry
- Multiple tunnel protocol support
- Active routing community with established peering infrastructure
- No anonymization — BGP peers see routing tables

**Mesh Infinity integration:** Same as Anonet — route WireGuard over dn42 connections. dn42 provides a richer routing infrastructure than Anonet with better uptime and more peers.

**Anonymization score:** 1/10. No anonymization — dn42 is explicitly a routing exercise, not an anonymizing network. BGP tables are visible to peers.

**Combined Anonet/dn42 note:** Some nodes participate in both networks. §5.29.1 and §5.29.2 are specced separately because the communities, address spaces, and tunnel protocols differ, but the Mesh Infinity integration model is identical for both.

#### 5.29.3 cjdns / Hyperboria

cjdns is a fully specced standalone transport in §5.19. As a community overlay network it uses the same BGP-style routing as Anonet and dn42. Refer to §5.19 for the complete specification. Solver entry: `TransportOverlay::Cjdns`.


#### 5.29.4 ZeroTier

ZeroTier is a fully specced standalone transport in §5.22. As a community overlay it provides a virtual Ethernet layer. Refer to §5.22 for the complete specification. Solver entry: `TransportOverlay::ZeroTier`.


#### 5.29.5 Solver Integration for Community Overlays

```rust
TransportOverlay additions (extending §5.2):
    Anonet,
    DN42,
    CjdnsHyperboria,
    ZeroTier { network_id: String },
}
```

Community overlay transports score in the solver:

| Overlay | Anonymization | Latency penalty | Availability |
|---------|--------------|-----------------|--------------|
| Anonet | 0.2 | Low | Low (small community) |
| dn42 | 0.1 | Low | Medium |
| cjdns | 0.3 | Low | Medium |
| ZeroTier | 0.1 | Very low | High |

These overlays are used when: the user is already connected to them, ThreatContext warrants avoiding clearnet, or the target service is only reachable via that overlay. They are never the default transport — clearnet WireGuard remains the default for connected peers.



---

### 5.30 Overlay Transport Client Architecture

Mesh Infinity is a **first-class client** for each overlay transport it supports (Tailscale, ZeroTier). It does not depend on external daemons or pre-existing installations of these services. This section specifies the architectural principles that apply across all overlay clients.

#### 5.30.1 Single VPN Slot (Mobile)

iOS and Android permit exactly one active VPN extension per device. A separate Tailscale app and a separate Mesh Infinity app cannot be active simultaneously. Mesh Infinity's VPN extension handles all virtual networking in a single slot:

- Mesh routing (always active when mesh is on)
- Tailscale overlay (when Tailscale transport is configured and authenticated)
- ZeroTier overlay (when ZeroTier transport is configured and authenticated)
- Infinet (§13.14, when joined to an Infinet)

All of these are multiplexed through a single TUN interface. The user does not run a separate Tailscale or ZeroTier app on mobile.

On desktop, Mesh Infinity manages its own virtual interfaces independently. If the user also has a system Tailscale daemon running, Mesh Infinity uses a separate interface and does not conflict with it. The user may prefer to run only Mesh Infinity and skip installing separate overlay apps.

#### 5.30.2 Credential Storage

Authentication credentials for all overlay clients (Tailscale auth keys, ZeroTier API keys) are stored encrypted in the vault (§3.5) under a dedicated key domain. They are never written to plaintext storage, never logged, and zeroized on vault wipe (§3.5.4).

#### 5.30.3 ACL Policy — Read, Not Write

Mesh Infinity reads ACL/flow policy from overlay controllers to understand what traffic is permitted and display it in the management UI. It does not write, modify, or suggest changes to ACL policy. The user's network configuration is theirs. If their ACL rules block certain traffic, that traffic is blocked — Mesh Infinity surfaces this as a diagnostic event (§22.56) and falls back to other transports.

**Exception:** Mesh Infinity can optionally request that exit node capability be advertised for this device (if the user enables it as an exit node in Settings > VPN > Act as exit node). This requires the tailnet ACL to permit it — Mesh Infinity will surface the required ACL change as a copyable snippet if the tailnet policy blocks it. The user applies it manually.

#### 5.30.4 Relay Infrastructure Preference

For NAT traversal, both Tailscale and ZeroTier operate relay infrastructure (DERP servers for Tailscale; PLANET/MOON servers for ZeroTier). When direct peer connections fail, traffic falls back to these servers.

Mesh Infinity prefers its own mesh relay nodes (§6.11) over vendor relay infrastructure:

- **Mesh relay:** Selected by trust level, runs on nodes the user chose to trust. Traffic is encrypted and the relay node cannot inspect it.
- **Vendor relay:** Run by Tailscale Inc. or ZeroTier Inc. (or a self-hosted instance). Visibility depends on whether the traffic is separately encrypted by the mesh layer (it is — WireGuard-in-WireGuard).

When vendor relay is active, an indicator is shown (§5.23.3 / §5.22.3). When mesh relay is active, a separate indicator is shown. The preference is configurable per transport.

#### 5.30.5 Traffic Indicators Reference

A consistent indicator language applies across all overlay and exit node scenarios:

| Scenario | Indicator color | Indicator text | Tap → |
|---------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| Mesh routing (normal) | None / green | "Mesh" | Network screen |
| Tailscale exit node for clearnet | Amber | "Tailscale Exit: [node]" | Info panel (§5.23.4) |
| ZeroTier relay (vendor) active | Amber | "ZeroTier Relay" | Info panel |
| Tailscale DERP relay active | Amber | "Tailscale Relay" | Info panel |
| Mesh exit node for clearnet | Green | "Mesh Exit: [node]" | Network screen |
| Mesh relay | None (normal operation) | — | — |
| LoSec mode active | Amber | "LoSec" | LoSec info (§6.9) |
| Lockdown / Critical threat | Red | "Lockdown" | Threat context |

**Information panel content for vendor relay / exit indicators** (all follow the same structure):

> **[Indicator name]**
>
> [One sentence: what this means for the user's traffic]
>
> [Two to four bullet points: what entity can observe what]
>
> [One sentence: what is NOT affected / what remains protected]

Plain language, no jargon. Security warnings are informative, not alarming. The user made an intentional choice to use these transports — the indicators confirm it and explain the tradeoffs; they do not suggest the user should stop.

---

### 5.31 KCP Reliability Sublayer

Mesh Infinity's transport is built on WireGuard, which runs over UDP. UDP provides no delivery guarantees — packets can be lost, reordered, or silently dropped at any hop. For applications that need reliable delivery, this would normally mean falling back to TCP tunneling. Instead, Mesh Infinity uses **KCP** as a uniform reliability sublayer applied to every WireGuard session across the mesh.

**Stack position:**
```
Application / mesh protocol
        ↓
    KCP (reliability, ordering, retransmission)
        ↓
  WireGuard (encryption, peer authentication)
        ↓
  Obfuscation layer (§5.26, if active)
        ↓
      UDP / network
```

KCP is transparent to everything above it. From the perspective of routing, S&F, the tunnel protocol (§12.3), or any application, KCP simply provides a reliable ordered byte stream. Nothing above KCP needs to know it exists.

#### 5.30.1 Why KCP Over TCP-in-WireGuard
