Progress calculated from amounts operators report manually or via payment integration. Over-donations carry forward. **Privacy:** no donation tracking metadata passes through the mesh — operators self-report their received totals.

**UI note:** The ASCII mockup above is a layout reference only. The actual visual design — progress bar styling, colour system, button appearance — is specified in §20 (UI/UX) during the §20 donation panel pass.

##### 10.2.16.5 Mesh Infinity Project Donation

Always displayed at the bottom of any donation panel. Cannot be suppressed. One line with a link. Mesh Infinity takes no cut of any other donations.

#### 10.2.17 Scheduled Events

Garden events are a first-class system — not a bot wrapper or a pinned message. Events are managed by the Garden service, rendered in a dedicated events tab, and integrated with stage/voice channels.

```rust
GardenEvent {
    event_id:       [u8; 16],
    title:          String,
    description:    Option<String>,
    created_by:     [u8; 32],
    starts_at:      u64,
    ends_at:        Option<u64>,
    location:       EventLocation,
    recurrence:     Option<RecurrenceRule>,
    rsvp_enabled:   bool,
    rsvp_counts:    EventRsvpCounts,     // server-visible aggregate; individual RSVPs encrypted
    cover_image:    Option<[u8; 32]>,   // content hash
    status:         EventStatus,
}

EventLocation {
    VoiceChannel([u8; 32]),     // links to a specific voice/stage channel
    ExternalUrl(String),        // clearnet link
    TextDescription(String),    // freeform location text
    InPerson(String),           // physical location — displayed as text only
}

RecurrenceRule {
    frequency:  RecurrenceFreq,
    interval:   u32,                // every N periods
    days_of_week: Option<Vec<u8>>, // for weekly: which days (0=Mon..6=Sun)
    end_condition: RecurrenceEnd,
}

RecurrenceFreq { Daily, Weekly, Monthly }

RecurrenceEnd {
    Never,
    AfterCount(u32),
    UntilDate(u64),
}

EventStatus { Scheduled, Active, Cancelled, Ended }

EventRsvpCounts { going: u32, maybe: u32, not_going: u32 }
```

**RSVP:** When `rsvp_enabled: true`, members can respond Going/Maybe/Not Going. Individual RSVP responses are encrypted (server sees only aggregate counts). RSVP status visible to the member themselves and to admins.

**Reminders:** Members who RSVP'd Going or Maybe receive notifications: 1 hour before, 10 minutes before, and at event start. Members who haven't RSVP'd receive a single reminder 1 hour before. Reminder timing is configurable per Garden admin.

**Recurring events:** Recurring events spawn individual event instances. Each instance is a separate `GardenEvent` record linked by a shared `series_id`. Cancelling an individual instance does not affect the series. Editing an instance prompts: "edit this event only" or "edit all future events."

**Stage channel integration:** When `location` is `VoiceChannel` pointing to a Stage channel, the event appears in the stage channel's header. At `starts_at`, the stage channel is automatically promoted to "event active" state — members see a join prompt. The event ends automatically at `ends_at` or when an admin marks it ended.

**Events tab:** Every Garden has a dedicated Events tab in the sidebar showing upcoming events in chronological order. Past events are archived and browsable. Events can be filtered by RSVP status.

#### 10.2.18 Call Recording

Call recording in Garden is configured at the zone level — admins define recording policies for channel groups.

```rust
GardenCallRecordingPolicy {
    zone:           RecordingZone,
    mode:           RecordingMode,
    storage_target: RecordingStorage,
    retention_days: Option<u32>,     // None = keep until manually deleted
}

RecordingZone {
    AllChannels,
    ChannelList(Vec<[u8; 32]>),
    CategoryList(Vec<[u8; 32]>),
}

RecordingMode {
    Disabled,
    ConsentRequired,    // all participants must consent before recording starts
    AutoRecord,         // recording starts automatically; members are notified on join
                        // (only valid on channels where all members have accepted Garden terms
                        //  that disclose auto-recording — e.g., moderation/support channels)
    AdminOnly,          // only admins with RecordCalls permission can initiate recording
}

RecordingStorage {
    LocalToInitiator,   // recording saved to the initiating member's device only
    GardenHosted,       // recording encrypted and stored on Garden cluster (member-accessible)
    Disabled,           // recording not stored (live transcription use cases)
}
```

**Consent flow for `ConsentRequired`:** When a member attempts to start recording, all other participants receive a consent prompt. Recording cannot start until all active participants accept. Members who join mid-recording also receive the consent prompt — they can join without consenting but cannot hear the audio until consent is resolved (or admin policy permits joining without consent in the recording zone).

**`AutoRecord` disclosure requirement:** Gardens using `AutoRecord` must disclose this in their rules acknowledgement (§10.2.14) and in the channel topic. Members who join a channel with auto-recording active see a persistent indicator: 🔴 Recording.

**Persistent indicator:** Any active recording displays a persistent 🔴 Recording badge in the voice channel UI, visible to all participants and all members browsing the channel list.

#### 10.2.19 Garden Backup, Export, and Migration

Admins can export the full Garden state as an encrypted archive. The export format is also the migration format — moving a Garden to new hosting is an export followed by an import on the new cluster.

```rust
GardenExport {
    format_version:  u32,
    exported_at:     u64,
    garden_id:       [u8; 32],
    export_sig:      [u8; 64],  // signed by an admin's mesh key — verifiable on import
    includes:        ExportScope,
    encryption:      ExportEncryption,
}

ExportScope {
    channels:    bool,
    messages:    bool,   // full message history per HistoryAccess policy
    members:     bool,   // member list and role assignments
    roles:       bool,
    settings:    bool,
    audit_log:   bool,
    emoji:       bool,
    events:      bool,
    webhooks:    bool,
}

ExportEncryption {
    Passphrase(String),         // Argon2id-derived key — suitable for offline archival
    GardenKey,                  // encrypted to current Garden community key
                                // any member with the community key can decrypt
    AdminKeyOnly([u8; 32]),     // encrypted to a specific admin's mesh key
}
```

**Export process:** The export is generated entirely server-side within the Garden's encrypted context. The admin selects scope and encryption mode. The resulting archive is a single encrypted file available for download via the admin panel. No data is transmitted to any third party during export.

**Import and verification:** On import, the receiving cluster verifies `export_sig` against the exporting admin's known public key. If verification passes, the cluster reconstructs channels, roles, message history, and member state from the archive. Members are not automatically notified of a migration — the DNS change (§17.11.2) handles transparent reconnection.

**Live migration:** For cluster migration, export and import happen with the Garden community key present at both source and destination. The source cluster continues serving until the import is confirmed complete. Then the source DNS record is updated to point to the new cluster. Old cluster can be decommissioned after the TTL expires. Members reconnect automatically.

**Backup schedule:** Admins can configure automated periodic exports (daily, weekly, monthly) with a retention count. Automated exports use `GardenKey` encryption by default and are stored on the hosting node. They are accessible from the admin panel for download.


### 10.3 Relay

Relay is a third communication type: **encrypted dead-drop boxes** for people who know their contents. Relay nodes are blind storage points that hold encrypted payloads without being able to read them. The relay has no concept of sender, recipient, or content — only that a payload exists and when it should expire.

#### 10.3.0 Overview

- **§10.3.1 Relay Protocol** — deposit and retrieval structs, expiry, size padding, retrieval key
- **§10.3.2 Relay Node Discovery** — stealth by design; optional public DNS record; no central registry
- **§10.3.3 Relay Use Cases** — dead drops, delayed delivery, anonymous tips, contact initiation
- **§10.3.4 Relay and the Memory Security Model** — management keys in memory only

**Design intent:** Relay fills the gap between Chat (requires trust relationship) and Garden (requires community membership). It enables:
- Secure dead drops between parties who don't want a persistent relationship
- Pre-positioned messages for someone to collect when they choose
- Asynchronous communication where neither party needs to be online simultaneously or know the other's mesh address
- Operational security patterns where contact initiation needs to be deniable

**Threat model:** The relay node learns: that a payload exists, its size class, and when it expires. It can also observe that a `RelayRetrieve` query was made for a given `relay_id` — but it does not learn whether the gate was passed (if set), who queried, or what the content was. The relay does not record retrieval events separately from receipt of the query itself. Traffic analysis at the network layer can potentially correlate deposit and retrieval queries — use over the native mixnet tier for stronger anonymity.


#### 10.3.1 Relay Protocol

```rust
RelayDeposit {
    relay_id:            [u8; 32],   // random 256-bit ID; becomes the retrieval address.
                                      // Collision probability ~10^-57 at 2^32 simultaneous deposits.
    payload:             Vec<u8>,    // encrypted blob; relay cannot read; padded to size class
    expiry:              u64,        // unix timestamp; relay deletes after this
    deposit_pubkey:      [u8; 32],   // Ed25519 public key of the depositor for this deposit only.
                                      // Mesh users: one of their mask keys.
                                      // Anonymous/clearnet depositors: a throwaway keypair generated
                                      // at deposit time; private key discarded after signing.
                                      // The relay stores this to verify expiry_sig.
    expiry_sig:          [u8; 64],   // Ed25519 signature over (relay_id || expiry) using deposit_pubkey.
                                      // Relay verifies on deposit. Cannot extend expiry without
                                      // the private key — which for throwaway keys is already gone.
    size_class:          u8,         // which padding bucket this deposit occupies
    retrieval_gate:      Option<RetrievalGate>,
}

RetrievalGate {
    // HMAC-SHA256(secret_key, relay_id) stored by relay.
    // Retriever sends the same HMAC; relay compares.
    // The secret_key never reaches the relay — only the HMAC.
    // This gates access without proving retrieval happened.
    hmac: [u8; 32],
}

RelayRetrieve {
    relay_id:     [u8; 32],
    gate_proof:   Option<[u8; 32]>,  // HMAC-SHA256(secret_key, relay_id) if gate set
                                      // relay checks gate_proof == stored hmac
                                      // relay learns nothing about secret_key
}
```

**Protocol agnosticism:** The relay wire protocol is not mandated. A relay node can expose its service over any transport it chooses:
- **Mesh protocol** (default): standard mesh messaging, relay_id as destination address
- **HTTPS/REST** (optional): clearnet-accessible endpoint; relay_id in URL path; payload in body
- **Raw TCP** (optional): for high-throughput scenarios

The relay is infrastructure. Depositors and retrievers use whatever protocol the relay advertises. Encryption and decryption are entirely the depositor's responsibility — the relay handles only authenticated bytes. Symmetric PSKs, GPG, age, OpenSSL — any scheme works. The relay has no opinion on the encryption used.

**Relay ID:** 256-bit random, generated by the depositor. Shared out-of-band. Collision probability negligible.

**Retrieval gate (HMAC):** Optional. Depositor computes `HMAC-SHA256(secret_key, relay_id)` and includes it in `RetrievalGate`. Relay stores the HMAC. Retriever computes the same HMAC and sends it in `gate_proof`. Relay compares and returns payload if they match. The `secret_key` never leaves the depositor/retriever side — the relay sees only the HMAC and cannot derive the key. This gates access without creating proof-of-retrieval — the relay cannot tell if the correct party retrieved or if someone guessed (they can't — 256-bit HMAC is unguessable).

**Payload encryption:** Entirely depositor-managed. The relay holds opaque bytes. The decryption key is shared out-of-band alongside the `relay_id` and optional `secret_key` for the gate. Any symmetric or asymmetric encryption scheme is valid.

**Relay listen mechanism:** A relay node enabling the relay service opens:
- One mesh service endpoint (always) — receives `RelayDeposit` and `RelayRetrieve` messages over the standard mesh protocol
- Optionally an HTTPS endpoint (port configurable, default 443) — for clearnet depositors/retrievers who cannot use the mesh directly. The HTTPS endpoint uses the same request/response semantics as the mesh protocol, with JSON or binary encoding.

The relay node operator configures which endpoints are enabled via the node management interface.

**Size padding protocol:** All payloads are padded to the next standard size class before deposit:

| Class | Padded size |
|-------|------------|
| 0 | 1 KB |
| 1 | 4 KB |
| 2 | 16 KB |
| 3 | 64 KB |
| 4 | 256 KB |
| 5 | 1 MB |
| 6 | 4 MB |
| 7 | 16 MB |

Padding is random bytes appended to the plaintext before encryption — indistinguishable from content. The `size_class` field tells the relay which bucket this deposit occupies for storage accounting. On retrieval, the recipient strips padding after decryption using a length prefix embedded in the plaintext.

**Expiry:** Mandatory. The depositor specifies `expiry` and signs `(relay_id || expiry)` with `deposit_pubkey`. Mesh users sign with one of their mask keys. Anonymous and clearnet depositors generate a throwaway Ed25519 keypair at deposit time, sign the expiry, then discard the private key — the relay holds only the public key and the signature, which is sufficient to verify but not to forge a new one. The relay cannot extend expiry without the private key. Minimum: 1 hour. Maximum: 30 days. After expiry the payload is deleted unconditionally. The relay does not track or report whether the payload was retrieved.

**Relay node operation:** Any server-mode mesh node can offer relay service by enabling the relay plugin. Relay nodes are not listed in the public network map by default — discovery is opt-in per §10.3.2. Depositors select relay nodes based on availability, trust level, geographic distribution (for redundancy), and capacity.

**Multi-relay redundancy:** A depositor can deposit the same payload (same `relay_id`, same ciphertext) to multiple relay nodes simultaneously with different node addresses. The first successful retrieval from any node is sufficient. Depositors selecting multiple nodes for redundancy should choose nodes operated by different parties via different discovery paths to avoid correlated failure or compromise.

**Active communication requirement:** Relay is not browsable. There is no index, no search, no enumeration. A relay node confirms only "yes, this relay_id exists" or "no, it doesn't" — it cannot enumerate its contents to any observer, including its own operator.


#### 10.3.2 Relay Node Discovery

**Relay nodes are stealth by design.** There is no central registry of relay nodes. There is no enumerable index. A relay node that doesn't want to be found cannot be found by network scanning — it simply appears as a mesh node running unspecified services.

**Optional public DNS record:** A relay node operator who wants to be discoverable can publish a record in the public mesh DNS (§17.11.2, `*.public.meshinfinity`). This is entirely opt-in. The record contains the relay node's mesh address and nothing else — it does not expose capacity, current load, or any other metadata. Operators who publish a DNS record accept that their relay is publicly associated with their mesh identity.

**How depositors find relay nodes:**
- **Out-of-band sharing** — a relay operator tells specific people their relay's mesh address directly (verbally, via QR, via another channel). This is the primary and most secure discovery path.
- **Public DNS lookup** — depositors can search `*.public.meshinfinity` for nodes advertising relay service. This sacrifices some anonymity for convenience — both the depositor and the relay node's address become linkable.
- **Trusted contacts** — a trusted contact who runs a relay can share their relay address through a trusted channel. The address is then stored locally as a known relay.

**Multi-relay redundancy:** Depositors selecting multiple relay nodes for redundancy (§10.3.1) should prefer nodes discovered via different paths and operated by different parties to avoid correlated failure or correlated compromise.

#### 10.3.3 Relay Use Cases

**Dead drop:** Alice deposits an encrypted message at a relay. She shares the relay_id and decryption key with Bob via a separate channel (voice call, QR code, a physical note). Bob retrieves and decrypts at any time before expiry. Alice and Bob never need to be online simultaneously. The relay never knows Alice sent Bob something.

**Delayed delivery:** Alice deposits a message to be retrieved by Bob, but Bob doesn't know about it yet. Alice can disclose the relay coordinates later — or not at all.

**Anonymous tip:** A source deposits sensitive information. A recipient publishes their relay retrieval key and a relay address as a standing "dead drop" address. Anyone can deposit; the recipient periodically checks. The relay node cannot tell the recipient apart from any other retriever.

**Operational security contact initiation:** A party who wants to initiate contact without revealing their mesh address can deposit an introduction at a publicly known relay address. The introduction contains their temporary contact key. The recipient retrieves, decrypts, and can choose to respond or not.

#### 10.3.4 Relay and the Memory Security Model

Relay nodes hold encrypted payloads in storage. Like the Garden community key, relay management keys (used to accept deposits and authorize deletions) are held in memory only, never on disk in plaintext. A seized relay node contains opaque encrypted blobs with no recoverable content.

### 10.4 Proximity Share

Proximity Share is rapid local file and content distribution over local mesh transports — the AirDrop equivalent. It requires no internet connectivity, no pre-existing trust relationship, and no account. It is designed for a specific scenario: a group of physically co-located people who need to share content quickly.

**Target use cases:**
- Distributing a strategic document among a locally present group
- Sharing media with people nearby at an event
- Moving files between personal devices without cloud intermediary
- Emergency information distribution in network-outage scenarios

#### 10.4.0 Overview

- **§10.4.1 Transport Layer** — BLE discovery + WiFi Direct transfer; local mesh only
- **§10.4.2 Protocol** — offer/accept/transfer flow; encryption without prior pairing
- **§10.4.3 Security Model** — ephemeral keys; malware mitigation; consent at every step
- **§10.4.4 Receive Settings** — who can send to you; visibility controls

#### 10.4.1 Transport Layer

Proximity Share uses the local mesh transports exclusively:

- **Discovery:** BLE advertisement (§5.6) with a `ProximityShareAdvertisement` payload — a temporary service token indicating the node is accepting Proximity Share offers
- **Transfer:** WiFi Direct (§5.5) as the primary high-bandwidth transport; BLE GATT as fallback for small payloads (<64 KB) when WiFi Direct is unavailable
- **Range:** Effective range ~10–50m (WiFi Direct); ~10m (BLE only)
- **No internet required:** operates entirely on local transports even when the global mesh is unreachable

#### 10.4.2 Protocol

```rust
ProximityOffer {
    offer_id:        [u8; 16],      // random; identifies this transfer session
    sender_name:     String,        // display name (not identity key — can be anything)
    sender_token:    [u8; 32],      // ephemeral X25519 public key for this offer only
    receiver_nonce:  [u8; 16],      // random nonce set by sender; used in gate HMAC derivation
                                    // gate_code_hash = HMAC-SHA256(code, receiver_nonce)
                                    // gate_code_proof = HMAC-SHA256(code, receiver_nonce)
                                    // receiver verifies gate_code_proof == gate_code_hash
    files:           Vec<FileOffer>, // max 64 files per offer
    expires_at:      u64,           // offer expires if not accepted (default: 60 seconds)
    gate_code_hash:  Option<[u8; 32]>, // HMAC-SHA256(4-digit code, receiver_nonce) if EveryoneWithCode
}

FileOffer {
    file_id:     [u8; 16],
    name:        String,
    size:        u64,               // exact plaintext size (before encryption/padding)
    mime_type:   String,
    sha256:      [u8; 32],          // hash of plaintext — verified after decrypt
    thumbnail:   Option<Vec<u8>>,   // max 8 KB inline preview; sandboxed render only
}

ProximityAccept {
    offer_id:          [u8; 16],
    accepted_files:    Vec<[u8; 16]>,  // subset of offered files
    receiver_token:    [u8; 32],       // ephemeral X25519 public key
    gate_code_proof:   Option<[u8; 32]>, // HMAC-SHA256(code, receiver_nonce) — proves code knowledge
    identity_claim:    Option<ProximityIdentityClaim>, // optional mesh identity assertion (§10.4.3)
}

// Chunks are sent sequentially, not as a single Vec.
// Each ProximityChunk is an independent encrypted+authenticated unit.
ProximityChunk {
    offer_id:    [u8; 16],
    file_id:     [u8; 16],
    chunk_index: u32,
    total_chunks: u32,
    data:        Vec<u8>,   // max 64 KB encrypted chunk
    is_last:     bool,
}

ProximityChunkAck {
    offer_id:    [u8; 16],
    file_id:     [u8; 16],
    chunk_index: u32,
    ok:          bool,       // false = request retransmit
}
```

**Ephemeral key exchange:** Sender includes `sender_token` (ephemeral X25519 public key) in the offer. Receiver generates its own ephemeral keypair, returns `receiver_token` in the accept. Both compute `X25519(their_priv, other_pub)` to derive a shared session key. Files are encrypted with ChaCha20-Poly1305 under this key. Neither ephemeral key persists after the transfer completes.

**EveryoneWithCode gate:** When the receiver's policy is `EveryoneWithCode`, a 4-digit code is displayed on their screen. The receiver computes `gate_code_hash = HMAC-SHA256(code, receiver_nonce)` and includes it in the offer broadcast. The sender enters the code, computes `gate_code_proof = HMAC-SHA256(code, receiver_nonce)`, and includes it in `ProximityAccept`. The receiver verifies equality. The code never travels over the air in plaintext — only the HMAC.

**Sequential chunked transfer:** Chunks are sent one at a time with acknowledgements (`ProximityChunkAck`). On `ok: false`, the sender retransmits that chunk. This avoids loading entire files into memory simultaneously and handles lossy local transport conditions. Maximum 64 KB per chunk. Large files are streamed.

**File count limit:** Maximum 64 files per offer. If a sender has more, they create multiple offers. This prevents resource exhaustion attacks via oversized offer payloads.

**Hash verification:** After all chunks for a file are received and decrypted, the receiver assembles and verifies SHA-256 against `FileOffer.sha256`. Mismatch → file discarded, sender notified via `ProximityChunkAck { ok: false }` on the final chunk.

**iOS BLE behavior:** iOS suspends BLE advertisement when the app moves to background or the screen locks. This is an OS constraint that cannot be worked around. The spec accommodates this:
- Proximity Share requires the app to be in the foreground on iOS — this is disclosed in the UI
- An active transfer that is interrupted by screen lock resumes if the screen is unlocked within the transfer timeout (default: 30 seconds)
- Transfers interrupted beyond the timeout must be restarted from the beginning
- Android and desktop do not have this limitation

#### 10.4.3 Security Model

**Consent at every step:**
1. Sender chooses to broadcast an offer (explicit action — not ambient)
2. Receiver sees the offer with: sender display name, file names, file sizes, MIME types, and an optional thumbnail. Receiver explicitly accepts or declines each file individually.
3. No file write occurs without explicit acceptance.
4. After transfer, the file lands in a quarantine folder, not the system media library. The user must explicitly move it.

**Malware mitigation:**
- Files are not auto-opened or auto-executed under any circumstances
- Thumbnails in the offer are rendered in a sandboxed context — they cannot execute code
- MIME type is advisory only — the receiver's OS determines actual file type after receipt
- The quarantine step gives the user a chance to inspect before integrating into their file system
- If the platform supports it (Android, macOS, Linux with appropriate sandboxing), the quarantine folder has no execute permission

**What Proximity Share cannot prevent:**
- A malicious sender offering a malicious file — the recipient must exercise judgement
- Social engineering ("this is the briefing document, please accept")
- Screen recording or secondary capture of viewed content

These limitations are honestly disclosed in the UI accept prompt: *"Only accept files from people you trust. Files are not scanned for malware."*

**Identity vs display name:** The `sender_name` in the offer is self-declared and unverified. A nearby adversary could claim any name. For high-sensitivity scenarios, verify the sender's identity through a separate channel (voice, safety number comparison) before accepting. If both parties are Mesh Infinity users with a trust relationship, the offer can optionally include a signed identity assertion linking the ephemeral token to a known peer ID.

**Optional mesh identity linking:**
```rust
ProximityIdentityClaim {
    peer_id:    [u8; 32],       // the sender's mesh peer ID
    offer_id:   [u8; 16],       // binds this claim to a specific offer
    signature:  [u8; 64],       // signed by peer_id's private key
}
```
If included, the receiver can verify the signature against the sender's known public key (if they have a trust relationship). Unverified offers show a grey "unknown sender" indicator; verified offers show the trust badge.

#### 10.4.4 Receive Settings

Users control who can send them Proximity Share offers:

```rust
ProximityReceivePolicy {
    Disabled,           // not discoverable; no offers received
    TrustedOnly,        // only offers from peers with Level 3+ trust are displayed
                        // requires sender to include a valid ProximityIdentityClaim (§10.4.3)
                        // anonymous senders (no identity claim) are silently dropped
    Everyone,           // visible to any nearby Mesh Infinity node
    EveryoneWithCode,   // visible only to nodes that know a 4-digit confirmation code
                        // displayed on the receiver's screen (reduces ambient discovery)
}
```

Default policy: `EveryoneWithCode` — the receiver shows a 4-digit code on screen; senders must enter it to send. This prevents ambient file-bombing in crowded spaces (conferences, public transit) while keeping the flow simple for legitimate nearby transfers.

The BLE advertisement only broadcasts when Proximity Share is actively open in the app — it is not a background service. Closing the screen stops discovery immediately.

---

## 11. File Sharing

File sharing in Mesh Infinity consists of two separate systems: **direct transfer** (live, peer-to-peer) and **distributed storage** (async, content-addressed, Freenet-style). These systems share the same encryption model and content addressing scheme but are distinct delivery mechanisms. Publishing to one does not imply publishing to the other.

### 11.0 Overview

- **§11.1 Direct File Transfer** — live P2P transfer; formal offer/accept/resume/complete protocol; resumable; session-key encrypted
- **§11.2 Distributed Object Storage** — content-addressed encrypted blobs; 5 security levels; manifest format; parallel fetch; stickiness metric; stop-storing signal
- **§11.3 Public File Hosting** — mesh HTTP serving; content-addressed advertising; hash-addressable cross-system
- **§11.4 Private File Sharing** — direct transfer to peers/groups; shared group repositories; distributed storage at Level 2–4

### 11.1 Direct File Transfer

Direct file transfers are live, peer-to-peer transfers where both parties are online simultaneously. No storage nodes are involved. Session key negotiation follows §7.3.

```rust
FileTransferOffer {
    file_id:     [u8; 32],   // SHA-256 of plaintext file — content address and dedup key.
                              // NOTE: two concurrent transfers of the same file produce the
                              // same file_id. The session key (§7.3) disambiguates sessions —
                              // (file_id, session_key) is the unique transfer identifier.
    name:        String,
    size:        u64,        // exact plaintext size in bytes
    mime_type:   String,
    chunk_size:  u32,        // proposed chunk size in bytes; default 65536 (64 KB).
                              // Recipient accepts or rejects the whole offer — no counter-proposal.
                              // Sender should choose based on observed link quality.
    chunk_count: u32,        // ceil(size / chunk_size)
}

FileTransferAccept {
    file_id:    [u8; 32],
}

FileTransferChunk {
    file_id:     [u8; 32],
    chunk_index: u32,
    data:        Vec<u8>,    // encrypted with session key; max chunk_size bytes
}

FileTransferChunkAck {
    file_id:     [u8; 32],
    chunk_index: u32,
    ok:          bool,       // false = corrupted or hash mismatch; sender retransmits this chunk
}

FileTransferResume {
    file_id:         [u8; 32],
    received_chunks: Vec<u8>,  // packed bitfield; ceil(chunk_count / 8) bytes;
                                // bit N set = chunk N already received and verified
}

FileTransferComplete {
    file_id: [u8; 32],
    ok:      bool,          // false = final SHA-256 mismatch; sender may retransmit
}
```

**Protocol:**

1. **Initiation:** Sender transmits `FileTransferOffer` as a Chat message.
2. **Acceptance:** Recipient replies with `FileTransferAccept`, triggering session key negotiation (§7.3). If the recipient cannot accept the proposed `chunk_size` (e.g. memory constraints), they decline with no `FileTransferAccept` — the sender may re-offer with a smaller chunk size.
3. **Transfer:** Sender transmits `FileTransferChunk` messages sequentially within a sliding window of 64 outstanding chunks. For each chunk received, the recipient sends `FileTransferChunkAck { ok: true }`. On `ok: false`, the sender immediately retransmits that chunk before advancing the window. The window bounds the receiver's reassembly buffer to at most `64 × chunk_size` bytes in memory simultaneously.
4. **Completion:** Recipient reassembles and verifies SHA-256 against `file_id`. Sends `FileTransferComplete { ok: true }` on success, `ok: false` on mismatch (sender may retransmit or abort).
5. **Resumption:** On reconnect after interruption, recipient sends `FileTransferResume` with a bitfield of already-received-and-verified chunks. Sender retransmits only missing chunks, resuming the sliding window from the first gap.

A file shared via direct transfer does not exist in distributed storage unless explicitly published there as a separate action.

### 11.2 Distributed Object Storage

Nodes volunteer storage capacity. Content is stored as encrypted blobs, content-addressed by SHA-256 hash. Storage nodes are completely blind — they hold opaque encrypted data with no readable metadata (at security levels 2–4). This follows the Mega.nz model: encrypt client-side, distribute the blob, share the key separately.

#### 11.2.0 Overview

- **§11.2.1 Storage Types** — distributed, sticky, scoped; announcement protocol; commitment model
- **§11.2.2 Content Security Levels** — 5 levels from public to direct; encryption and revocability properties
- **§11.2.3 Chunking Model** — constant chunk size per file; power-of-two; manifest format; `ContentSecurityLevel` enum
- **§11.2.4 Parallel Fetching and Resumable Transfers** — simultaneous multi-node fetch; `FetchState` tracking
- **§11.2.5 Metadata Security Model** — metadata follows content security level; thumbnails
- **§11.2.6 Stickiness Metric** — computed reversibility score; shown before publish
- **§11.2.7 Stop-Storing Signal** — wire format; signed by publisher; anonymous publish tradeoff

#### 11.2.1 Storage Types

- **Distributed object storage** — Freenet-style, probabilistic availability. Content is replicated across volunteering nodes. Availability is proportional to demand and node participation.
- **Sticky storage** — pinned to specific nodes, reliable availability. The node operator commits to hosting the content for a defined period.
- **Scoped distributed storage** — restricted to group or LAN. Content is replicated only within the group's member nodes or the LAN's participating nodes.

**Storage node announcement protocol:** When a node stores a chunk, it announces to its immediate peers that it holds that content hash. This announcement propagates via the existing routing gossip (§6.2) scoped to the appropriate level — global for distributed, group-channel gossip for scoped.

```rust
StorageAnnouncement {
    content_hash:  [u8; 32],    // SHA-256 of the encrypted chunk ciphertext
    node_id:       [u8; 32],    // announcing node's mesh peer ID
    expires_at:    u64,         // when this node's storage commitment expires
    scope:         StorageScope,
    signature:     [u8; 64],    // Ed25519 over (content_hash || node_id || expires_at)
}

StorageScope {
    Global,                     // any node may fetch
    Group([u8; 32]),            // only members of this group_id may fetch
    LAN,                        // only nodes on the same local network segment
}
```

Fetchers discover candidate nodes by collecting `StorageAnnouncement` records for the manifest's chunk hashes. The routing layer indexes these by content hash. Multiple nodes announcing the same hash means parallel fetch is available.

**Sticky storage commitment:** A publisher pins a file to a specific node by sending a signed `StorageCommitment` request to that node. The node operator accepts or rejects. Accepted commitments are stored and the node commits bandwidth and storage for the duration.

```rust
StorageCommitment {
    manifest_hash:   [u8; 32],
    chunk_hashes:    Vec<[u8; 32]>,
    committed_until: u64,           // unix timestamp; hard deadline
    publisher_sig:   [u8; 64],      // Ed25519 over (manifest_hash || committed_until)
}
```

The node stores the commitment and begins announcing each chunk hash. If a node goes offline before `committed_until`, it is recorded in the local trust graph and affects the solver's willingness to use that node for future commitments.

**Scoped storage enforcement:** For `StorageScope::Group`, the storing node verifies that fetch requests come from peers authenticated as members of the specified group via the group's trust channel. Non-members receive a rejection with no content disclosed — the node does not confirm or deny that the chunk exists.

#### 11.2.2 Content Security Levels

| Level | Name | Key distribution | Revocability |
|-------|------|-----------------|--------------|
| 0 | Public | Unencrypted; hash published in indexes | Effectively irrevocable |
| 1 | Unlisted | Unencrypted; hash NOT published; shared out-of-band | Practically irrevocable |
| 2 | Trust-gated | Key via trusted channel | Best-effort via stop-storing signal |
| 3 | Group-scoped | Key via group key mechanism | Group can issue stop-storing |
| 4 | Direct | Key via direct trusted-channel | Reliable via stop-storing signal |

**Level 0 (Public):** Content and metadata are unencrypted. The content hash is published in indexes and the network map. Anyone can fetch and read. Effectively irrevocable once distributed.

**Level 1 (Unlisted):** Content and metadata are unencrypted — same as Level 0. The distinction is addressing only: the content address is NOT published in any index. Anyone with the address can fetch and read freely. Security is purely address obscurity, not encryption. Practically irrevocable because any node that has fetched the content can redistribute it.

**Level 2 (Trust-gated):** Content and metadata are encrypted with a file key. The file key is distributed through trusted channels to specific peers. Storage nodes hold only encrypted blobs. Best-effort revocability via stop-storing signal — cooperative nodes honor it, but copies already distributed to trusted peers cannot be recalled.

**Level 3 (Group-scoped):** Content and metadata are encrypted with a key distributed through the group key mechanism. Only group members can decrypt. The group can issue a stop-storing signal. Revocability is better than Level 2 because the key distribution is scoped to the group's Sender Key infrastructure.

**Level 4 (Direct):** Content and metadata are encrypted with a key distributed through a direct trusted-channel message. Only the specific recipient(s) can decrypt. Reliable revocability via stop-storing signal because the distribution is point-to-point and the number of key holders is minimal.

#### 11.2.3 Chunking Model

**Chunk size:** Chosen at publish time and **constant within a file** — every chunk of a given file is the same size, except the final chunk which may be smaller. Chunk size must be a power of two between 128 KB and 16 MB inclusive. Default: 1 MB. The constant-within-file invariant allows any node to verify that it has all chunks for a file (total_size / chunk_size, ceiling) and to request missing chunks by index without a manifest round-trip. Chunk size may vary across different files — it is a per-file property, not a global setting. Larger chunks produce fewer manifest entries and fewer round trips; smaller chunks enable finer-grained parallel fetching and better partial recovery on lossy transports.

**Chunk addressing:** Each chunk is independently content-addressed by SHA-256 of its ciphertext. Chunks are stored as opaque encrypted blobs by storage nodes — nodes cannot distinguish chunks of the same file from chunks of different files.

**File manifest:**

```rust
ContentSecurityLevel {
    Public       = 0,  // unencrypted; hash published in indexes
    Unlisted     = 1,  // unencrypted; hash NOT published; shared out-of-band only
    TrustGated   = 2,  // encrypted; key via trusted channel
    GroupScoped  = 3,  // encrypted; key via group key mechanism
    Direct       = 4,  // encrypted; key via direct trusted-channel message
}

FileManifest {
    version:         u32,
    file_name:       Option<Vec<u8>>,    // plaintext for Level 0-1; encrypted for Level 2-4
    mime_type:       Option<Vec<u8>>,    // same
    total_size:      u64,
    chunk_size:      u32,                // bytes; power of two; constant within this file
    chunk_count:     u32,
    chunks:          Vec<ChunkEntry>,
    thumbnail_hash:  Option<[u8; 32]>,  // SHA-256 of thumbnail blob (separate content-addressed
                                         // blob at same security level); None if no thumbnail
    file_key_hint:   Option<Vec<u8>>,   // for Level 2-4: the file encryption key, itself
                                         // encrypted to the recipient(s) via the appropriate
                                         // key distribution channel. Not present in the manifest
                                         // for Level 0-1 (unencrypted) or when the key is
                                         // distributed entirely out-of-band. Present here as a
                                         // convenience field when the publisher embeds a
                                         // recipient-encrypted key blob alongside the manifest.
    security_level:  ContentSecurityLevel,
    created_at:      u64,
    publisher:       Option<[u8; 32]>,  // peer ID for attribution; None for anonymous publish.
                                         // This field is NOT a signature — it is attribution only.
                                         // Manifest integrity is guaranteed by content addressing:
                                         // the manifest hash the recipient holds is authoritative.
                                         // A storage node cannot tamper with a manifest without
                                         // changing its hash, which the recipient would detect.
}

ChunkEntry {
    index:           u32,
    content_hash:    [u8; 32],   // SHA-256 of encrypted chunk ciphertext
    size:            u32,        // actual size of this chunk (last chunk may be smaller)
}
```

**Level 1 (Unlisted) addressing:** The manifest hash is shared out-of-band by whoever published it — verbally, via QR code, via a trusted channel message, or via a Relay dead-drop (§10.3). This is the same model as Relay `relay_id`: obscurity through address secrecy, not encryption. Any node with the manifest hash can fetch the content freely.

The manifest itself is content-addressed. Sharing a file means sharing the manifest hash (and the file key for Level 2–4 files). Fetching the manifest gives everything needed to reconstruct the file.

#### 11.2.4 Parallel Fetching and Resumable Transfers

Chunks can be fetched from different storage nodes simultaneously. The fetcher discovers candidate nodes from `StorageAnnouncement` records (§11.2.1) indexed by content hash. Chunks are verified against their `content_hash` before assembly. A corrupted or missing chunk triggers re-fetch from an alternate node.

Fetch state is tracked locally per manifest:

```rust
FetchState {
    manifest_hash:    [u8; 32],
    received_chunks:  Vec<u8>,      // packed bitfield; ceil(chunk_count / 8) bytes
    verified_chunks:  Vec<u8>,      // bitfield of chunks that passed content_hash check
    started_at:       u64,
    last_progress:    u64,          // timestamp of last verified chunk; used for timeout
}
```

`FetchState` is persisted in the encrypted vault so a partial fetch survives app restart. On resume, the fetcher loads state, skips verified chunks, and requests only missing ones. No restart from the beginning is required.

#### 11.2.5 Metadata Security Model

Metadata (file name, MIME type, size, thumbnail) follows the same security model as the file content:

- **Level 0 (Public):** Metadata is unencrypted in the manifest. Publicly readable by anyone with the manifest hash.
- **Level 1 (Unlisted):** Metadata is unencrypted — same as Level 0. Security is purely address obscurity. The manifest hash alone is the access control.
- **Level 2–4 (Trust-gated, Group-scoped, Direct):** Metadata is encrypted with the file key. File key distributed through the appropriate channel (trusted channel, group key mechanism, direct trusted-channel message). Storage nodes hold encrypted chunks with no readable metadata.

**Thumbnails:** For image and video files, a thumbnail can be generated and stored as a separate content-addressed blob. The thumbnail follows the same security level as the parent file — a private file's thumbnail is also private. The thumbnail hash is stored in the manifest alongside chunk entries.

#### 11.2.6 Stickiness Metric

The stickiness metric answers "how reversible is this sharing decision?" as a 1–5 integer shown to the user before publish completes.

**Scoring (additive, clamped to 1–5):**

| Condition | Score contribution |
|-----------|-------------------|
| `security_level == Public (0)` | base 4 |
| `security_level == Unlisted (1)` | base 3 |
| `security_level == TrustGated (2)` | base 2 |
| `security_level == GroupScoped (3)` | base 1 |
| `security_level == Direct (4)` | base 1 |
| `storage_method == Distributed` | +1 |
| `storage_method == Sticky` | +0 |
| `publisher == None` (anonymous) | +1 (cannot issue stop-storing) |
| `scope == Global` | +1 |
| `scope == Group` | +0 |
| `scope == LAN` | -1 |
| `estimated_node_count > 10` | +1 |
| `time_since_publish > 7 days` | +1 (retroactive; score updates over time) |
| `observed_demand > 100 fetches` | +1 (retroactive) |

**Output labels:**
- **1:** Fully revocable — stop-storing signal will reach all holders
- **2:** Mostly revocable — a few copies may persist beyond your control
- **3:** Partially revocable — removal is best-effort; widely distributed copies may remain
- **4:** Mostly permanent — content is broadly distributed; removal unlikely to succeed
- **5:** Effectively permanent — removal is not realistic

Displayed before publish as: *"Sharing this file at this security level is [label]. [One-sentence consequence]."* This is informational — the user makes the final decision.

#### 11.2.7 Stop-Storing Signal

Stop-storing signals target the manifest hash. Storage nodes that receive a valid stop-storing signal for a manifest hash should also honor stop-storing for all chunk hashes listed in that manifest. This ensures the entire file is removable, not just the manifest.

```rust
StopStoringSignal {
    manifest_hash:  [u8; 32],        // content address of the manifest to remove
    chunk_hashes:   Vec<[u8; 32]>,   // all chunk hashes from the manifest; storage nodes
                                      // delete each of these as well
    issued_at:      u64,             // unix timestamp; nodes reject signals >30 days old
    issuer:         [u8; 32],        // peer ID of the publisher (must match manifest.publisher)
    signature:      [u8; 64],        // Ed25519 over (manifest_hash || issued_at); only the
                                      // original publisher can issue a valid stop-storing signal
}
```

Anonymous publishes (`publisher: None`) cannot issue stop-storing signals — the publisher has no key to sign with. This is a deliberate tradeoff: anonymous publishing sacrifices revocability. The stickiness metric (§11.2) reflects this — anonymous Level 0/1 publishes score 5 (effectively permanent).

Stop-storing for individual chunks (without a manifest) is also valid — for cases where a chunk is shared across multiple files via content-addressed deduplication.

The effectiveness of stop-storing depends on the content security level:
- Level 0–1: Effectively unenforceable — any node that already has the content can ignore the signal
- Level 2: Best-effort — cooperative nodes honor it, but previously distributed copies persist
- Level 3: Group-scoped enforcement — the group can collectively enforce via rekeying
- Level 4: Reliable — minimal key holders, point-to-point distribution

### 11.3 Public File Hosting

A node running in server mode (§2.2) can host files publicly as a service, serving them over the mesh HTTP layer (§12.2) with a stable, advertised address.

**Service advertisement:** A publicly hosting node advertises its hosted files in its service record (§12.1):

```rust
HostedFileEntry {
    manifest_hash:  [u8; 32],   // content address; also usable as sha256:<hex> address
    name:           String,
    size:           u64,
    mime_type:      String,
    description:    Option<String>,
    path:           String,     // URL path under the mesh HTTP service, e.g. "/files/report.pdf"
    published_at:   u64,
}
```

**Access:** Clients fetch via the mesh HTTP layer at `<node_address>:<port><path>`. The connection is session-encrypted (§7.3). No authentication is required for public files.

**Content addressing:** A publicly hosted file is simultaneously addressable by its host path and by its content hash (`sha256:<manifest_hash_hex>`). The content-hash address enables fetching from any node that holds the content in distributed storage (§11.2) — the same file can be served from its original host or from the distributed storage network interchangeably. The receiving node verifies the manifest hash regardless of source.

**Bandwidth and rate limiting:** Hosting nodes can configure per-file and per-client rate limits. Mesh transport is already bandwidth-capped at the transport layer (§5.2), but application-level limits allow finer control. Default: no rate limit. Configuration is per-service in the node management interface (§17.12).

**Caching:** Any mesh node that fetches a public file may cache it locally and serve it to subsequent requesters. Cache validity is proven by content hash — content-addressed files are immutable by definition: a changed file produces a different SHA-256, which is a different file with a different manifest hash. There is no cache invalidation problem; a cached chunk with a verified hash is unconditionally valid forever. The original host benefits from reduced load; the network benefits from improved availability and resilience against the original host going offline.

### 11.4 Private File Sharing

Private file sharing delivers files only to intended recipients using the trust and encryption infrastructure established in §7 and §8.

**Direct transfer to a peer:** Use §11.1 (`FileTransferOffer` → `FileTransferAccept` → chunks). The session key (§7.3) ensures only the paired recipient can decrypt. Files never touch storage nodes.

**Direct transfer to a group:** Same protocol, but the offer is sent to the group channel (§10.1.2). The group's Sender Key (§7.0) encrypts the offer; each group member receives the offer and can accept independently, initiating their own direct transfer session. The file is not broadcast — each recipient fetches independently from the sender.

**Shared group file repository:** Groups can maintain a persistent, shared collection of files:

```rust
GroupFileRepository {
    group_id:    [u8; 32],
    files:       Vec<GroupFileEntry>,
    version:     u64,               // monotonically increasing; sync anchor
    sigs:        Vec<[u8; 64]>,     // one Ed25519 signature per admin who approved this version;
                                     // at least one required; threshold configurable per group
}

GroupFileEntry {
    manifest_hash:  [u8; 32],
    name:           String,
    size:           u64,
    mime_type:      String,
    added_by:       [u8; 32],       // member peer ID
    added_at:       u64,
    description:    Option<String>,
}
```

**File encryption key for group repository entries:** Files in the repository are encrypted with a per-file key (`file_key`). The `file_key` is wrapped with the group's current Sender Key epoch key: `wrapped_key = encrypt(group_epoch_key, file_key)`. The wrapped key is included in the `file_key_hint` field of the `FileManifest` (§11.2.3). When a member fetches the manifest, they unwrap the file key using their copy of the group epoch key. On group rekeying (member removal), existing files remain accessible to members who held the old epoch key; newly uploaded files use the new epoch key.

Files in a group repository are published to group-scoped distributed storage (§11.2, Level 3) — storage nodes hold only encrypted blobs. Only group members can decrypt. The repository manifest is signed by one or more admins and versioned; members sync it via the group channel on join and on update notifications.

**Adding a file to a group repository:** The uploader publishes the file to Level 3 distributed storage, receives a manifest hash, then proposes a `GroupFileRepository` update to the group channel. Admins sign and countersign. Once the threshold of admin signatures is reached, the update is broadcast to the group. Non-admin members can propose additions but cannot broadcast without admin countersignature.

**Concurrent admin conflict resolution:** If two admins produce `GroupFileRepository` updates with the same `version` number simultaneously (concurrent adds), the conflict is resolved by: taking the entry union of both versions, incrementing `version` by 1, and requiring a new admin signature on the merged result. This merge is triggered automatically by any admin who receives two valid signed updates at the same version. The merged version supersedes both conflicting updates.

**Access control within a group:** Repository access respects the existing group role model. By default, all group members can read; only admins can add or remove entries. Fine-grained per-file ACLs are not specified at this layer — use separate groups or trust levels if per-file access control is needed.

**Distributed storage at Level 2–4:** Any file can be published to distributed storage at the appropriate security level (§11.2) and the manifest hash shared privately via Chat, Garden, or Relay. The recipient fetches from distributed storage using the manifest hash. This is the async equivalent of direct transfer — sender and recipient don't need to be online simultaneously.

### 11.5 Storage Backend Adapter Interface

Mesh Infinity's distributed storage (§11.2), store-and-forward (§6.8), and backup (§3.7) systems use a pluggable storage backend model. The default backend is local vault storage (§17.9). Additional backends provide decentralized redundancy, censorship resistance, and offline availability.

#### 11.5.1 Adapter Interface

All storage backends implement a common async interface:

```rust
trait StorageBackend: Send + Sync {
    /// Store a content-addressed blob. Returns the content ID (CID/hash).
    async fn put(&self, data: &[u8], metadata: BlobMetadata) -> Result<ContentId>;

    /// Retrieve a blob by content ID. Returns None if not found.
    /// MUST verify SHA-256(returned_data) == id.hash before returning.
    /// Returns Err on hash mismatch — corrupted or malicious data from backend.
    async fn get(&self, id: &ContentId) -> Result<Option<Vec<u8>>>;

    /// Check if a blob exists without retrieving it.
    async fn has(&self, id: &ContentId) -> Result<bool>;

    /// Request that a blob be removed (best-effort — some backends cannot guarantee deletion).
    async fn remove(&self, id: &ContentId) -> Result<RemoveResult>;

    /// List available blobs (for sync and garbage collection).
    async fn list(&self, prefix: Option<&[u8]>, limit: u32) -> Result<Vec<ContentId>>;

    /// Backend capabilities and health.
    fn capabilities(&self) -> BackendCapabilities;
}

ContentId {
    hash:      [u8; 32],    // SHA-256 of content
    size:      u64,
    backend:   BackendId,   // which backend stores this blob
}

BackendCapabilities {
    mutable:         bool,   // can blobs be updated in place?
    deletable:       bool,   // can blobs be reliably deleted?
    persistent:      bool,   // does the backend guarantee long-term storage?
    max_blob_size:   u64,    // maximum single blob size (0 = unlimited)
    redundancy:      RedundancyModel,  // None | Replication(n) | ErasureCoding(k, n)
    latency_class:   LatencyClass,     // Local | LAN | Internet | HighLatency
}

RemoveResult { Deleted, NotFound, BestEffort }
```

**Content addressing:** All backends use SHA-256 content addressing. The same blob stored on multiple backends has the same `ContentId.hash`. This enables transparent migration between backends and verification that retrieved content matches the expected hash.

#### 11.5.2 IPFS Backend

**IPFS** (InterPlanetary File System) provides content-addressed distributed storage with a global DHT for content discovery. Files are split into blocks, each identified by a CID (Content Identifier), and distributed across participating nodes.

```rust
IpfsBackendConfig {
    api_endpoint:    String,        // IPFS HTTP API (default: http://127.0.0.1:5001)
    gateway:         Option<String>,// HTTP gateway for reads (default: http://127.0.0.1:8080)
    pin_on_add:      bool,          // pin content locally to prevent GC (default: true)
    remote_pinning:  Vec<PinningService>,  // remote pinning services (Pinata, web3.storage, etc.)
}
```

**Integration model:** Mesh Infinity connects to a local IPFS node via its HTTP API (Kubo, Iroh, or any IPFS implementation). Content is added via `POST /api/v0/add`, retrieved via the gateway or `POST /api/v0/cat`, and pinned via the Pinning Service API.

**Capabilities:** `{ mutable: false, deletable: false (best-effort unpin), persistent: true (if pinned), max_blob_size: 0, redundancy: Replication (DHT-based), latency_class: Internet }`

**Privacy considerations:** IPFS DHT queries reveal interest in specific CIDs to DHT neighbours. Content added to IPFS is discoverable by anyone who knows the CID. For private content, encrypt before adding (standard — §11.2 Level 3-4 files are already encrypted before storage). The CID itself is opaque and reveals nothing about content without the encryption key.

**Use cases:** Public file hosting (§11.3), Garden media storage, backup distribution, large file distribution.

#### 11.5.3 Tahoe-LAFS Backend

**Tahoe-LAFS** (Least-Authority File Store) provides encrypted, erasure-coded distributed storage across a grid of storage servers. Files are encrypted client-side, split into shares using Reed-Solomon erasure coding, and distributed across the grid. Any K of N shares suffice to reconstruct the file.

```rust
TahoeLafsConfig {
    introducer_furl:  String,     // Tahoe introducer node FURL
    shares_needed:    u8,         // K — shares needed to reconstruct (default: 3)
    shares_happy:     u8,         // H — minimum distinct servers for upload success (default: 7)
    shares_total:     u8,         // N — total shares generated (default: 10)
}
```

**Integration model:** Mesh Infinity runs a Tahoe-LAFS client node that connects to a storage grid via the introducer. Files are uploaded via the WAPI (Web API): `PUT /uri` returns a read capability (immutable) or write capability (mutable). Retrieval: `GET /uri/<cap>`.

**Capabilities:** `{ mutable: true (with write cap), deletable: false (garbage collected when no caps reference it), persistent: true, max_blob_size: 0, redundancy: ErasureCoding(3, 10), latency_class: Internet }`

**Security model:** Tahoe-LAFS provides confidentiality (AES encryption, key in the capability URI), integrity (Merkle tree verification), and availability (erasure coding — survives loss of N-K servers). Storage servers never see plaintext. The capability URI is the secret — anyone who holds it can read the file. This maps naturally to Mesh Infinity's content-addressed model: the capability URI is distributed through encrypted mesh channels (Chat, Garden, Relay).

**Erasure coding vs. secret sharing:** Tahoe-LAFS uses erasure coding for availability, not secret sharing for confidentiality. Fewer than K shares do not provide a security guarantee — confidentiality comes from the AES encryption, not the share distribution. This is correct for Mesh Infinity's model, which already encrypts content before storage.

**Use cases:** High-availability file storage, encrypted backups (§3.7), store-and-forward payload storage where multiple S&F nodes should hold redundant copies.

#### 11.5.4 Hypercore Backend

**Hypercore** is a secure, distributed append-only log. Each log is identified by a public key; entries are signed, hash-chained, and replicable between peers. Hypercore is the foundation of the Dat/Hyperswarm ecosystem.

```rust
HypercoreConfig {
    storage_dir:     String,         // local directory for hypercore data
    replication:     ReplicationMode, // Manual | OnConnect | AlwaysOn
    discovery:       bool,           // announce on Hyperswarm DHT (default: false for privacy)
}

ReplicationMode {
    Manual,       // replicate only on explicit request
    OnConnect,    // replicate when a peer holding the same key connects
    AlwaysOn,     // actively seek peers and replicate continuously
}
```

**Integration model:** Mesh Infinity uses the `hypercore` Rust crate (datrs/hypercore). Each data collection (message history, file chunks, backup segments) can be stored as a Hypercore log. Replication syncs the log with peers who hold the public key.

**Capabilities:** `{ mutable: true (append-only — new entries, no modification of existing), deletable: false (append-only log), persistent: true, max_blob_size: 0, redundancy: Replication (peer-to-peer), latency_class: Internet }`

**Use cases:** Multi-device message history sync (§3.7.8 — each device maintains a Hypercore log of its message history; devices replicate logs bidirectionally). Cross-device backup sync. Append-only audit logs (Garden §10.2.10).

**Privacy consideration:** If `discovery` is enabled, the Hyperswarm DHT reveals that a node is interested in a specific Hypercore key. For private data, keep `discovery: false` and replicate only with known mesh peers via existing WireGuard tunnels.

#### 11.5.5 Additional Backends

The following backends are supported through the same adapter interface. They are briefly specified here; full configuration follows the same pattern as §11.5.2–11.5.4.

**BitTorrent/WebTorrent:**
- Content distributed as standard BitTorrent infohashes
- WebTorrent adds browser-based seeding via WebRTC
- Mesh nodes act as seeders; external BitTorrent clients can participate
- Use case: large public file distribution (§11.3)
- Privacy: torrent participation reveals IP to other peers in the swarm. Use only for content intentionally made public. Mesh nodes participating via Tor or I2P as transport avoid direct IP exposure.

**Storj (S3-compatible):**
- S3-compatible API backed by a decentralized storage network
- Client-side encryption before upload (standard Mesh Infinity behavior)
- Use case: encrypted cloud backup storage (§3.7.5)
- Privacy: Storj network sees encrypted blobs and metadata (size, timestamp). Content is opaque.

**Arweave:**
- Permanent storage on a blockchain-backed data network
- Content is stored indefinitely with a one-time payment
- Use case: permanent public archives, immutable Garden history export
- Privacy: Arweave is a public blockchain — all stored data is permanently public. Only use for content intended to be permanently public. Always encrypt before storing if any privacy is required.
- Note: blockchain dependency — see §5.20 for the general policy on blockchain-dependent infrastructure. Arweave is treated as a storage medium, not an anonymizing network.

#### 11.5.6 Backend Selection and Routing

The storage layer selects backends based on content type and security level:

| Content type | Default backend | Alternatives |
|-------------|----------------|-------------|
| Message history | Local vault (§17.9) | Hypercore (multi-device sync) |
| S&F payloads | Local vault on S&F node | Tahoe-LAFS (redundancy across S&F nodes) |
| Public files (§11.3) | IPFS | BitTorrent, Arweave (permanent) |
| Private files (§11.4) | Local vault + direct transfer | Tahoe-LAFS (availability), Storj (cloud) |
| Backups (§3.7) | Local + optional cloud | Storj, Tahoe-LAFS, Hypercore |
| Garden media | Local vault on cluster | IPFS (public Gardens), Tahoe-LAFS (private Gardens) |

Users configure preferred backends in Settings → Storage. The storage layer falls back to local vault if no external backend is available — the system always works without external dependencies (§2.1 principle 5).

---

## 12. Hosted Services

Any TCP or UDP service can be hosted over Mesh Infinity and made accessible to other nodes. This is the mechanism by which Mesh Infinity provides an alternative to web hosting, API services, SSH access, and any other internet service.

**Default model — services are private by default.** A service exists on an address. Nothing about its existence is announced unless the operator explicitly chooses to announce it. The service is reachable only if the connecting peer already knows how to reach it.

Knowledge of a private address already implies knowledge of services on it — trusted peers who know the private addresses know the private services implicitly via the trust relationship. No additional announcement is needed for private services between trusted peers.

**The privacy property:** An adversary watching the mesh sees no service landscape unless nodes choose to announce. Probing for unknown services reveals the prober. The mesh does not leak service topology by default.

### 12.0 Overview

- **§12.1 Service Addressing** — port-based addressing as compatibility layer; mesh-native service IDs; 32-bit port space allocation; reserved mesh-native ports (§12.1.1); port addressing agent (§12.1.2)
- **§12.2 Mesh HTTP Layer** — transparent proxy; browser integration; DNS via §17.11; VPN mode note
- **§12.3 TCP and UDP Tunneling** — `TunnelRequest`/`TunnelAccept` protocol; session lifecycle; local port binding
- **§12.4 Service Registration, Exposure, and Announcement** — `ServiceRecord` struct; exposure vs announcement vs indexing; `TransportHint`; `AnnouncementScope`
- **§12.5 Service Indexes** — `ServiceIndex` struct; no central registry; re-advertisement model
- **§12.6 Service Access Model** — open/credentialed/trust-gated/key-required/combined
- **§12.7 Access Control Enforcement** — connection-layer enforcement; peer ID from WireGuard public key (§3)
- **§12.8 Service Health and Availability** — last-alive timestamps; mirroring; `ServiceMirrorAnnouncement`

### 12.1 Service Addressing

**A service address is a complete, self-contained reference.** Services with their own mesh identity — server-mode nodes, Garden instances, Relay nodes — are addressed directly by their mesh address or `service_id` (§12.4). No port is required for the address to be valid and routable. Ports on a service address are additive: they enable sub-multiplexing within a service, extra stream transports, or compatibility with port-speaking protocols. A client that only knows a service's address can connect; a client that also knows a port can address a specific sub-channel.

**The 32-bit port space is defined by the highest-order bit:**

- **Bit 31 clear (0x00000000–0x7FFFFFFF): Addressed ports.** These are service ports — stable, assigned, used for inbound addressing and sub-multiplexing. Structured in 1000-port blocks.
- **Bit 31 set (0x80000000–0xFFFFFFFF): Ephemeral ports.** These are connection-tracking ports, used by mesh-native software for outbound connection state. The bit value alone defines a port as ephemeral — there is no other distinction. Standard TCP software never uses these as service ports; a TCP stack that encounters one as a destination port would not handle it gracefully.

**Virtual addressing and the IANA port space:** When a standard TCP/IP application runs over a mesh virtual address, it uses the IANA port space (0–65535) as normal — including the IANA ephemeral range (49152–65535) for outbound source ports. The mesh passes this through unchanged. Standard TCP/IP devices operating on IANA ports are treated as such. Mesh services are never hosted on IANA ports; the IANA range is a compatibility mapped address space for virtual addressing only.

**Service port space allocation (0x00000000–0x7FFFFFFF):**

| Range | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| 0–65535 | **IANA-compatible** — exact mirror of internet port conventions, including the 49152–65535 ephemeral sub-range. Preserved as-is for virtual addressing compatibility. |
| 65536–66999 | **Reserved critical zone** — currently unassigned by design. This range sits between the IANA space and the service block range deliberately. No legitimate service is ever hosted here. Any inbound connection attempt to this zone is anomalous by definition, making it a natural honeypot. Future use: connections to this range will only be accepted when handed off from a prior authenticated connection on a higher-order service port — a port-knocking model where the higher port vouches for the critical zone access. Implementation is deferred; the design intent is recorded now. |
| 67000–133999 | **Mesh Infinity native service blocks** — 67 reserved blocks of 1000 ports each, 5 currently assigned. See §12.1.1. |
| 134000–2,147,483,647 | **Free application/operator space** — available in 1000-port blocks or as individual ports. A port addressing agent (§12.1.2) manages allocation to prevent conflicts between block and individual assignments. |

**The 1000-port block structure:** Port blocks are numbered by `port ÷ 1000` (integer division). The sub-port within a block is `port mod 1000` (0–999). Block 67 = ports 67000–67999; block 134 = ports 134000–134999. This is unambiguous at any port number. Within a block, the service decides which sub-ports carry which traffic — the mesh has no opinion. Knowing a service exists on a node does not reveal which sub-port it is using. This is an incidental property, not a security model — 1000 ports is a trivially scannable space. The real value is detection: a sweep across a block is a recognisable pattern the mesh can flag.

#### 12.1.1 Mesh Infinity Native Service Port Blocks

67 blocks reserved (67000–133999); 13 currently assigned. The remaining 54 (80000–133999) are reserved for future first-party mesh services. Adapters share sub-port ranges within their parent protocol block — they do not occupy separate blocks.

| Block | Range | Service |
|-------|-------|---------|
| 67 | 67000–67999 | Garden plugin (§18) |
| 68 | 68000–68999 | Infinet (§13.14) |
| 69 | 69000–69999 | Relay service (§10.3) |
| 70 | 70000–70999 | Node management interface (§17.12) |
| 71 | 71000–71999 | Chat application (§10.1) |
| 72 | 72000–72999 | Agentic Distribution plugin (§18.6) |
| 73 | 73000–73999 | MNRDP — Mesh-Native Remote Desktop (§20.2): native server 73000–73099; VNC adapter 73100–73199; NX adapter 73200–73299; ARD adapter 73300–73399 |
| 74 | 74000–74999 | MNSP — Mesh-Native Shell (§20.3): native server 74000–74099; SSH adapter 74100–74199 |
| 75 | 75000–75999 | MNFP — Mesh-Native File Access (§20.4): native server 75000–75099; SMB/CIFS adapter 75100–75199; NFS adapter 75200–75299; SFTP adapter 75300–75399 |
| 76 | 76000–76999 | API Gateway (§20.5) |
| 77 | 77000–77999 | Screen Share (§20.6) |
| 78 | 78000–78999 | Clipboard Sync (§20.7) |
| 79 | 79000–79999 | Print Services (§20.8): native server 79000–79099; IPP adapter 79100–79199 |
| 80–133 | 80000–133999 | Reserved for future first-party protocols |

Note: Proximity Share (§10.4) is a peer-to-peer direct service link — it has no mesh port. BLE and WiFi Direct transports may use ports at the transport layer, but Proximity Share itself operates outside the port space.

#### 12.1.2 Port Addressing Agent

A port addressing agent running on each node manages the 134000+ free space, enforcing two rules:

1. A block already claimed as a whole (e.g. 134000–134999 assigned to application X) cannot have individual ports assigned within it.
2. A block with any individually-assigned ports cannot subsequently be claimed as a whole block.

First-come-first-served. The agent is a node-local service; its allocation state is persisted in the node's encrypted vault. External assignment requests (from plugins, third-party services) go through the agent. Mesh Infinity native service blocks (67000–133999) are pre-allocated and not managed by the agent.

**`service_id` and port blocks are independent but complementary.** A service may have a `service_id` (§12.4) for identity-based addressing and a port block for sub-multiplexing. A `service_id` lookup returns the service's mesh address and, optionally, a specific port within its block. Neither requires the other.

**Mesh addressing constructs:** The routing layer delivers packets to mesh addresses. Services at those addresses may implement additional indirection — wrapper nodes, port forwarders, address translation. These are service-layer constructs configured via the node management interface (§17.12); the routing layer is not aware of them.


### 12.2 Mesh HTTP Layer

The mesh HTTP layer is the compatibility bridge between standard web software and the mesh.

#### 12.2.1 Access Modes

**VPN mode (§13) is the primary and recommended access method.** In VPN mode, the mesh operates as a system-wide network layer — all traffic is routed through the mesh transparently, no application configuration required. This is the full solution and the preferred deployment for any node where it is practical.

**Proxy mode is a compromise for systems where VPN mode is unavailable.** VPN mode requires operating system-level privileges (TUN/TAP interface creation). On systems where those privileges cannot be obtained — shared machines, restricted corporate environments, or situations where the user cannot or does not want to grant admin access — proxy mode provides partial coverage. It is explicitly not a full solution; it only covers applications that can be configured to use a proxy. It is the best available option in a constrained environment, and we provide it without apology for that constraint.

Both modes are configured through the same control panel as all other mesh settings.

#### 12.2.2 Local Proxy Services

Three proxy types are exposed, all on localhost. All are configured from the mesh control panel:

```
HTTP proxy:   127.0.0.1:7888  (configurable)
HTTPS proxy:  127.0.0.1:7889  (configurable)
SOCKS5 proxy: 127.0.0.1:1080  (configurable)
```

**HTTP proxy:** Plain HTTP CONNECT proxy. Applications that support HTTP proxy settings can use this. No TLS on the proxy connection itself — traffic between application and proxy is unencrypted on the loopback interface. Traffic from the proxy onward into the mesh is WireGuard-encrypted as normal.

**HTTPS proxy:** HTTPS proxy using a locally-generated self-signed certificate. On first run, the user is prompted to add this certificate to the system trust store. This is a one-time setup step — the prompt explains what it is and why it is needed. Once added, browsers and HTTPS clients connect to the proxy without security warnings. This is the preferred proxy mode for browser use.

**Important — this proxy performs local TLS interception (HTTPS proxy only).** The browser's TLS connection terminates at the proxy using the locally-trusted certificate. The proxy reads the decrypted application-layer content, then re-encrypts it over WireGuard toward the mesh destination. This is architecturally necessary for browser integration — browsers require a TLS handshake, and the proxy must be a party to it. The trust implication: the local proxy process sees plaintext traffic, the same as any other process running under the user's account on the local machine. This is not a new trust boundary — it is the same trust level as the OS and all other local processes. **This interception applies only to the HTTPS proxy. It is not present in VPN mode (§13), where traffic routes through WireGuard without TLS termination at the mesh layer.**

**SOCKS5 proxy:** SOCKS5 proxy for TCP and UDP applications that speak SOCKS5 but not HTTP proxy. Covers database clients, game clients, custom protocol applications, and any other software that supports SOCKS5 but would not route through an HTTP proxy. SOCKS5 does not perform TLS interception — it tunnels the raw connection.

All three proxies route to the same mesh session. Configuring multiple proxies on the same device is valid — a browser might use the HTTPS proxy while a game client uses SOCKS5.

**Protocol semantics:** WebSocket upgrades, Server-Sent Events, long-polling, chunked transfer, HTTP CONNECT tunnelling, and any other mechanism a client uses are passed through faithfully. The proxy does not transform, filter, or log application-layer content beyond what is required for the TLS termination step described above.

**Supported HTTP versions:** HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 required. HTTP/3 (QUIC) supported. QUIC is not a mesh transport (§5) — it is supported here as an obfuscation mechanism. HTTP/3 traffic to a mesh service is indistinguishable from standard web traffic to a network observer. The broader principle: we support as many standard protocols as possible at the compatibility layer, because each one is another face the mesh can wear.

#### 12.2.3 TLS Model

**Mesh-to-mesh:** The HTTPS proxy's locally-trusted cert handles browser compatibility. WireGuard provides transport encryption. No public TLS involved.

```
Browser → [HTTPS proxy: locally-trusted self-signed cert] → WireGuard → Mesh service
```

**Clearnet-to-mesh via Inlet (§12.2.4):** The Inlet relay holds a standard public TLS certificate (e.g. Let's Encrypt). It terminates the clearnet TLS connection and forwards the request into the mesh over a WireGuard session.

```
Clearnet client → [Inlet: public TLS cert] → WireGuard → Mesh service
```

The Inlet relay is a trusted gateway — it sees decrypted request content. The mesh service sees requests arriving from the Inlet relay's mesh address. The clearnet client's identity is the Inlet relay's business, not the mesh service's.

#### 12.2.4 Inlet — Clearnet Ingress

An **Inlet** is an edge node with a public IP that accepts inbound clearnet connections and forwards them into the mesh. Inlet services are provided by people — node operators who choose to expose mesh services to the internet. No central mesh authority manages Inlet access.

```rust
InletConfig {
    service_id:    [u8; 16],         // which mesh service this Inlet exposes
    listen_addr:   SocketAddr,       // public IP:port to accept clearnet connections on
    tls_cert:      TLSCertSource,    // where to get the public TLS certificate
    rate_limit:    Option<u32>,      // requests/second per clearnet client; None = unlimited
    access_policy: InletAccessPolicy,
}

TLSCertSource {
    LetsEncrypt { domain: String, email: String },  // automatic ACME provisioning
    Manual      { cert_path: String, key_path: String },
    SelfSigned,  // for non-public Inlets (internal-facing, test environments)
}

InletAccessPolicy {
    Public,                   // any clearnet client may connect
    AllowList(Vec<IpNet>),    // specific IP ranges only
    MeshMemberOnly,           // clearnet URL but only authenticated mesh members can use it
}
```

**Clearnet clients receive clearnet treatment.** Request headers — `X-Forwarded-For`, `X-Real-IP`, `User-Agent`, and all other standard headers — are forwarded to the mesh service unchanged. Clearnet users are on the clearnet and have clearnet-level expectations. Traffic controls, rate limiting, analytics, and abuse management all depend on this information being present. Operators who want to strip headers may do so in their service application layer; the Inlet does not strip by default.

**What the Inlet operator sees:** Source IP of the clearnet client, destination service, request timing and size. The Inlet relay is a trusted party. Run your own Inlet for maximum privacy, or use one run by a trusted community member.

**DDNS integration:** Inlet registers the exposed address with a DDNS provider for a stable domain name. Supported: Cloudflare DNS API, deSEC, any RFC 2136 server. Full DDNS flow: §13.16.

**Relationship to Funnel (§13.16):** Inlet is the protocol layer specced here.
